pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time
pages.epigraph: When we understand what a child is still learning, difficult behaviour becomes easier to guide.

blocks.text: The Morning Everything Went Wrong

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: It was 8:10 in the morning, and Leena was already late. Her five-year-old daughter, Tara, was sitting on the floor in her nightclothes. “Please get dressed,” Leena said. Tara continued arranging her crayons. “I’ve asked you three times.” No response. Leena took the crayons away. Tara shouted, knocked over the box and began crying. Now Leena was upset too. “Why do you make every morning so difficult?” The moment the words left her mouth, she regretted them.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Tara was not trying to ruin the morning. She was absorbed in an activity, struggling to stop, unhappy that the crayons had been taken and unable to manage all those feelings quickly. Leena was not a bad mother either. She was under pressure, watching the clock and trying to manage several responsibilities at once. Neither of them needed blame. They needed a better plan. That is where this book begins—not with the assumption that children are behaving badly or that parents are failing, but with a more useful question:
What is happening here, and what does this child still need to learn?

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Behaviour Is Only the Part We Can See
When a child refuses, screams, grabs, runs away or ignores an instruction, we see the behaviour. We do not immediately see everything underneath it. Perhaps the child is:

items: unable to understand a long instruction;

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: testing whether a boundary will remain firm;
lacking the words to explain what is wrong;
repeating behaviour that has worked before;
still developing the skill the situation requires.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Understanding the reason does not mean permitting unsafe or hurtful behaviour. A child may have an understandable reason for hitting and still need to be stopped. A child may be genuinely disappointed and still be unable to have another sweet. A child’s feelings can be accepted while the boundary remains unchanged. That balance—understanding the child while guiding the behaviour—is one of the central tasks of parenting.
“Won’t” and “Can’t Yet” Can Look Very Similar
Parents often think:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: “She can behave when she wants to.”
“He listened yesterday, so why not today?”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Children can sometimes demonstrate a skill without being able to use it consistently. A child may wait patiently when rested but struggle when hungry. She may share at home but become possessive in a crowded classroom. He may remember the rule when calm but forget it during excitement. This does not mean children have no responsibility for their behaviour. It means responsibility must be taught at a level their developing abilities can support.
Before deciding that a child will not cooperate, ask whether the child can do what the situation demands at that moment. Does the child have:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: the language to explain the problem?
the attention to follow the instruction?
the emotional control to tolerate disappointment?
the physical ability to complete the task?
enough practice to do it independently?
the energy to manage one more demand?
a clear understanding of what is expected?

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Sometimes the answer is yes, and the child still resists. That is normal too. Growing children want increasing influence over their own lives. The parent’s task is not to remove every protest. It is to make expectations clear, hold necessary limits and help the child practise better responses.
What Young Children Are Still Learning
Between approximately two and seven years of age, children make enormous progress. However, many abilities adults take for granted are still developing.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: 1. Managing Strong Feelings

Young children experience frustration, excitement, fear and disappointment intensely. They do not automatically know how to calm their bodies, choose safe words or wait until a feeling passes. They learn these skills gradually through development, practice and support from caregivers.
2. Controlling Impulses

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Knowing a rule is not the same as being able to stop an impulse every time. A child may know not to grab the toy and still reach for it. The desire appears faster than the child’s ability to pause.
3. Moving Between Activities
Transitions can be difficult because the child must:

items: accept that an adult has changed the plan;

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: “Put the toys away; we’re leaving” may sound simple to an adult. To a young child, it contains several challenges at once.
4. Understanding Time
“Later,” “in ten minutes” and “next week” are abstract ideas. Young children understand routines and visible sequences more easily:
“First we put on our shoes. Then we go to the park.”
5. Seeing Another Person’s Point of View

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Empathy develops gradually. A young child may care deeply about another person and still struggle to understand why that person wants something different.
6. Using Words Under Stress
A child who can speak beautifully while calm may lose access to those words when overwhelmed. This is why asking, “Why did you do that?” during a meltdown rarely produces a useful answer.
7. Remembering Several Instructions

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: “Put your cup in the kitchen, bring your shoes, find your bag and wait by the door” may be too much. A child who completes only one part may not be ignoring the parent. The rest of the instruction may simply have disappeared from working memory.
Development Changes the Question
Consider these two interpretations:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

rows: The child cries when playtime ends
“Stopping is hard, and she needs help with transitions.”
“He wants the toy and is still learning to wait and ask.”
“She wants independence or is overwhelmed by the task.”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

rows: “His body needs movement, and the rule needs to be made clearer.”
The child lies about a broken object
“She may be frightened of the adult’s reaction and needs help telling the truth safely.”
“He needs to be stopped and taught a safer response to conflict.”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

rows: The child becomes silly at bedtime
“She may be overtired, seeking connection or struggling to settle.”

blocks.text: These possibilities are not excuses. They are starting points. A useful explanation helps us choose a better response. It does not make the limit disappear.
Understanding Is Not the Same as Permissiveness

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Some parents worry that recognising the feeling behind behaviour will teach a child that anything is acceptable. It will not—provided understanding is followed by guidance. You can say:
“You wanted the toy. I won’t let you grab it. You can ask for a turn.”
“You’re angry that we have to leave. It is still time to go.”
“You didn’t want your sister to touch your building. Hitting is not safe. I will help you move the blocks.”
“You wish you could keep watching. The screen is finished for today.”
The structure is simple:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: Show the child what to do instead.

blocks.text: The parent remains kind without becoming uncertain. The child is understood without being placed in charge of every decision.
Use the Four-Lens Behaviour Check
When behaviour puzzles or frustrates you, look through four lenses before reacting.
Lens 1: The Body
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: Is there pain, illness or physical discomfort?
Is the environment too noisy, crowded, hot or stimulating?

blocks.text: Behaviour often deteriorates when the body is struggling.
Lens 2: The Situation
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: Was the child asked to stop a preferred activity?
Is this situation too difficult or unfamiliar?
Does this behaviour happen at a particular time or place?

blocks.text: Patterns provide useful information.
Lens 3: The Skill
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: What ability does this moment require?
Can my child wait, take turns or explain the problem?
Has this skill been taught and practised?
Am I expecting more than the child can manage independently?

blocks.text: A child cannot use a skill that has not yet been learned.
Lens 4: The Relationship
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: Does my child need a moment of connection?
Have we been rushing or correcting all day?
Am I available enough to help, or am I already overwhelmed?
Is there tension, separation or change affecting the family?
Does my child feel safe enough to cooperate?

blocks.text: Connection is not a reward for good behaviour. It is part of the foundation that makes guidance possible.
A Real-Life Example: Refusing to Leave the Park
Imagine that your child refuses to leave the playground.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: The Immediate Interpretation
“She is disobedient.”
What May Be Happening

items: She has little understanding of the schedule.
She is tired and has less emotional control.
Refusal has previously resulted in extra playtime.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: A More Helpful Response
Before the transition
Give simple, concrete preparation:
“Three more turns on the slide, then we are leaving.”
A timer or a predictable closing ritual may help.
At the transition
Acknowledge the feeling and state the plan:
“You want to stay. It is hard to stop. It is time to go.”
Offer limited control
“Would you like to walk to the gate or hold my hand?”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Both choices must be acceptable to the parent.
Follow through
If the child refuses, remain calm and help physically if necessary:
“You are having trouble walking. I will help you.”
Do not reopen the decision because the child protests. Doing so can teach the child that a sufficiently large reaction changes the boundary.
Afterward
When everyone is calm, practise what to do next time:
“Leaving was hard today. Tomorrow, when the timer rings, you can choose one last thing and then come with me.”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: This approach does not guarantee a tear-free departure. Success is not the absence of disappointment. Success is the parent remaining clear and the child gradually learning how transitions work.
Give Directions a Child Can Follow
Sometimes what appears to be disobedience begins with an instruction that is too vague, too long or delivered from too far away. Instead of:
“Behave yourself.”
Say:
“Please keep your feet on the floor.”
Instead of:
“Stop making a mess.”
Say:
“Put the blocks in this basket.”
Instead of calling repeatedly from another room:
“Get ready!”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Move close, gain the child’s attention and give one clear step:
“Please put on your shoes.”
Helpful directions are:

items: phrased as what the child should do;
followed by enough time to respond;

blocks.text: The aim is not to sound pleasant while issuing endless requests. It is to communicate clearly and follow through consistently.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Notice What Happens Before and After
Behaviour is shaped by what surrounds it. Use this simple pattern:

rows: What did the child actually do?

blocks.text: For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

rows: Child shouts and pulls the parent’s arm
Parent ends the call and gives attention
Child is told to put toys away
Parent notices and thanks the child

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: This exercise is not about blaming the parent. It helps reveal what the behaviour may be achieving. If screaming repeatedly brings a toy, delays bedtime or produces immediate attention, the child may use it again—not as part of a clever plan, but because behaviour that works tends to be repeated. The solution is not to withdraw warmth. It is to respond more intentionally.
The Difference Between a Pattern and a Difficult Day

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Every child has difficult days. Parents do too. One tantrum after a missed nap does not define a child. Refusing dinner once does not create a feeding problem. Hitting during an isolated conflict does not make a child aggressive. Look for patterns:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: Does it interfere with learning, relationships, sleep or ordinary family life?
Is the behaviour changing or becoming more severe?
Are there concerns about language, movement, play, attention or development?

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Patterns help parents decide whether a change at home may be enough or whether professional support is needed.
Common Mistakes
1. Calling the Child a Name
“Lazy,” “naughty,” “selfish,” “dramatic” and “difficult” turn a behaviour into an identity. Describe what happened instead:
“You left the toys on the floor.”
That gives the child something specific to change.
2. Expecting Adult Self-Control

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: A young child cannot reliably manage every impulse, delay or disappointment. Expectations should grow with the child’s developing ability.
3. Explaining Too Much During a Meltdown
When a child is overwhelmed, use fewer words. Safety and calm come first. Teaching comes later.
4. Asking Questions When Giving a Necessary Direction
“Would you like to get into the car now?” sounds optional. If it is not optional, say:
“It is time to get into the car.”
Then offer choice within the boundary:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: “Would you like to climb in yourself or have my help?”
5. Repeating Without Following Through
Ten unanswered instructions teach the child that the first nine do not matter. Give a clear direction, allow time and then help the child complete it.
6. Treating Every Protest as Disrespect
Children are allowed to dislike a decision. They are not allowed to hurt people or behave unsafely. The parent can tolerate the feeling without abandoning the limit.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: 7. Correcting Constantly
A child who receives attention mainly for mistakes may begin seeking connection through misbehaviour. Notice cooperation, effort, kindness and recovery too.
8. Assuming Every Behaviour Is Developmental

Development explains many challenges, but persistent or severe difficulties may require assessment. “Children grow out of it” should never replace careful observation when a parent is genuinely concerned.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

items: Advice encourages humiliating, frightening or physically hurting the child.
A child’s fear of an adult is described as respect.
Every difficult behaviour is blamed on poor parenting.
A child is labelled without a proper assessment.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: Developmental concerns are repeatedly dismissed without observation or evaluation.
A child loses skills they previously used.
Behaviour regularly causes injury or creates serious safety risks.
Difficulties occur across several settings and significantly affect daily life.
The child’s distress is intense, persistent or increasing.
A caregiver feels close to losing control or fears they may hurt the child.

blocks.text: If immediate safety is at risk, move the child and caregiver to safety and seek urgent local help.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: When to Seek Additional Support
Speak with a paediatrician or appropriately qualified child-development professional if you are concerned about your child’s development or behaviour. Seek support particularly when:

items: your child has lost previously acquired abilities;
communication, movement, play or social interaction concerns you;
behaviour is unusually intense, persistent or difficult to manage;
the child frequently harms themselves or others;

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: eating, sleeping, learning or ordinary family life is substantially affected;
preschool or school reports similar concerns;
your child has experienced trauma, violence, major loss or significant disruption;
your own stress is making safe, consistent parenting difficult.

blocks.text: Parents do not need to diagnose a child before asking for help. A clear description of what you observe is enough:

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Early conversations can provide reassurance, practical strategies or timely assessment.
Your Behaviour Detective Page
Choose one behaviour you would like to understand better.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: One thing I can change before the behaviour begins: ____________________________________________________

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: The safer or more helpful behaviour I will teach: ____________________________________________________
A Practical Script
When you are unsure what to say, try:
“I can see that this is hard. I won’t let you ________. You can ________ instead. I’ll help you.”
For example:
“I can see that you’re angry. I won’t let you hit. You can say, ‘Move away,’ or come to me. I’ll help you.”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: The words matter, but the parent’s follow-through matters more.
Remember This

Your child’s behaviour is not a verdict on your parenting, and one difficult moment is not a verdict on your child. Look beneath the behaviour without ignoring it. Accept the feeling without permitting harm. Teach the missing skill without expecting instant mastery. Hold the boundary without withdrawing love. Your child is not a finished person who has decided to make life difficult. Your child is growing—and growth requires guidance.

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Try This Today
Choose one behaviour that usually irritates you. Instead of asking:
“Why is my child doing this to me?”
ask:

items: What might my child be communicating?
What can I teach or change?

blocks.text: Write down one sentence you can use next time. For example:
“You want to keep playing. It is time to leave. You may walk beside me or hold my hand.”

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: You do not need to solve every behaviour today. Begin by seeing one familiar moment differently.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

items: [World Health Organization — Nurturing Care for Early Childhood Development](https://www.who.int/teams/maternal-newborn-child-adolescent-health-and-ageing/child-health/nurturing-care)

pages.chapterTitle: Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time
Your Child Is Growing, Not Giving You a Hard Time

blocks.text: Editorial note for review: Developmental expectations, safeguarding language and referral pathways should be reviewed for the intended Indian edition by qualified paediatric, child-development and mental-health professionals. Local emergency and child-protection information should be added before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You
pages.epigraph: Behaviour is communication—but understanding the message is only the beginning. Children also need help learning what to do instead.

blocks.text: The Biscuit Before Dinner

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Four-year-old Kabir asked for a biscuit while his mother was preparing dinner. “Not now,” she said. “We’re eating soon.” Kabir asked again. His mother repeated the answer. He began to whine. She tried to ignore him. The whining became crying. Then he lay on the kitchen floor and screamed. Exhausted and unable to finish cooking, his mother handed him the biscuit. The crying stopped. In that moment, both Kabir and his mother received relief.
Kabir learned:
“If I continue for long enough, the answer may change.”
His mother learned:
“Giving him the biscuit ends the screaming.”

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Neither of them planned this lesson. It simply developed from what happened before and after the behaviour. This is how many family patterns begin—not because a child is manipulative or a parent is weak, but because everyone is trying to get through a difficult moment. To change the pattern, we first need to see it.
Behaviour Communicates, but It Does Not Speak Clearly
Parents are often told:
“All behaviour is communication.”

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: That idea is helpful, but it can also make parents feel they must discover a deep emotional explanation for everything their child does. Usually, the message is more ordinary. A behaviour may be communicating:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: “I cannot manage this feeling safely yet.”

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: The same behaviour can carry different messages on different days. A child may shout because they are excited in one situation, frightened in another and trying to delay bedtime in a third. That is why we should be curious without pretending to know more than we do. Instead of declaring:
“You’re only doing this for attention.”
try thinking:
“Attention may be part of this pattern. Let me observe what happens.”
Curiosity leaves room for new information.
Describe Before You Interpret

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: When parents are upset, descriptions can quickly become judgements. “She was completely out of control.” “He was being impossible.” “She had a terrible attitude.” These statements express the parent’s experience, but they do not tell us exactly what happened. A useful description is something another person could have seen or heard:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: “He threw three toys after I turned off the television.”
“She covered her ears and hid beneath the table.”
“He left his chair six times during the meal.”
“She hit her brother when he picked up the doll.”
“He did not begin dressing after two instructions.”

blocks.text: Clear descriptions help us:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: judge whether a strategy is helping;
communicate accurately with teachers or professionals;
separate the child’s identity from the behaviour.

blocks.text: “He is aggressive” describes a child. “He hit twice this week when another child took his toy” describes a behaviour we can investigate and address.
The ABC Behaviour Map
One of the simplest ways to understand a repeated behaviour is to examine three parts:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: A — What Happened Before?
This is sometimes called the antecedent , but you do not need to remember that word. Ask:

items: What had the child been doing?
What did an adult say or do?
Was something removed, refused or changed?
Was the child hungry, tired, uncomfortable or overstimulated?
Was the task too difficult, too long or unclear?

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: B — What Did the Child Do?
Describe the behaviour without judgement. What could you see or hear? How long did it last? How intense was it?
C — What Happened After?
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: Did the child escape a noisy or difficult situation?
Did the parent change the answer?
Did the child eventually complete the task?

blocks.text: The “after” does not always cause the behaviour. However, it may help explain why the pattern continues.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: A Completed ABC Map

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

rows: Parent says screen time is finished
Child screams, kicks the sofa and grabs the device
Child jokes loudly and leaves the seat
Worksheet is removed while the class settles
Parent stops and scolds the older child for several minutes

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

rows: Child screams and holds tightly to it
Parent gives a short instruction and waits nearby
Child puts shoes in the cupboard
Parent says, “You put them away the first time I asked—thank you.”

blocks.text: The purpose of this table is not to criticise anyone. It is to make invisible patterns visible.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Five Common Messages Behind Behaviour
Behaviour can have several influences at once, but these five questions provide a practical starting point.
1. Is the Child Trying to Obtain Something?
The child may want:
Example

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: A child screams in the shop after being refused a toy.
What the child needs to learn

items: when the desired item or activity may be available.

blocks.text: A helpful response
“You really want that toy. We are not buying it today. You may take a photograph for your wish list or help me find the next item.”
The alternative should not secretly reverse the boundary.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: 2. Is the Child Trying to Avoid or Escape Something?
The child may be avoiding:
Example
A six-year-old develops a stomach ache every school morning. It would be unhelpful to assume either:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: “The child is pretending.”
or:
“The child must be seriously ill.”
The parent should consider physical health and also explore whether schoolwork, separation, friendship, bullying, toileting or another concern is involved.
What the child needs

The response depends on the reason. A child avoiding a task because it is challenging may need the task broken into smaller steps. A frightened child may need reassurance and gradual support. A child facing bullying needs adult protection—not merely encouragement to be brave.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: 3. Is the Child Seeking Connection or Attention?
Attention is a genuine developmental need. Children may seek it through:

items: breaking a rule that reliably brings an adult close.

blocks.text: Calling behaviour “attention-seeking” can sound dismissive. A more useful phrase is:
connection-seeking through an unhelpful behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Example
Every time a parent begins a work call, the child interrupts loudly.
What may help

items: explain the plan before the call;
give a small dose of focused attention first;
use a visible signal for when interruption is allowed;
keep expectations suitable for the child’s age;

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: The solution is not unlimited attention on demand. It is helping the child learn a more successful way to request and wait for connection.
4. Is the Child Trying to Regulate Their Body?
Some behaviours help children manage physical or sensory needs. A child may:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: These behaviours should not automatically be labelled naughty—or diagnosed by an unqualified person. Observe:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: whether the child appears calmer afterward;
whether it interferes with daily life;
whether broader developmental concerns are present.

blocks.text: If sensory or regulation differences are persistent or significantly affect the child, discuss them with a qualified health or developmental professional.
5. Is the Child Communicating a Feeling or Missing Skill?

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Sometimes the behaviour is the child’s best available solution. The child may not yet know how to:

items: say that a task is confusing;
cope with a change of plan.

blocks.text: Punishing the behaviour without teaching the missing skill leaves the child with the same problem and no better answer.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Find the Replacement Behaviour
“Stop doing that” tells a child what must end. Effective guidance also teaches what should begin.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

rows: “Can I have it when you’re finished?”
Say “Stop,” move away or call an adult

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

rows: Choose one last activity, then hold an adult’s hand
Place a hand gently on the parent’s arm and wait for acknowledgement
Ask for a short break or one smaller step

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

rows: Tell what happened and help repair it
Ask for an approved snack or check the meal routine

blocks.text: Replacement behaviours must be:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: taught while the child is calm;

blocks.text: A child who is told to “use your words” still needs to know which words. Give the actual sentence:
“Say, ‘Can I have a turn?’”
Prevention Is Part of Parenting
Parents often focus on what to do after behaviour begins. Sometimes the most effective change happens beforehand.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Prepare for Predictable Difficulties
If leaving the park is always hard:

items: avoid leaving only when the child is exhausted;
state the boundary before play begins.

blocks.text: Adjust the Environment

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: If a child repeatedly touches fragile objects, move them temporarily rather than creating twenty unnecessary battles. Environmental changes do not replace teaching. They reduce avoidable conflict while the child develops.
Make the Task Manageable
“Clean your room” may be too broad. Try:
“Put the books on the shelf. I’ll start the blocks with you.”
Use Visual Support
Young children may benefit from:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Offer Limited Choices
Choice supports independence when both options are acceptable.
“Would you like the red cup or the blue cup?”
“Will you brush your teeth before or after putting on your pyjamas?”
Do not offer a choice when there is none. “Would you like to leave now?” creates confusion if staying is not possible.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: What Happens After Behaviour Matters

Children learn from outcomes. This does not mean every behaviour needs a punishment. It means adults should respond in a way that does not accidentally strengthen the problem while actively supporting the desired behaviour.
Give Attention to What You Want Repeated
Instead of noticing only mistakes, describe useful behaviour:
“You waited while I finished speaking.”
“You were angry, and you kept your hands safe.”
“You asked for help instead of throwing the puzzle.”

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: This is sometimes called labelled praise because it names the behaviour being encouraged. Compare:
“Good girl.”
with:
“You put the crayons back in the box when you finished.”
The second statement tells the child exactly what worked.
Keep the Original Boundary
If the answer changes only after screaming, screaming may become more likely. You can comfort the child without changing the decision:
“I know you’re disappointed. The answer is still no. I’m here with you.”
Use Related Outcomes

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Whenever possible, the response should connect logically to what happened.

items: Water deliberately spilled during play is cleaned up with adult help.
A toy used to hurt someone is temporarily put aside.
A child who draws on the wall helps wipe it.
A game pauses when players cannot keep one another safe.

blocks.text: The goal is learning and repair—not suffering.
Return to Connection

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Once the situation is addressed, the relationship does not need to remain cold. A limit should not become hours of rejection.
Be Careful with Ignoring
Some minor behaviours may decrease when they no longer receive a large reaction. However, “ignore it” is not a universal parenting strategy. Never ignore:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: behaviour that places another person at risk.

blocks.text: Even when reducing attention to harmless whining or repeated complaining, remain available to notice appropriate communication immediately:
“I can hear you now that you’re using your regular voice.”
Ignoring the child is different from withholding attention from a specific, harmless behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: The Behaviour Conversation Comes Later
During emotional overload, the child may be unable to reflect usefully. First:
Later, when calm, have a short conversation. Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Avoid turning the conversation into a courtroom. A child may not fully understand why they acted as they did. The parent can still help create a plan.
“You were angry when Aarav took the truck. Next time, say ‘I’m using it,’ and call me if you need help.”
One Behaviour, Different Responses

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Imagine a child leaves the dinner table repeatedly. The correct response depends on the pattern.
Possibility 1: The meal is too long
The expectation may need to become more age-appropriate.
Possibility 2: Leaving brings exciting attention
Repeated chasing and arguing may be strengthening the pattern.
Possibility 3: The child is no longer hungry
The family may need a clear mealtime routine rather than forcing food.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Possibility 4: Sitting is physically difficult
A stable seat, foot support or shorter meal may help.
Possibility 5: The child is avoiding an unfamiliar food
Pressure may be increasing anxiety around eating.
Possibility 6: There is a broader feeding or developmental concern
Professional assessment may be appropriate. The behaviour alone does not tell us which explanation is correct. Observation does.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
1. Searching for One Perfect Explanation
Behaviour is influenced by development, health, environment, learning, relationships and circumstances. More than one factor may be involved.
2. Assuming Motive
“You wanted to embarrass me” attributes an adult intention the child may not have. Describe and investigate instead.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: 3. Giving the Child What They Want After Escalation
This can unintentionally teach that a larger reaction is more effective.
4. Removing Every Difficult Demand
If behaviour always makes an ordinary, manageable task disappear, avoidance may grow stronger. Support the child and adjust the task—but help them return to it when appropriate.
5. Focusing Only on Misbehaviour
Desired behaviour needs attention and practice too.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: 6. Choosing a Replacement Skill That Is Too Difficult
“Control yourself” is not a concrete skill. “Put your hands in your pockets and call me” is.
7. Expecting Immediate Change
A new response may initially produce stronger protest, especially if the old behaviour previously worked. Consistency and safety matter.
8. Using the ABC Map to Blame
The map is a learning tool, not evidence that someone caused the child’s difficulties.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

items: Someone claims to know a child’s motive without observing the context.
Behaviour advice relies on fear, humiliation, pain or withholding affection.
A strategy asks adults to ignore dangerous behaviour or genuine distress.
A child is forced repeatedly into a situation that appears frightening or painful.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: The behaviour is described as manipulation when the child may have communication or developmental difficulties.
Parents are advised to remove food, sleep, toileting access or necessary comfort as punishment.
A professional dismisses the family’s observations without asking when, where and how the behaviour occurs.
The child’s functioning is deteriorating across home, school or relationships.
Behaviour suddenly changes after a particular person, place or event.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: A sudden or concerning behavioural change deserves thoughtful attention. Safety and possible health, developmental or safeguarding concerns must come before behaviour management.
When to Seek Additional Support
Discuss the behaviour with a qualified professional when:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: it is severe, frequent or increasing;
the child or another person is regularly injured;
it substantially affects family life, learning, friendships, sleep or daily care;
similar concerns occur in more than one setting;
language, communication, movement, attention or social development also concerns you;
the behaviour begins suddenly without a clear explanation;
you suspect pain, illness, trauma, bullying, abuse or another safety issue;

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: ordinary parenting adjustments have not helped;
the caregiver is exhausted, frightened or struggling to respond safely.

blocks.text: Bring a short behaviour record. Specific observations are more useful than labels.
The Seven-Day Behaviour Record
Choose only one repeated behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: At the end of the week, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

items: Does it happen at a particular time?
Is hunger, tiredness or overstimulation involved?
Does the behaviour help the child obtain or avoid something?
What response from adults usually follows?
What replacement skill should be taught?
What can we change before the behaviour begins?

blocks.text: Do not track every difficulty. That becomes exhausting. Observe one pattern carefully enough to make a thoughtful change.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: A Simple Behaviour Plan
How we will prevent predictable difficulties: ____________________________________________________
How we will respond if the behaviour occurs: ____________________________________________________

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: How we will notice and encourage the new skill: ____________________________________________________

When we will review the plan: ____________________________________________________ All regular caregivers should understand the plan. Perfectly identical responses are unrealistic, but the central boundary should not change from one adult to another.
Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Behaviour is information, not permission. Understanding what a child may be communicating helps us respond wisely. It does not require us to accept hitting, unsafe actions or broken boundaries. Look for the pattern. Identify the need or missing skill. Teach a safer, clearer alternative. Notice when the child uses it. Then practise again. Children do not need adults who can interpret every feeling perfectly. They need adults who are willing to observe, remain curious and teach patiently.
Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Choose one behaviour you usually describe with a label. Replace the label with an observation. Instead of:
“She is demanding.”
write:
“She asks repeatedly and raises her voice after I say no.”
Then complete this sentence:
“She may be trying to ____________________. The skill I can teach is ____________________.”
Finally, prepare one sentence for the next time it happens:
A clearer response begins with a clearer understanding of the behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You
What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You
What Behaviour Is Trying to Tell You

blocks.text: Editorial note for review: Behavioural guidance, safeguarding language and referral pathways should be reviewed for the intended Indian edition by qualified paediatric, developmental, mental-health and child-protection professionals. Persistent or sudden behaviour change may have medical, developmental, educational, psychological, environmental or safeguarding causes and should not be addressed through discipline alone.

pages.epigraph: Your child may understand a rule before their developing brain can apply it reliably.

blocks.text: The Paint on the Wall

Six-year-old Ishaan knew that paint belonged on paper. He had heard the rule many times. One afternoon, while his father was answering the door, Ishaan decided to paint an enormous blue road across the bedroom wall. When his father returned, he stared in disbelief. “You know you’re not allowed to paint on the wall!” Ishaan nodded. “Then why did you do it?” Ishaan looked at the floor. “I don’t know.” Adults often hear those words as avoidance.

Sometimes they are simply true. At the moment Ishaan imagined the blue road, the exciting idea was stronger than his ability to pause, remember the rule, predict the consequence and make a different choice. He knew the rule. He had not yet mastered the mental skills required to follow it in every situation. That distinction matters. It does not mean his father should ignore the wall. Ishaan can help clean it, the paints can be stored differently and the rule can be practised again.
But the most useful response begins with development—not humiliation.
A Developing Brain Is Not a Smaller Adult Brain
Young children are not adults with less information. Their brains and abilities are still developing. Between two and seven, children make remarkable progress in:

But progress is uneven. A child may speak like an older child and still struggle to wait. They may explain the rule perfectly after an incident but fail to remember it while excited. They may cope well at school and fall apart after coming home. They may manage disappointment on Monday and be overwhelmed by the same experience on Tuesday. Development does not move in a perfectly straight line. Skills are influenced by:
This is why “but she knows better” does not always tell the whole story.
The Brain’s Management Skills
A useful group of mental abilities is often called executive function . The term sounds technical, but the skills are part of everyday life. They help a child:

items: move from one activity to another;

blocks.text: Three closely connected abilities are especially useful for parents to understand.
1. Working Memory
Working memory helps us hold information in mind long enough to use it. A child uses working memory when they:

items: remember what they were asked to bring;
keep a rule in mind while playing;
hold the beginning of a sentence while forming the end;
use instructions while completing a task.

blocks.text: What Difficulty May Look Like
The parent says:
“Put the cup in the sink, bring your shoes and wait by the door.”
The child puts the cup down, notices a toy and begins playing. The parent thinks:
“She ignored me.”
The child may simply have lost the instruction.
How Parents Can Help

items: Give one or two steps at a time.
Ask the child to repeat the direction.
Use pictures or a short checklist.
Keep routines in the same order.
Help the child begin the first step.

blocks.text: Instead of:
“Get everything ready for school.”
try:
“First, put your water bottle in the bag.”
Then give the next step. Support is not doing everything for the child. It is making the task manageable enough for the child to practise successfully.
2. Inhibitory Control
Inhibitory control helps us pause, resist an impulse and choose what to do. A child uses it when they:

items: stop at the edge of the road;

blocks.text: Knowing Is Not Yet Stopping
A child may know:
“I must not grab.”
But the toy appears, desire takes over and the hand moves before the child pauses. The skill is not absent. It is unreliable.
How Parents Can Help

items: Stay physically close in high-risk situations.
Remove temptations that exceed the child’s current ability.
Practise stopping and waiting through games.
Give reminders before the difficult moment.
Avoid setting the child up to fail repeatedly.

blocks.text: A three-year-old near a busy road needs an adult’s hand or another safe physical arrangement. A verbal reminder alone is not an adequate safety plan.
3. Cognitive Flexibility
Cognitive flexibility helps us change perspective, adjust to new information and move from one plan to another. A child uses it when they:

items: follow a different rule in a new setting;
shift from playtime to bath time;
understand that another person has a different idea;
cope when the preferred cup is unavailable;
recover when a game does not go as expected.

blocks.text: What Difficulty May Look Like

A child asks for the blue bowl. The blue bowl is being washed. The parent offers the green one. The child cries as though something terrible has happened. To an adult, the bowls are almost identical. To the child, the plan has collapsed.
How Parents Can Help

items: Prepare children for changes when possible.
Practise small changes when the child is calm.
Name the problem and help find another plan.
Avoid mocking distress over things that seem minor.

blocks.text: You might say:
“You expected the blue bowl. It isn’t ready. That is disappointing. You can use the green bowl or wait while I wash the blue one.”
The child may still be unhappy. The goal is not to make every change pleasant. It is to help flexibility grow.
The Skills Work Together
Imagine a child playing a game in which the rule suddenly changes. To adjust successfully, the child needs to:
Working memory, inhibitory control and flexible thinking work together. Everyday instructions can demand the same combination.
“Stop building, remember where the blocks belong and change to getting ready for bed.”
What sounds like one instruction may require several developing brain skills. This does not mean parents should never expect cooperation. It means we should make cooperation easier to achieve.
When Feelings Become Bigger Than Skills
Executive skills are harder to use when a child is:

Adults experience this too. We may know that shouting is unhelpful and still raise our voice when overwhelmed. We may know where the keys belong and still lose them during a rushed morning. The difference is that adults have had many more years to develop strategies. Young children often borrow organisation and calm from the adults around them. This is sometimes called co-regulation .
What Co-Regulation Means

Co-regulation does not mean the parent must make every feeling disappear. It means the adult provides enough safety, structure and support for the child to move gradually toward regulation. That may include:

items: helping the child breathe or slow down;
waiting until the child can think again;

blocks.text: A child eventually learns self-regulation through many experiences of being supported, practising skills and recovering from difficulty. The adult does not “regulate the child” like switching off a machine. The child is an active participant whose capacity grows over time.
Calm First, Teach Later
When a child is highly overwhelmed, this is usually not the moment for a lecture.
During the Difficult Moment
Focus on:
You might say:
“I won’t let you hit. I’m moving the toys. I’m here when your body is ready.”
After the Child Is Calm
Then teach:
“You were angry when the tower fell. Next time, say, ‘Help me,’ or move away from the blocks.”
A calm child can listen, remember and practise. An overwhelmed child may only hear more noise.
The Parent’s Nervous System Is in the Room Too

Co-regulation is difficult when the parent is dysregulated. A child screams. The parent’s heart beats faster. The parent feels judged, disrespected or helpless. The adult’s voice rises. The child becomes more distressed. Soon, two overwhelmed nervous systems are reacting to each other. Parents are human. Remaining perfectly calm is not a realistic standard. The practical goal is to recognise escalation early enough to choose one stabilising action.
Try:

items: lowering your voice instead of raising it;
placing both feet on the floor;
taking one slow breath before speaking;
asking another trusted adult to take over;
stepping back briefly when the child is safe;

blocks.text: A parent can say:
“I am becoming too angry to speak kindly. I’m going to take a moment. You are safe, and I will come back.”
Taking a safe pause is different from abandoning or frightening the child.
Scaffolding: Help That Changes as the Child Grows

Builders use scaffolding to support a structure while it is being created. Parents provide a similar kind of temporary support. At first, the adult may do much of the organising. Later, the child takes over more. Consider packing a school bag.
Stage 1: Adult Leads
The parent places everything in the bag while naming each item.
Stage 2: Child Helps
The parent says:
“Find your water bottle.”
Stage 3: Visual Support
The child follows a picture checklist.
Stage 4: Child Leads
The child packs independently while the parent checks afterward.
Stage 5: Independent Skill

The child remembers and manages the routine alone most days. If the parent withdraws all support too early, the child may repeatedly fail. If the parent never withdraws support, the child has little opportunity to become capable. Good scaffolding changes with the child.
Match the Support to the Moment
A skill the child usually manages may temporarily require more help.

rows: A changed plan or professional guidance

blocks.text: Supporting a tired child more than usual does not undo independence. It recognises that capacity changes with circumstances.
Everyday Activities That Build Brain Skills
Children strengthen executive skills through ordinary life and play.
Games with Stopping and Starting
Try:
These invite children to listen, remember and pause.
Matching and Sorting
Sort:
Then change the sorting rule to practise flexibility.
Turn-Taking Games

Simple board games, ball play and shared building help children practise waiting, remembering rules and managing disappointment. Keep the game suitable for the child’s age. A game that is far too difficult practises frustration more than self-control.
Storytelling
Pause during a story and ask:

items: “How else could the character solve the problem?”

blocks.text: Pretend Play
Pretend play allows children to practise roles, rules, planning and perspective-taking.
Household Participation

Cooking, setting the table, watering plants and organising belongings involve sequencing, memory and responsibility. Children do not need expensive “brain-training” products. Responsive interaction, conversation, play, movement, sleep, nutrition, safety and opportunities to participate all support healthy development.
Use Routines to Reduce Mental Load
Routines allow the brain to rely less on remembering and deciding. A morning routine might be:

The sequence becomes familiar. The parent does not need to invent new instructions every morning, and the child gradually knows what comes next. A routine should serve the family. It does not need to be exact to the minute, and it should be flexible enough for illness, travel and ordinary life. Visual routines can be especially helpful for young children and children who benefit from additional structure.
Practise Before the Difficult Moment
Parents often teach a skill only after it has gone wrong. Practise when nobody is upset.
Road Safety
At home, rehearse:
“When I say ‘stop,’ freeze your feet.”
Make it a game before relying on it outdoors.
Turn-Taking
Use a short timer while playing together.
Asking for Help
Create a deliberately difficult puzzle and practise saying:
“Please help me.”
Handling a Change
During pretend play, announce:
“The teddy planned to take the bus, but the bus is not coming. What else can Teddy do?”
Calming the Body
Explore strategies while calm:
A strategy practised only during distress may be difficult to remember.
What Developmentally Appropriate Does Not Mean
“Developmentally appropriate” does not mean:

items: the child always gets their way;
every child of the same age behaves identically;

blocks.text: It means the expectation, instruction and support fit the child’s present abilities. The goal is growth. Growth needs both warmth and structure.
Avoid Turning Brain Science into a Label
Parents may hear phrases such as:

Simple metaphors can sometimes help adults pause and respond more thoughtfully. But they can also create false certainty. A parent cannot see exactly what is happening inside a child’s brain during an ordinary conflict. Use brain science to become more compassionate and practical—not to diagnose, excuse or reduce a child to a slogan. It is usually enough to say:
“My child is overwhelmed and cannot use the skill well right now.”
Then respond to what you can observe.
Common Mistakes
1. Confusing Intelligence with Self-Control
A verbally advanced child may still have age-typical difficulty waiting, shifting attention or managing disappointment.
2. Giving Too Many Instructions
Long chains overload working memory.
3. Expecting Skills to Be Consistent Under Stress
A child who can cope while rested may need additional help when overwhelmed.
4. Removing All Frustration
Children need manageable opportunities to wait, try, fail, adjust and recover. The aim is not a frustration-free childhood. It is supported practice.
5. Demanding Independence Too Early
Repeated failure can create conflict and shame. Break the task into achievable steps.
6. Helping Too Much for Too Long
Children also need time to attempt tasks, make small mistakes and develop competence.
7. Lecturing During Emotional Overload
Reduce words and return to teaching later.
8. Using Neuroscience to Excuse Harm
Development may explain hitting. The adult must still stop it and teach a safer response.
9. Comparing Children
Development varies. Compare the child primarily with their own previous abilities and seek guidance when a pattern concerns you.
10. Treating Regulation as the Child’s Job Alone
The environment, adult response, predictability and relationship all influence a young child’s ability to cope.
RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

items: A product promises to rapidly increase a child’s intelligence or “rewire” the brain.
Ordinary childhood behaviour is diagnosed through social-media content.
Brain language is used to excuse violence or remove all boundaries.
A child is pushed through distress because an adult claims it will strengthen the brain.
Parents are blamed for every developmental difference.
A programme promises that one technique works for every child.
Sleep, nutrition, movement, safety and responsive care are ignored in favour of expensive activities.
A child loses abilities they previously demonstrated.
Developmental concerns are dismissed solely because every child develops differently.
A caregiver is told that seeking an assessment will unnecessarily label the child.

blocks.text: Accurate assessment can clarify needs and open access to helpful support. It does not change who the child is.
When to Seek Additional Support
Speak with a qualified paediatric or child-development professional if:

items: your child has lost previously acquired skills;
you have concerns about language, movement, learning, attention, play or social interaction;
the child struggles far more than peers with ordinary routines across several settings;
emotional reactions are unusually intense, prolonged or disruptive;
the child cannot participate in preschool, school or family life without substantial difficulty;
teachers or other regular caregivers share similar observations;
there has been a sudden and unexplained change;
your child appears distressed by their own difficulties;

blocks.text: Developmental screening and assessment should use appropriate professional tools and consider the whole child—not one isolated behaviour.
The Support Ladder
Choose one task your child finds difficult.
Step 1 — Make the expectation clear
Step 2 — Reduce the number of steps
Step 3 — Add a support
Could you use:
Step 4 — Notice success
Step 5 — Reduce help gradually
Step 6 — Review
Is the task becoming easier? What still causes difficulty? ____________________________________________________
A Pause Plan for Parents
Complete this while calm.
What I will do if I fear I may respond unsafely: ____________________________________________________ A safety plan is responsible parenting, not evidence of failure.
Remember This

A child can know a rule and still need help using it. Working memory develops. Impulse control develops. Flexible thinking develops. Emotional regulation develops. Parents support that development by creating safety, keeping expectations realistic, breaking difficult tasks into steps, practising skills and holding clear boundaries. You are not lowering the standard when you provide appropriate support. You are building the path that helps your child reach it.
Try This Today
Choose one instruction your child often struggles to follow. Make it easier for the developing brain:

items: Use a gesture or visual cue.
Help the child begin if necessary.
Notice the exact behaviour when it happens.

blocks.text: Instead of:
“Why are you never ready on time?”
try:
“Please put your shoes on now. I’ll hold the bag while you begin.”
One small change in the instruction may reveal that the child needed support—not a louder reminder.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

items: [World Health Organization — Nurturing Care for Early Childhood Development](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241514064/)

[Center on the Developing Child at Harvard University — Enhancing and Practising Executive Function Skills](https://developingchild.harvard.edu/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/Enhancing-and-Practicing-Executive-Function-Skills-with-Children-from-Infancy-to-Adolescence-1.pdf)

blocks.text: Editorial note for review: The brain-development explanations in this chapter are intentionally simplified for general readers. They should be reviewed by qualified paediatric and developmental professionals for accuracy, inclusion and suitability for the intended Indian edition. Developmental differences must not be diagnosed from this chapter; concerns should be assessed individually.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have
pages.epigraph: Parenting becomes clearer when we stop asking why a child cannot be more like someone else and begin learning how this particular child meets the world.

blocks.text: Two Children at the Birthday Party

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: When the family arrived at the birthday party, five-year-old Mira ran straight towards the balloons. Within minutes, she was introducing herself to children she had never met. Her younger cousin, Rohan, stood behind his father’s legs. Relatives called to him. “Come here!” “Don’t be shy.” “Look at Mira. She’s already playing.” The more they encouraged him, the more tightly he held his father. After twenty minutes, Rohan began watching the other children.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: After forty minutes, he moved towards the toys. By the time the cake was served, he was participating happily. Mira and Rohan were not examples of confidence and weakness. They were two children approaching a new situation differently. Mira moved towards novelty quickly. Rohan needed time to observe before joining. Neither style was wrong. The difficulty began when the adults expected one child to enter the room like the other.
What Is Temperament?

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Temperament describes a child’s characteristic way of responding to people, experiences and the environment. It can influence:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: how active the child tends to be;
how quickly they approach unfamiliar people or situations;
how easily they adjust to change;
how sensitive they are to sound, touch, taste or other stimulation;
how long they remain with an activity;
how quickly they recover after frustration.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Temperament has biological foundations, but it is not a fixed destiny. A child’s behaviour is also shaped by:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: A cautious child can become socially confident. An intense child can learn to express strong feelings safely. A highly active child can develop concentration. Understanding temperament tells us where a child may need support. It does not tell us who the child will become.
Temperament Is Not Character

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: A child’s natural style should not be confused with moral character. A slow-moving child is not lazy. A cautious child is not weak. An active child is not bad. An intense child is not dramatic. A persistent child is not automatically stubborn. A child who notices every sound is not deliberately difficult. These descriptions matter because labels influence how adults respond. Consider the difference:
“She is so stubborn.”
and:
“She finds it difficult to change plans once she has formed an idea.”

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: The first closes the conversation. The second suggests what may help: preparation, limited choices, practice with flexibility and a calm boundary.
Every Trait Has More Than One Side
The same temperament trait can appear helpful in one situation and challenging in another.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

rows: Difficulty remaining still or slowing down
Calm, observant and content with quiet play
May require more time to begin or finish
May hesitate with new people or places

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

rows: May enter situations without enough caution
Determined and willing to keep trying
May resist stopping or changing direction
Notices changes and can be redirected

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

rows: May not notice instructions or transitions
Reactions may become loud or overwhelming
Needs or distress may be missed

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

rows: Perceptive and aware of subtle changes
May be overwhelmed by noise, texture or emotion
May agree before expressing a preference
Changes and transitions may require more support

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: A trait is not a problem simply because it inconveniences an adult. The important questions are:

items: Can the family meet essential needs?
Is the child learning to manage the trait?
Does the environment provide an appropriate fit?
Is the pattern causing significant distress or impairment?

blocks.text: Look at Patterns, Not Boxes
Children do not fit neatly into one temperament type. A child may be:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: cautious with people but adventurous physically;
highly active outdoors but deeply focused while drawing;
easygoing at home but slow to adapt at school;
sensitive to noise but comfortable with change;
persistent with preferred activities but easily distracted during routine tasks.

blocks.text: Temperament is better understood as a collection of tendencies than as a single category. Avoid declaring:
“She is an introvert.”
“He is a difficult child.”
“She is the easy one.”

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: “He has a strong personality.”
These descriptions may become roles the family keeps reinforcing. Observe specifically instead:
“She usually watches unfamiliar groups before joining.”
“He responds strongly when a preferred activity ends.”
“She needs more reminders when there is background noise.”
Specific observations help. Global labels limit.
Nine Areas Parents Can Observe
You do not need to score or diagnose your child. Simply notice patterns over time.
1. Activity Level

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: How much movement does the child naturally seek?
A child with higher activity may:

items: need physical play before a quiet task;

blocks.text: Helpful support

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: build safe movement into the day;
break long seated periods into shorter ones;
prepare movement before travel or events;

blocks.text: Do not expect a highly active child to become calm through repeated criticism. Teach where, when and how movement is safe.
2. Regularity

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: How predictable are sleep, appetite, energy and toileting patterns? Some children settle naturally into routines. Others remain less predictable even in a structured household.
Helpful support

items: maintain a general rhythm without demanding exact timing;
watch for early signs of hunger or tiredness;
prepare for variable needs when leaving home;
avoid turning food or sleep into a power struggle;
seek health guidance when a pattern concerns you.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: A routine creates predictability. It cannot force a body to eat or sleep on command.
3. Approach to New Experiences
Does the child move quickly towards unfamiliar people, foods and activities, or pause and observe?
For the quick-to-approach child
Teach safety:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: For the cautious child
Provide:

items: permission to warm up without public pressure.

blocks.text: Do not introduce a cautious child by saying:
“She is very shy.”
Try:
“She likes to watch for a little while before joining.”
That describes the process without making it an identity.
4. Adaptability

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: How easily does the child adjust after a change has occurred? A child may enter a new situation willingly but then struggle once the routine feels unfamiliar.
Helpful support

items: use pictures or a simple sequence;
preserve one familiar part of the routine;
provide a clear ending and reunion plan;

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Adaptation cannot always be rushed. But avoiding every new experience can make the unfamiliar remain frightening. The aim is gradual, supported participation.
5. Intensity

How strongly does the child express pleasure, anger, fear or disappointment? A highly intense child may laugh loudly, love enthusiastically and protest dramatically. A lower-intensity child may express significant distress very quietly.
Helpful support for high intensity

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: teach safe ways to express strong emotion;
avoid shaming the child for feeling deeply.

blocks.text: Helpful support for lower intensity

items: check in rather than assuming everything is fine;
give the child enough time to answer;
teach clear ways to communicate needs.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: The loudest child does not always have the greatest need. The quietest child does not always have the least.
6. General Mood
Some children appear cheerful much of the time. Others notice what is wrong before what is right. A child with a more serious or negative style may say:
Helpful support

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: avoid giving every complaint a large audience.

blocks.text: You might say:
“You don’t like this plan. Tell me the most difficult part.”
The goal is not to replace every negative feeling with positivity. It is to help the child express concern constructively and remain open to possibility.
7. Persistence and Attention

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: How long does the child stay with a task, particularly when it becomes difficult? A highly persistent child may work patiently but struggle to stop. A child with shorter persistence may change activities quickly or give up when frustrated.
Helpful support for strong persistence

items: allow important projects to remain unfinished;

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Helpful support for shorter persistence
Do not confuse temperament with a diagnosis. Persistent attention difficulties across settings should be discussed with a qualified professional when they affect daily functioning.
8. Distractibility

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: How readily does the child notice and shift towards sights, sounds or events? Distractibility can be useful when redirecting a distressed young child. It can become difficult when dressing, eating, learning or following instructions.
Helpful support

items: give one direction at a time;
place materials in a less distracting space;
help the child return without anger.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Instead of:
“You never listen.”
try:
“Your attention moved to the television. Look at me for the next step.”
9. Sensitivity
How strongly does the child respond to sensory and emotional stimulation? A sensitive child may notice:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Helpful support

items: believe the child’s experience even when you do not share it;
offer suitable clothing or food choices;
seek guidance when sensitivity substantially affects daily life.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Respecting sensitivity does not require removing every challenge. It means helping the child approach challenges safely and gradually.
The Goodness of Fit
A child’s temperament does not exist alone. It interacts with:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: This interaction is sometimes called goodness of fit . A highly active child may thrive in a family with frequent outdoor activity but struggle in a home where quietness is expected for long periods. A cautious child may cope well with a patient caregiver but feel overwhelmed by adults who demand immediate participation. A spontaneous parent and a child who needs predictability may frustrate each other. Neither person is necessarily wrong.
The fit needs work.
Your Temperament Matters Too

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Parents often focus entirely on the child. But your own natural style affects what you notice and what you find difficult. Perhaps you:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: value punctuality and become frustrated by a slow-moving child;
enjoy social events and worry about a cautious child;
need quiet and feel overwhelmed by an intense, talkative child;
prefer flexibility and dislike your child’s need for routine;
decide quickly and become impatient with a child who needs time;
are highly sensitive yourself and struggle with noise and conflict;
recover quickly while your child remains upset for a long time.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Self-awareness is not self-blame. It helps you separate:
“This behaviour is unsafe.”
from:
“This behaviour clashes with my personal preference.”
Both may need attention, but not in the same way.
A Parent–Child Fit Example
Aarav moves slowly in the morning. His mother, Naina, wakes quickly, makes decisions fast and values punctuality. Every school day, she says:
“Hurry up.”
Aarav becomes more distracted and slower. Naina interprets this as defiance. A better fit might include:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: giving one step at a time;
using a timer for one familiar task;
building in a small time cushion;

blocks.text: Aarav still needs to learn to prepare on time. Naina does not have to accept being late every day. The adaptation creates a better path to the same necessary goal.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Adapt the Approach, Not Every Boundary
Understanding temperament does not mean arranging the entire family around one child’s preferences. Some expectations remain necessary:

items: school or childcare attendance may be required;
medical care cannot always be avoided;
routines must serve more than one person;

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Adapt how the child reaches the expectation.
The Boundary
“We are going to school.”
Support for a cautious child
The Boundary
“It is time to leave the park.”

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Support for a persistent child
The Boundary
“We keep our bodies safe indoors.”
Support for an active child

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

items: state exactly where running is allowed.

blocks.text: The expectation remains. The route becomes more suitable.
Avoid Comparisons Between Siblings

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Siblings can have very different temperaments. One may sleep predictably. Another may not. One may greet every relative. Another may hide. One may complete homework quickly. Another may need movement and reminders. Comparison creates shame without teaching a skill. Avoid:
“Why can’t you sit like your sister?”
“Your brother never behaves this way.”
“She is the easy child.”

These statements can damage both children. The “easy” child may feel pressure to remain undemanding. The “difficult” child may begin living inside the role the family has assigned. Speak to each child about their own behaviour and progress.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Help Others Understand Your Child
Children interact with grandparents, teachers, childcare workers and extended family. Offer practical information without presenting a list of excuses. Instead of:
“He cannot handle anything new.”
say:
“He usually needs a few minutes to observe. Please greet him without asking him to perform, and he will often join when he feels ready.”
Instead of:
“She is hyperactive.”
say:
“She participates better after movement. Could she help carry the books before circle time?”
Instead of:
“He is extremely sensitive.”
say:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: “Loud hand dryers distress him. Please tell him before switching one on and allow him to stand farther away.”
Clear information helps other adults support the child effectively.
Temperament and Culture
Families and communities may value different qualities. Some value:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have
Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: A child’s temperament may fit some expectations more easily than others. Parents can teach family and cultural values without shaming the child’s natural style. For example, a cautious child can learn to greet an elder respectfully without being forced into physical affection. An expressive child can learn when a quieter voice is needed without being told that strong feelings are unacceptable. A highly independent child can learn family cooperation without having their initiative crushed.
The goal is not to choose between temperament and values. It is to teach values in a way the child can absorb.

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Temperament Is Not a Diagnosis
Temperament differences are part of ordinary human variation. However, behaviour that appears temperamental may sometimes reflect:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: Parents should not diagnose a child from a temperament description. Likewise, genuine concerns should not be dismissed as:

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: “That is just his personality.”
If a pattern significantly affects the child’s safety, learning, relationships or daily functioning, seek qualified guidance.
Common Mistakes
1. Dividing Children into “Easy” and “Difficult”
A child who is convenient for adults is not more worthy. A child who requires more support is not less good.
2. Treating Temperament as an Excuse

pages.chapterTitle: Temperament: Understanding the Child You Have

blocks.text: “He is intense” does not make hitting acceptable. The trait helps guide the teaching approach.
3. Treating Temperament as Fixed
Children develop skills. Environments change. Experience matters.
4. Trying to Remove the Trait
