The consequence involves contact with an unsafe person.
Adults force apologies to conceal ongoing harm.
Injuries are hidden or explained dishonestly.

blocks.text: Violence is not made safe by calling it discipline.
When to Seek Additional Support
Seek qualified support if:

items: aggressive or destructive behaviour is frequent or severe;
ordinary discipline repeatedly escalates into danger;
the child appears unable to understand expected behaviour;
the same problems occur across home, school and community settings;
language, attention, learning, sensory or developmental concerns are present;
the child appears frightened of a caregiver;
family members disagree in ways that undermine safety;
discipline has become physically or emotionally harmful;
the caregiver feels unable to stop hitting, shouting or humiliating;
reasonable, consistent approaches are not helping.

blocks.text: Parenting support is not an admission of failure. It can provide individual strategies, identify unmet needs and reduce family stress.
The Discipline Decision Guide
1. Is anyone unsafe?
Yes: Stop the behaviour and protect people immediately. No: Continue to the next question.
2. Is the expectation appropriate for the child’s age and ability?
No or unsure: Adjust the task or seek guidance. Yes: Continue.
3. Does the child know what to do instead?
No: Teach and practise the replacement behaviour. Yes: Continue.
4. Is a consequence needed?
Consider:

items: temporary removal of a related privilege;
planned selective attention for a harmless behaviour;
a carefully structured time-out, if the family uses it appropriately.

blocks.text: 5. What will reconnect the child with successful behaviour?
The Repair Plan
How the adult will support success next time: ____________________________________________________
Turn Punishment into Teaching
What I feel like saying or doing
What I need the child to learn
The related outcome
The replacement skill
The repair
The words I will use
Remember This

The purpose of discipline is not to make a child feel worse. It is to help the child behave better with growing understanding and skill. Stop harm. State the rule. Use a related, proportionate response. Teach what to do instead. Guide repair. Return to connection. Children need accountability without humiliation and guidance without violence. The strongest discipline does not prove that the adult has power. It helps the child gradually develop power over their own choices.
Try This Today
Choose one repeated behaviour. Complete these four sentences:

items: The behaviour I want to reduce is:
The skill my child needs instead is:

blocks.text: ________________________________________________ The next time the behaviour occurs, keep the response short enough to teach:
Authoritative References for This Chapter

items: [American Academy of Pediatrics — Effective Discipline to Raise Healthy Children](https://publications.aap.org/pediatrics/article/142/6/e20183112/37452/Effective-Discipline-to-Raise-Healthy-Children)

blocks.text: A Note on Indian Law

Indian law treats discipline at school and at home differently. The Right of Children to Free and Compulsory Education Act, 2009 (Section 17) bans all physical punishment and mental harassment in schools, and the National Commission for Protection of Child Rights defines physical punishment broadly—any action causing pain or discomfort, however light. A school is not permitted to hit, cane or humiliate a child, and this can be reported to the school itself, the District Education Officer, or the National or State Commission for Protection of Child Rights.

Within the home, Indian law is less absolute: Section 27 of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023 provides a “good faith” defence for a guardian’s reasonable disciplinary action toward a child under twelve, but this does not cover the use of an implement (belt, stick or similar), or force severe enough to cause injury—both can still amount to an offence under the law on hurt. This companion’s guidance goes further than the legal minimum: the research on child development is consistent that any form of hitting is minimally effective and carries real developmental risk, whatever the law permits, and that guidance stands regardless of how common the practice remains in Indian households today.

Editorial note for review: This section reflects general legal information current as of 2026, not legal advice, and state-level variations should be checked by a qualified Indian child-protection or family-law professional before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour
pages.epigraph: Young children may use their bodies before they can use self-control, language and problem-solving reliably. The behaviour must be stopped—and the missing skill must be taught.

blocks.text: The Bite at Playgroup

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Three-year-old Nivaan was building a road with wooden blocks when another child reached for the longest piece. Nivaan pulled it back. The other child grabbed harder. Before either adult could reach them, Nivaan bit the child’s arm. The room became silent. One parent rushed towards the crying child. Another said:
“What is wrong with him?”
Nivaan’s mother felt shock, embarrassment and fear. Was her child becoming aggressive? Had she failed to teach kindness? Should she make him apologise immediately? The first priorities were simpler:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: remain calm enough not to retaliate;
understand what led to the incident;
teach Nivaan what to do instead;
prevent the same situation where possible.

blocks.text: One bite did not define Nivaan. But it could not be ignored either.
Common Does Not Mean Acceptable
Young children sometimes hit, bite, kick, push, scratch or throw. Possible reasons include:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: pain or teething in younger children;
a desire to make someone move;

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: behaviour that has achieved a useful outcome before.

blocks.text: Understanding these possibilities helps adults respond effectively. It does not mean:
“Children will be children, so nothing needs to happen.”
The correct message is:
“This behaviour can occur during development, and adults must stop it, protect people and teach safer alternatives.”
The Immediate Response: STOP
Use the STOP sequence when behaviour becomes physically unsafe.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: S — Separate and Secure
Move between children or guide them apart. Remove dangerous objects. Protect the child who was hurt. Use only the physical intervention necessary to prevent immediate harm. Say:
“I won’t let you hit.”
“I’m moving you back to keep everyone safe.”
Do not wait for the aggressive child to choose safety independently once harm is occurring.
T — Tend to the Person Who Was Hurt
Check:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Give attention to the injured child first. This communicates that harm receives care rather than giving the aggressor a large, dramatic audience. Avoid forcing the injured child to:

items: resume playing before they are ready;
surrender the disputed object to keep peace.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Their body and sense of safety matter.
O — Offer a Brief Limit
Use few words:
“Hitting hurts. I won’t let you hit.”
“Biting is not safe.”
“Blocks are not for throwing at people.”
Avoid asking:
“Why did you do that?”
in the immediate moment. The child may not know, may be overwhelmed or may say whatever seems most likely to end the adult’s anger. Investigation and teaching come later.
P — Pause the Activity and Plan the Next Step
The activity may need to stop temporarily.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: “You are having trouble playing safely, so we are taking a break from the blocks.”
A pause is not revenge. It protects people and creates an opportunity to reset. When the child is calm enough, teach what to do next time.
Do Not Retaliate
Never:

items: pull hair to “show how it feels”;

blocks.text: Retaliation teaches:
“Larger people may use violence when they disapprove.”

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: It may stop behaviour temporarily through fear, but it does not teach self-control, communication or empathy. The adult must model the exact principle being taught:
Bodies are not for hurting.
Hitting
Children may hit when they want to:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Teach Specific Alternatives
For “Move away”:
“Say, ‘Move back,’ and hold your hand up.”
For “I want that”:
“Say, ‘Can I use it next?’”
For “I am angry”:
“Step back and call me.”
For “Stop touching me”:
“Say, ‘Stop. My body needs space.’”
For physical energy:
“You may push the wall, carry these cushions or run outside with me.”

Do not tell the child to punch another object as the only anger strategy. Rehearsing forceful striking can keep the body activated and may not teach the social skill needed in the original conflict. Offer a range of safe regulation choices.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Biting
Biting often alarms adults because it causes pain and can break the skin. Young children may bite because:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: they are frustrated and lack words;
they are experimenting with cause and effect;
the behaviour quickly makes another person move;
teething or mouth discomfort is present in younger children.

blocks.text: Immediate response

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: Follow appropriate first-aid and medical guidance, particularly if skin is broken.

blocks.text: “Biting hurts. I won’t let you bite.”

items: End or pause the disputed activity.

blocks.text: Teach the replacement
Practise:
“Move.”
“My turn.”
“Help.”
“Stop.”
“Too close.”
If language is limited, teach a gesture, picture or simple signal alongside words.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Prevention
Notice whether biting occurs:
Adjust supervision and the environment accordingly.
Throwing
Throwing is a normal physical skill. The problem is not always throwing itself. The questions are:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: What damage or danger is possible?

blocks.text: Teach categories
“Balls may be thrown outside.”
“Blocks stay on the floor.”
“Food stays on the table.”
“Nothing is thrown at people.”
If an object is thrown unsafely
“The truck was thrown at your sister, so I’m putting it away.”
Then offer an appropriate throwing activity later if the child is seeking movement:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Safe alternatives help the child practise control rather than merely suppress movement.
Kicking, Pushing and Scratching
The same principles apply:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Useful alternatives include:
“Say, ‘I need space.’”
“Stand behind this line.”
“Call an adult.”
“Move your body away.”
“Use your hands to hold your own toy.”
The child needs a response that can work quickly in the original situation.
Spitting
Spitting may occur because of:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: a desire for a strong reaction;

blocks.text: State the rule:
“Spit belongs in the sink or tissue, not on people.”

Clean the area without a dramatic performance. Have the child help appropriately when able. If drooling, swallowing, oral-motor control or repeated spitting raises a health or developmental concern, seek professional advice rather than treating it only as misconduct.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Destructive Behaviour
Tearing, breaking or overturning objects may occur during:
Immediate response
“I won’t let you break the chair.”
Move the object or child as needed.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Related outcome
“The book was torn. We need to repair it before using another.”
Investigation
Ask later:

items: Was the child curious or angry?
Did the child understand the object could break?
Does it occur in multiple settings?
Is the child experiencing significant stress?

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: The response should match the pattern, not merely the cost of the object.
Words Can Hurt Too
As language develops, physical behaviour may be joined or replaced by:
Do not say:
“At least he didn’t hit.”
Teach:
“You may say, ‘I’m angry.’ You may not call people names.”
“You may choose not to play. You may not organise everyone to humiliate one child.”

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Respond to the impact and pattern. Repeated intentional cruelty, coercion or bullying needs more than a reminder. Adults must protect the targeted child, investigate and coordinate with the relevant setting.
Understand the Trigger Without Excusing the Harm
Suppose a child hits after a sibling destroys their construction. Two things are true:

items: The child had a genuine reason to feel angry.

blocks.text: Say:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: “You worked hard, and your tower was knocked down. You are angry. I won’t let you hit. Next time, move your blocks and call me.”

Then address the sibling’s behaviour too. Do not focus only on the child whose reaction was loudest. Fair guidance investigates the entire conflict while holding each person responsible for their own behaviour.
Protect Siblings from Repeated Harm
Families sometimes expect siblings to tolerate aggression because:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Repeated harm is not an ordinary responsibility of siblinghood. Parents should:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: create an individual safety plan if one child is regularly targeted;
seek professional support when harm persists.

blocks.text: Do not make the injured child responsible for preventing the other child’s aggression.
Repair After Harm

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Repair begins after everyone is safe and regulated. Guide the child through appropriate steps.
1. Notice the impact
“Your friend is crying because the block hit her arm.”
Do not exaggerate or shame.
2. Check safety
“Let’s ask whether she needs help.”
The injured child may not want the aggressor close. Respect that.
3. Make practical repair
The child might:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: bring a cold pack with permission;

blocks.text: 4. Plan
“When someone takes your block, say, ‘I’m using it,’ and call the teacher.”
5. Allow another chance
The child should not be treated as permanently dangerous because of one incident. Return to supervised participation when appropriate.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Do Not Force Affection as Repair
Avoid:
“Give her a hug.”
“Kiss him and say sorry.”
The injured child may not want touch. The child who caused harm may perform affection without understanding. Ask:
“Would you like some space, words, or help fixing what happened?”
Repair should respect both children’s bodies.
Prevention Through Supervision
Young children need adults close enough to intervene before predictable harm. High-risk situations may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: a child entering another’s personal space;
play involving hard or heavy objects;

blocks.text: Supervision means more than being in the same building. It means being able to:

items: see or hear what is happening;

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: If aggression is recurring, increase supervision while teaching skills. Do not wait for another child to be hurt in order to test whether the problem has disappeared.
Change the Environment
Environmental support may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: providing duplicates of a highly disputed item;
offering active play before quiet play;
keeping safe sensory or movement alternatives available.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Changing the environment is not surrender. It prevents avoidable failure while skills develop.
Coordinate with Preschool or School
When an incident occurs outside the home, ask for facts rather than accepting a label. Useful questions include:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: Is there a pattern involving time, activity or peers?
What plan will be used next time?
How will adults protect all children?
When will the plan be reviewed?

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Avoid demanding confidential details about another child. Focus on your child’s behaviour, the setting’s response and the safety plan. Parents and educators should share strategies where possible.
Do Not Publicly Shame the Child
Avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: making the child confess before an audience;
calling the child a bully after an isolated event;
warning other children dramatically in front of them;

blocks.text: Accountability can be private, clear and firm. Children need the opportunity to learn without being trapped inside their worst moment.
When the Child Laughs After Hurting Someone

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Adults may interpret laughter as cruelty. Children sometimes laugh because they are:
Do not assume the meaning. State the issue:
“Your sister is hurt. This is serious. We are going to help her.”

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Observe the broader pattern. A single nervous laugh does not establish lack of empathy. Repeated enjoyment of another person’s distress deserves closer professional attention.
Notice Safe Behaviour
Do not wait for aggression to recognise progress. Say:
“You wanted the truck, and you asked for a turn.”
“You moved away when you became angry.”
“You kept your hands safe even though you were frustrated.”
“You called me before the fight grew.”
Specific acknowledgement strengthens the replacement behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
1. Retaliating
Hitting or biting back teaches violence.
2. Giving a Long Lecture Immediately
Safety and regulation come first.
3. Forcing an Apology
Repair requires more than a word.
4. Ignoring the Injured Child
Attend to the person who was hurt.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: 5. Labelling the Child
Describe the behaviour rather than defining the child.
6. Treating Every Incident as Equal
A two-year-old impulsively pushing is not the same as a repeated pattern of deliberate intimidation.
7. Excusing Repeated Harm as Developmental
Common behaviour still needs intervention.
8. Focusing Only on the Final Reaction
Investigate the whole conflict and environment.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: 9. Removing Every Opportunity to Socialise
Children need carefully supervised practice, not permanent exclusion.
10. Returning to the Same Situation Without a Plan
Hope is not a safety strategy.
11. Assuming Aggression Always Reflects Anger
Communication, sensory, attention, fear and learning factors may contribute.
12. Waiting Too Long to Seek Help

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Early support can protect the child, peers and family.
RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

items: A caregiver hits, bites or kicks the child back.
Adults encourage another child to retaliate.
Injuries are hidden or left untreated.
A child is repeatedly targeted and told to tolerate it.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: Aggression involves choking, weapons, fire or serious threats.
The child appears to enjoy causing fear or pain repeatedly.
Behaviour suddenly changes after contact with a particular person or setting.
There are unexplained injuries or signs of possible abuse.
Aggression occurs alongside loss of developmental skills.
The child is excluded repeatedly without assessment or a support plan.

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: Caregivers cannot keep siblings or peers safe.
An adult fears they may seriously hurt the child.

blocks.text: Immediate danger requires immediate local assistance.
When to Seek Additional Support
Speak with a qualified paediatric, developmental or mental-health professional if:

items: aggression is frequent, severe or increasing;
the behaviour appears across multiple settings;
ordinary activities require constant crisis management;

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: the child cannot play safely even with appropriate supervision;
language, social communication, attention, sensory processing, sleep or learning concerns are present;
the child has experienced trauma, violence, major loss or instability;
preschool or school is considering exclusion;
the child harms themselves, animals or vulnerable people;
consistent teaching and environmental changes are not helping;

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: For younger children with persistent behaviour difficulties, effective support often includes coaching caregivers in relationship-building and behaviour-management strategies tailored to the child and family. An assessment should look beyond the incident to health, development, communication, relationships, environment and safety.
The Safety-and-Skills Plan
Behaviour we need to stop

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: When it usually happens
Common triggers
Who may be at risk
Immediate safety response
The exact words adults will use

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: “I won’t let you ____________________. I’m going to ____________________ to keep everyone safe.”
The replacement skill
How we will practise it
Environmental changes
Supervision plan

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Repair steps
When we will seek additional help
Replacement-Language Practice
When I want a toy
“Can I have a turn?”
When someone is too close
“Move back, please.”

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: When I am angry
“I’m angry. I need help.”
When I want play to stop
“Stop. I don’t like that.”
When a task is too difficult
“Please help me.”
When I need space
“I need space.”
When words are difficult

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Choose a gesture, picture or agreed signal: ____________________________________________________ Practise these phrases through play, not only after an incident.
Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Safety comes first, but safety alone is not the whole lesson. Stop the hitting. Separate the children. Attend to the person who was hurt. Use a brief, firm rule. Investigate the trigger. Teach a specific alternative. Guide repair. Supervise practice. A child who hits, bites or throws is not beyond help—and the people around that child should not be asked to remain unsafe while learning occurs. Compassion for the child and protection for others belong in the same response.
Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Choose one unsafe behaviour that has occurred before. Complete this sentence:
Now choose the replacement:
“Instead, I will teach them to ____________________.”

Practise it three times while everyone is calm. Keep the words short enough for the child to use during a real conflict. The best replacement skill is not the most impressive one. It is the one the child can remember before their body acts.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

items: [American Academy of Pediatrics — Preventing Aggressive Behaviour in Young Children](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/toddler/Pages/Aggressive-Behavior.aspx)

[American Academy of Pediatrics — Addressing Behavioural Challenges in Preschool](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/preschool/Pages/preventing-preschool-expulsions-aap-policy-explained.aspx)

pages.chapterTitle: Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour
Hitting, Biting, Throwing and Other Difficult Behaviour

blocks.text: Editorial note for review: Guidance on aggression, biting injuries, restraint, first aid, school exclusion, crisis response and safeguarding should be reviewed by qualified paediatric, developmental, dental, mental-health, educational and child-protection professionals for the intended Indian edition. Local medical, emergency, school and reporting pathways must be added before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You
pages.epigraph: Clear communication is not about finding magic words that guarantee obedience. It is about making it easier for a child to understand what is expected and what happens next.

blocks.text: The Instruction from the Kitchen
Seven-year-old Myra was building a house with magnetic tiles in the living room. From the kitchen, her father called:
“Myra, clean everything up, wash your hands, put your bottle in your bag and come for dinner!”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Myra continued building. A few minutes later, he called again.
“Did you hear me?”
“Yes!” she replied. But she remained on the floor. By the third reminder, her father was angry.
“You never listen until I shout.”
Myra had heard the sound of his voice. That did not mean she had successfully:

items: shifted attention away from the tiles;
carried the whole sequence in mind.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: A clearer approach would begin differently. Her father could walk into the room, move close and say:
“Myra, look at me for the next step.”
After gaining her attention:
“It is time to stop building. Put the tiles in this box.”
When she began:
“Thank you. When the tiles are away, I’ll give you the next step.”
The task did not become optional. The communication became usable.
Hearing Is Not the Same as Processing
A child may hear words without fully processing or acting on them. Successful communication requires the child to:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: manage any feeling about the request;

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: This is why children may appear to ignore an instruction even when they heard it. Sometimes the child is resisting. Sometimes the direction was unclear, too long or poorly timed. Often both are involved. Better communication does not remove the need for boundaries and follow-through. It ensures the child has a fair opportunity to understand them.
Connect Before You Direct
Calling across rooms is convenient but unreliable. Before giving an important direction:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: place yourself within the child’s field of attention;
wait until the child acknowledges you;
use a gesture or visual cue if helpful.

blocks.text: You do not always need eye contact. Some children listen better while looking away, and forced eye contact can be uncomfortable or distracting. Try:
“Rohan, pause your hands and listen to this step.”
“Aanya, I need your attention for a moment.”
“Kabir, look towards me or show me you are listening.”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: The goal is shared attention—not submission through staring.
Choose the Moment
Communication is less effective when a child is:

items: in the middle of a conflict;
receiving several instructions from different adults.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Some directions cannot wait. Safety instructions must be immediate. But non-urgent teaching can often be timed better. Instead of explaining the sharing rule while two children are screaming, separate them and discuss it after calm returns. Instead of introducing tomorrow’s complex schedule at bedtime when the child is exhausted, discuss it earlier and use a visual plan. The best words can fail in the wrong moment.
Give One Clear Direction
A clear direction tells the child exactly what action is expected. Instead of:
“Be good.”
say:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “Keep your feet on the floor.”
Instead of:
“Get ready.”
say:
“Put on your shoes.”
Instead of:
“Sort out this mess.”
say:
“Place the books on the shelf.”
Instead of:
“Stop bothering me.”
say:
“Stand beside me and wait while I finish this sentence.”
Instead of:
“Why is your bag still here?”
say:
“Put your bag on the hook.”
Questions often hide directions. If you need action, state the action.
Tell the Child What to Do, Not Only What to Stop
“Don’t,” “no” and “stop” are necessary in some situations. A child running towards traffic needs:
“Stop!”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: But after stopping an unwanted action, provide the alternative.
“Stop. Stand beside me.”
“Don’t throw the spoon. Put it on the table.”
“No hitting. Move back and call me.”
“Don’t climb the shelf. Use the climbing frame outside.”
A negative instruction leaves an empty space. A positive direction fills it with a safer action.
Keep Instructions Short
The younger the child—or the more distracted, tired or stressed—the shorter the instruction should be. Compare:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “I have already asked you twice to put all these toys back where they belong because dinner is nearly ready and someone is going to trip.”
with:
“Put the cars in the blue basket.”
The explanation may be reasonable. It is simply competing with the action. Explain first if needed:
“We need a clear floor so nobody trips.”
Then direct:
“Put the cars in the blue basket.”
Do not hide the instruction inside a speech.
One Step at a Time
A long instruction can overload working memory. Instead of:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “Go upstairs, change your clothes, put the uniform in the laundry, bring your homework and wash your hands.”
try:
“Go upstairs and change your clothes.”
When that is complete:
“Put the uniform in the laundry basket.”
Older children may manage several steps, especially in a familiar routine. For repeated sequences, use a checklist so the child does not depend entirely on spoken reminders.
Ask the Child to Show Understanding
Avoid:
“Do you understand?”
Children often say yes because they want the conversation to end. Try:
“Tell me the first step.”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “Show me where the shoes go.”
“What will you do when the timer rings?”
This checks the communication without turning it into an interrogation. If the child cannot repeat the expectation, simplify it.
Allow Time to Respond
Some adults repeat a direction almost immediately.
“Put on your shoes.”
Two seconds later:
“Shoes, please.”
Then:
“How many times must I ask?”
A child may need a brief moment to:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Give the direction and pause. The amount of time depends on the child and task, but the adult should be able to see whether the child is beginning. If nothing happens, move to follow-through rather than endless repetition.
Follow Through After the Direction
Clear language without follow-through becomes another form of background noise. Use this sequence:

items: Give one reminder or warning if appropriate.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Example:
“Put the crayons in the box.”
Pause.
“The crayons need to be away before we eat. You may put them in, or I will help you.”
If the child does not begin:
“You are having trouble starting, so I’m helping.”
Follow-through may involve:
It should not involve escalating threats.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Use Choices Carefully
Choices can increase cooperation by giving the child some control. Useful choices are:
“Would you like to wear the red shirt or the white one?”
“Will you put away the animals or the cars first?”
“Do you want to walk to the bathroom or hop?”
“Would you like my help or try alone first?”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Avoid too many options. “Choose anything you want to wear” may overwhelm a young child. Avoid false choices.
“Do you want to go to school?”
is misleading if staying home is not available. Say:
“It is a school day. Would you like to carry your bag or your water bottle?”
Use “First–Then” Language
First–then language makes the sequence visible.
“First teeth, then story.”
“First shoes, then the park.”
“First we clean the paint, then we choose another activity.”
“First homework, then your agreed screen time.”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: This is not always a reward system. It often describes the ordinary order of events. The “then” should be something that will genuinely happen. Do not promise something you cannot provide.
Explain the Reason Briefly
Children benefit from understanding why a rule exists.
“Hold my hand because cars may not see you.”
“The medicine must be stored here because it is unsafe for children.”
“We use a quieter voice because the baby is sleeping.”
Explanations support learning. But a reason is not an invitation to debate indefinitely. After explaining:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “You may disagree. The rule remains.”
Avoid inventing false reasons simply to gain compliance. Children deserve honest, age-appropriate explanations.
Tone Matters, but Volume Is Not Authority
A firm voice can be calm. Firm communication is:
It does not require:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: If you usually begin softly and become serious only after ten repetitions, the child may learn that the early voice does not count. Use the same clear tone the first time.
Avoid the Question Trap
Parents often ask questions that invite argument.
“Why can’t you ever listen?”
“How would you like it if I did that to you?”
“What is wrong with you today?”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “Do you think this is acceptable?”
These questions are usually expressions of frustration, not genuine requests for information. They may produce:
Ask useful questions later:
“What made it difficult to stop?”
“Which step did you forget?”
“What could help you next time?”
Questions should help the parent understand or help the child plan.
Avoid “Always” and “Never”
“You never listen.”
“You always make us late.”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “You never remember anything.”
These statements turn a moment into an identity. They are also rarely accurate. Describe the present situation:
“Your shoes are not on yet, and we need to leave.”
“You did not stop when I called.”
“The bag was left on the floor today.”
Specific language makes change possible.
Avoid Shame and Comparison
Do not say:
“Even your little sister can do this.”
“Why can’t you behave like the other children?”
“A child your age should know better.”
Comparison may create resentment without teaching the skill. Address the child’s own action:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “You need help remembering the routine. Let’s use the checklist.”
Communication During Safety Emergencies
In immediate danger, do not prioritise a gentle explanation. Use direct commands:
“Stop!”
“Come back!”
“Drop it!”
“Stand still!”
Move physically to protect the child where necessary. Once safety is restored, explain and practise:
“When I say ‘stop’ near traffic, your feet must freeze. Let’s practise in a safe place.”
Urgency and respect can coexist.
When the Child Says “No”
“No” may mean:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: “I want to see whether the rule changes.”

blocks.text: Do not automatically begin a power struggle. Respond according to the situation.
If the task is optional
“Okay. You may choose not to join.”
If the task is necessary

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: “You don’t want to. It still needs to happen. Would you like to begin, or should I help?”
If fear or difficulty may be involved
“Something is making this hard. Show me which part.”
A child’s refusal is information. It is not always the final decision.
When the Child Keeps Asking
Children may ask repeatedly because:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: they hope the answer will change;
they are having trouble tolerating disappointment.

blocks.text: Use a closing statement:
“You asked, and I answered. The answer is no.”
If the request continues:
“I won’t discuss it again right now.”
Then reduce attention to the repeated demand while remaining available for appropriate communication. Do not generate a new explanation each time.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Humour and Playfulness
Playful communication can help with ordinary routines.
“Can your socks reach your feet before I count to ten?”
“Shall we march to the bathroom like elephants?”
“The toy basket is hungry for blocks.”

Playfulness can create cooperation. But it is a tool, not an obligation. Parents do not need to turn every instruction into entertainment. Do not use humour to mock the child, dismiss fear or avoid a necessary boundary. A calm, direct instruction is enough.
Visual Communication

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Some children understand routines better through:
Visual support may help children who are:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Visual support does not prevent language development. It can reduce the burden on spoken language while understanding grows.
Communication in Multilingual Families
Children can grow up learning more than one language. Families do not need to abandon a home language in order to communicate effectively. Helpful practices include:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: speaking naturally in the language the caregiver uses comfortably;
keeping important rules clear across caregivers;
pairing words with gestures and routines;
allowing the child time to respond;
sharing useful words with teachers or childcare providers;
seeking qualified assessment if there are broader communication concerns.

blocks.text: A child’s skills should be considered across all languages they use—not judged from one language alone.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: When Communication Differences May Be Involved
A child may appear not to listen because of:

items: a task that exceeds current ability.

blocks.text: Seek qualified guidance if the child:

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: often does not respond to sounds or their name;
becomes highly distressed by ordinary communication;
is difficult to understand well beyond expected development;
struggles across home and educational settings.

blocks.text: Do not respond to a possible communication difficulty by simply becoming louder.
Coordinate Adult Communication

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Children become confused when one adult says:
“Put it away now.”
while another says:
“Leave it—I’ll do it.”
Caregivers should agree on:

items: how adults will avoid undermining one another.

blocks.text: Adults do not need identical personalities. They need enough consistency that the child can predict essential expectations. Discuss disagreements away from the child whenever possible.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
1. Calling from Another Room
Move closer for important directions.
2. Giving Too Many Steps
Reduce the instruction or use a checklist.
3. Repeating Too Quickly
Allow processing time.
4. Repeating Without Follow-Through
Act calmly after the direction.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: 5. Hiding a Direction Inside a Question
State necessary actions clearly.
6. Telling Only What to Stop
Provide the alternative behaviour.
7. Overexplaining
Separate the reason from the direction.
8. Shouting to Create Seriousness
Make the first calm instruction meaningful.
9. Offering Too Many Choices
Limit options.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: 10. Using Sarcasm
Young children may misunderstand it, and older children may experience it as contempt.
11. Asking “Why?” During Overwhelm
Return to reflection after calm.
12. Assuming Non-Response Is Defiance
Consider attention, hearing, language, development and the environment.
RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: Communication relies on threats, insults or humiliation.
Adults force eye contact through fear or physical control.
A child is punished for using a home language.
Speech or communication differences are mocked.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: A child is discouraged from reporting unsafe touch or adult misconduct.
“Listening” means unquestioning obedience to every adult.
The child is taught that politeness is more important than personal safety.
Adults deliberately confuse the child or deny things that occurred.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: Communication support is withheld because it is seen as giving an unfair advantage.
A child loses language or communication skills.
Failure to follow directions repeatedly creates serious safety risks.
A caregiver’s shouting or threats are escalating.

blocks.text: Children need to learn that they should follow legitimate safety directions—and that they may also question, refuse or report unsafe adult behaviour.
When to Seek Additional Support

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Speak with a qualified paediatric, hearing, speech-language, developmental or educational professional if:

items: the child frequently does not respond to voice or environmental sounds;
speech or communication skills are lost;
the child cannot express essential needs;

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

items: communication difficulties cause frequent distress or aggression;
difficulties occur across languages and settings;
the child relies heavily on others to interpret them;
attention or sensory concerns also affect daily life;
ordinary communication strategies are not helping.

blocks.text: Assessment should respect the child’s language background, culture, development and preferred communication methods.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: The Clear-Direction Checklist
Before giving an important direction, ask:

items: Do I have the child’s attention?
Is it appropriate for the child’s ability?
Have I said what to do?
Have I allowed time to respond?
Am I prepared to follow through?

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Turn These into Clear Directions
Instead of:
“Be careful.”
Instead of:
“Behave yourself.”
Instead of:
“Get ready.”
Instead of:
“Stop making a mess.”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Instead of:
“How many times must I ask?”
Instead of:
“Why aren’t you listening?”
The One-Instruction Practice
Choose a routine that often requires repeated reminders.

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You
Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Useful Communication Scripts
Gaining attention
“Pause and listen for one step.”
Clear direction
Limited choice
First–then

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: After refusal
“You do not want to. It still needs to happen. You may begin, or I will help.”
Repeated request
“You asked, and I answered. I won’t discuss it again right now.”
Safety
“Stop. Stand beside me.”
Parent becoming overwhelmed
“I am becoming frustrated. I’m going to slow down and say this clearly.”

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Repairing after shouting
“I shouted. That was not respectful. The direction remains, and I will say it again calmly.”
Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Children listen more successfully when adults make the message easier to receive, understand and act upon. Move close. Gain attention. Use one specific direction. Tell the child what to do. Offer limited choices where appropriate. Allow time. Follow through. Communication cannot guarantee cooperation. But it can remove unnecessary confusion and reduce the need for shouting, repetition and power struggles. A clear voice is not always a loud voice.
It is a voice whose words and actions match.
Try This Today

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Choose one direction you usually repeat. Say it once using this structure:
“Name, pause and listen. Please ____________________.”
Wait. If the child does not begin:
“You may do it yourself, or I will help you.”
Then follow through calmly. Notice whether changing your delivery changes the interaction—even slightly.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Talking So Your Child Can Hear You
Talking So Your Child Can Hear You
Talking So Your Child Can Hear You

blocks.text: Editorial note for review: Guidance concerning communication development, multilingual children, hearing, speech-language needs, physical assistance and safeguarding should be reviewed by qualified paediatric, audiology, speech-language, developmental, educational and child-protection professionals for the intended Indian edition. Local referral pathways should be added before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood
pages.epigraph: Listening does not mean agreeing with everything a child says. It means creating enough safety and attention to understand what the child is trying to communicate.

blocks.text: “Nothing Happened”
Every afternoon, six-year-old Anvi’s mother asked the same question:
“How was school?”
“Fine.”
“What did you do?”
“Nothing.”
“Who did you play with?”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: “I don’t remember.” Her mother began to wonder whether Anvi simply disliked talking. One evening, they sat together sorting clean clothes. Without being asked, Anvi said:
“Rhea told everyone not to sit with me today.”
Her mother stopped folding. She wanted to ask ten questions immediately. Why? What did you do? Did you tell the teacher? Has this happened before? Instead, she said:
“You were left out today.”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Anvi nodded. After a pause, she began telling the story. The information had been inside her all afternoon. What she needed was not a better interrogation. She needed a moment in which speaking felt easier than protecting herself.
Listening Is More Than Remaining Silent
A parent may hear every word while also:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: comparing the child with someone else;
waiting for the child to finish.

blocks.text: Active listening requires the adult to understand before directing the conversation elsewhere. It may involve:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: noticing the child’s body and tone;
asking a small number of useful questions;
checking whether the interpretation is correct;
resisting the urge to solve everything immediately.

blocks.text: Listening communicates:
“Your experience is worth understanding.”
Children Do Not Always Speak on Demand
Children may talk:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: when helping with a household task;

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: A child who says nothing after school may talk freely while building blocks. A child who avoids eye contact may communicate well while sitting side by side. A child who cannot explain directly may show the experience through play. Parents cannot remain fully available every minute. But they can create repeated openings.
“I’m cooking now, but I want to hear this. Can we talk while I chop, or shall I come to you in ten minutes?”
If you promise to return, return. That follow-through teaches the child that their words matter.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Attention Does Not Have to Be Perfect
Listening does not require an uninterrupted hour. Even a few focused minutes can help. Where possible:

items: turn your body towards the child;

blocks.text: You do not have to force eye contact. Say:
“I’m listening.”
Then show it through your body.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Begin with an Observation
Children may respond more easily to an observation than a direct question. Instead of:
“What’s wrong?”
try:
“You became very quiet when we left school.”
Instead of:
“Why are you angry?”
try:
“You closed the door hard and moved away.”
Instead of:
“Did somebody upset you?”
try:
“Something seems different today.”
An observation leaves room for the child to explain—or not.
Reflect the Child’s Words
Reflection means repeating or summarising what the child has said. Child:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: “I’m never going back to school.”
Parent:
“School feels impossible right now.”
Child:
“Nobody lets me play.”
Parent:
“You felt left out.”
Child:
“You always help the baby first.”
Parent:
“You notice how much time the baby needs, and you want more of me.”
Reflection is not agreement with every fact. It shows the child that the message was received.
Reflect Feelings Tentatively
You may say:
“You sound disappointed.”
“I wonder if that embarrassed you.”
“You look worried.”
“Perhaps you felt angry and left out.”
If you guess incorrectly, the child may correct you.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: “I wasn’t worried. I was annoyed.”
Reply:
“Annoyed. Thank you—that helps me understand.”
Listening is not a performance in which the parent must always interpret correctly. Correction is part of the conversation.
Reflect Before Reassuring
Suppose the child says:
“Nobody likes me.”
The parent may rush to say:
“That’s not true. Everyone likes you.”
The reassurance is loving, but it may close the conversation. Try:
“Something happened that made you feel unwanted.”
The child might then explain:
“They chose teams, and I was last.”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Now the parent can respond to the actual experience. Reassurance is more useful after understanding.
Listen Before Advising
Parents often move immediately to solutions.
“You should have told the teacher.”
“Just ignore him.”
“Play with somebody else.”
“You need to be more confident.”
Advice may eventually help. But first ask:
“Do you want me to listen, help you think, or take action?”
A young child may need simpler choices:
“Would you like me to listen, or help solve it?”
Sometimes children want empathy. Sometimes they need protection. Sometimes they need both.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Ask Fewer, Better Questions
Too many questions can feel like an examination. Use open, neutral prompts:

items: “What would you like to happen now?”
“Is there more you want me to know?”

blocks.text: Avoid questions that suggest the answer:
“Your teacher shouted at you, didn’t she?”
“He touched you there, right?”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: “You hit because she teased you first?”

Leading questions can confuse children and distort important information. For ordinary conversations, they may make the child agree just to satisfy the adult. For possible abuse or serious safeguarding concerns, they can interfere with accurate professional investigation.
Silence Is Part of Listening
Adults often fill pauses because silence feels uncomfortable. A child may need time to:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: decide whether it is safe to continue;

blocks.text: Pause. Remain available. You can say:
“Take your time.”
“You don’t have to tell it perfectly.”
“I’m still listening.”
Do not mistake slower speech for dishonesty.
Listen to Play
Young children often communicate through:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: A child may make a toy say:
“The teacher keeps shouting.”
This does not automatically prove that a real event occurred. It is an opening for gentle curiosity.
“That toy looks frightened. What is happening in the game?”
Avoid turning play into an investigation. Notice patterns and seek qualified advice if the content raises serious concern.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Listen to Behaviour
A child who cannot explain may communicate through:

items: fear of a person or place;
sudden changes in mood or play.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Behaviour alone does not reveal one certain cause. These changes may relate to:
Notice the change, listen without assumption and seek appropriate help.
Everyday Questions That Invite More Than “Fine”
Instead of:
“How was school?”
try:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: “Was there a moment you needed help?”
“What do you want me to know before tomorrow?”

blocks.text: Do not ask all of them at once. One genuine question is enough. Some days the answer will still be:
“I don’t know.”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: That is acceptable. A listening relationship is built over time, not extracted from every afternoon.
Listening Does Not Remove the Boundary
A child says:
“I hate bedtime.”
The parent can listen:
“You want more time with us, and stopping the day is hard.”
Then hold the limit:
“Bedtime is still beginning now.”
A child says:
“I hit him because he took my car.”
The parent can reflect:
“You were angry that he took it.”
Then guide:
“Hitting is still not safe. Next time, say, ‘I’m using it,’ and call me.”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Understanding the child’s experience does not make every action acceptable. It makes discipline more accurate.
Listen Without Making the Child Responsible for You
A child may hesitate to speak if they expect the parent to:
When a child shares something painful, regulate your first response. Avoid:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: “How could you do this to me?”
“I can’t believe this.”
“Your father will be devastated.”
Try:
“Thank you for telling me.”
“I’m glad you came to me.”
“We will work out what to do.”
Your feelings matter, but the child should not have to manage them in that moment. Seek support from another safe adult or professional away from the child.
When the Child Complains About Another Adult
Do not automatically dismiss the child:
“Your teacher would never do that.”
Also avoid immediately deciding:
“The teacher is terrible.”
Listen for detail.
“Tell me what happened.”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: “What words did the teacher use?”
“Who else was there?”
“Has this happened before?”
Then consider:

items: the child’s safety and emotional response.

blocks.text: Children can misunderstand. Adults can also behave improperly. A respectful investigation holds both possibilities until more is known.
When the Child Tells an Unlikely Story
Young children may mix:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Avoid accusing them immediately of lying. Say:
“I want to understand. Is this something that happened, something you imagined, or a story you are making?”
For serious allegations or possible harm, do not conduct your own extensive interview. Record the child’s exact words as soon as possible and seek appropriate professional guidance.
Responding to a Serious Disclosure
A child may disclose:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: being told to keep a secret;

blocks.text: The adult’s first response matters.
1. Remain as Calm as Possible
Strong shock or rage may make the child stop talking or feel responsible.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: 2. Listen
Allow the child to use their own words. Do not repeatedly question.
3. Reassure
Say:
“I’m glad you told me.”
“This is not your fault.”
“You are not in trouble.”
Do not make promises you cannot guarantee. Avoid:
“Nothing bad will happen now.”
4. Do Not Promise Secrecy
Say:
“I may need to tell people whose job is to help keep you safe. I will only tell people who need to know.”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: 5. Protect

If there is immediate danger, act immediately through appropriate local emergency or child-protection channels. Do not send the child back into a situation you reasonably believe is unsafe merely to avoid conflict.
6. Record
As soon as possible, write:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: any visible injuries or immediate concerns;

blocks.text: Do not add guesses to the record.
7. Report or Seek Guidance

Follow current local safeguarding laws and professional procedures. Parents do not need certainty before raising a genuine safety concern. Investigation belongs to appropriately authorised professionals.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: 8. Do Not Confront the Suspected Person in Front of the Child
A confrontation may increase danger, pressure the child or interfere with protective action. Obtain professional guidance.
Safe and Unsafe Secrets
Children may be taught the difference between:
A temporary surprise
A birthday gift that will soon be revealed.
An unsafe secret
Something involving:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: being told a parent will be angry;
harm to the child or someone else.

blocks.text: Teach:
“No one should ask you to keep a secret about touching, fear or safety. You can always tell me or another trusted adult.”
Do not promise that the child will never face an uncomfortable response. Build a network of several safe adults the child can approach.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: When the Child Does Not Want to Talk
Respect can sound like:
“You do not have to talk now. I’m available later.”

But safety may require further action even without a full conversation. If there are signs of serious danger, injury, abuse, self-harm or major distress, the adult should seek qualified help rather than waiting indefinitely. Privacy is important. Protection is more important when a child may be unsafe.
Special Time for Listening

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: A short period of child-led play can strengthen communication. Choose a manageable time—perhaps ten minutes. During this time:

items: let the child choose from safe activities;
describe what the child is doing;

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: The purpose is not to produce a disclosure or lesson. It is to let the child experience the parent’s attention without having to misbehave or perform.
Listen to Children Who Communicate Differently
Listening is not limited to spoken language. Children may communicate through:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Adults should:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: provide access to devices or supports;
avoid speaking as though the child is not present;

blocks.text: A child who cannot speak fluently can still report discomfort, refusal and harm. Communication support is a safety need.
Listen Across Languages

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: A multilingual child may describe an experience more accurately in one language than another. Allow:

For serious safeguarding or professional conversations, use an appropriately qualified, independent interpreter where required. Do not use a person who may have a conflict of interest or be connected to the alleged harm.
Common Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: 1. Asking Too Many Questions
Conversation becomes interrogation.
2. Correcting Before Understanding
The child’s message gets lost.
3. Giving Advice Too Quickly
Listen first.
4. Dismissing “Small” Problems
Small to an adult may be significant to a child.
5. Agreeing with Every Conclusion

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Validation does not require endorsing every interpretation.
6. Forcing Eye Contact
Attention can be shown in different ways.
7. Demanding Immediate Conversation
Some children need time.
8. Sharing the Child’s Story Widely
Protect privacy.
9. Reacting So Strongly That the Child Comforts the Parent
Seek adult support elsewhere.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: 10. Promising Secrecy
Safety concerns may require action.
11. Conducting an Amateur Investigation
Use minimal, neutral questions and involve qualified professionals.
12. Waiting for Perfect Proof
A reasonable safety concern can justify seeking guidance.
RED FLAG: Be Careful If…

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: An adult repeatedly tells a child they are lying without listening.
A child is punished for reporting harm.
Someone demands secrecy about touching, photographs, gifts, threats or fear.
An adult insists on being present whenever the child speaks to another trusted person.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: The child suddenly fears a particular person or place.
A caregiver destroys, changes or conceals a child’s disclosure.
Adults ask leading questions until the child agrees.
A child’s communication device is removed as punishment.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: An interpreter connected to the suspected harm controls the conversation.
A child talks about death, self-harm, disappearing or being unwanted.
The parent is pressured to handle a serious safeguarding concern privately.

blocks.text: A child’s safety should never be traded for family reputation, social comfort or institutional convenience.
When to Seek Additional Support
Seek qualified help when:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: the child reports harm, abuse, unsafe touch, threats or violence;
the child becomes fearful, withdrawn or unusually aggressive;
sleep, eating, toileting or school attendance changes significantly;
the child repeatedly talks about death or self-harm;
communication difficulties prevent the child from expressing essential needs;

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: the child appears afraid to speak in front of someone;
parents are unsure how to interpret a concerning disclosure;
family conversations repeatedly become hostile or frightening.

blocks.text: Local child-protection, emergency, medical, mental-health and legal pathways should be followed according to the situation.
The Listening Ladder
1. Notice
“You became quiet when ____________________.”

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: 2. Invite
“Would you like to tell me about it now or later?”
3. Reflect
4. Check
“Have I understood correctly?”
5. Ask what is needed
“Do you want me to listen, help think, or take action?”
6. Respond

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: What action, boundary, support or protection is needed? ____________________________________________________
The Five-Minute Listening Practice
Choose one short period today.

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: Let the child choose the topic or activity.
Reflect one thing the child says.
Avoid giving advice unless requested or necessary.

blocks.text: Afterward, note:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: If a Child Tells You Something Serious
Keep this sequence available:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: Ask only minimal, neutral questions needed for immediate safety.
Follow current local reporting and professional procedures.

blocks.text: Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Listening does not require perfect words. It requires enough steadiness to let the child’s words remain at the centre of the conversation. Reflect before reassuring. Understand before advising. Allow silence. Accept correction. Protect privacy. Act when safety requires it. The everyday conversations about lost pencils, unfair games and difficult school days build the path a child may later use to tell you something much more important.
Try This Today
When your child tells you something, delay your first piece of advice. Use three responses instead:

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: “Do you want listening, help thinking, or action?”

blocks.text: Notice what the child says when the conversation is not immediately taken over by the adult.
Authoritative References for This Chapter

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood
Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

items: [Government of the United Kingdom — Reporting Child Abuse](https://www.gov.uk/report-child-abuse)

pages.chapterTitle: Listening So Your Child Feels Understood

blocks.text: Editorial note for review: The safeguarding section must be reviewed and localised by qualified Indian child-protection, legal, medical and mental-health professionals. Reporting duties, emergency contacts, documentation standards, interviewing guidance and referral pathways vary by jurisdiction and professional role. Current Indian procedures must replace or supplement general guidance before publication.

pages.epigraph: “Children often feel most loved not during the grand occasions, but in the small moments when we pause and truly join them.”

blocks.text: The Moments Children Remember

A mother once told me that she worried she was not doing enough with her children. Other families seemed to be visiting new places every weekend. Their photographs showed craft activities, elaborate birthday celebrations and carefully planned outings. Her own evenings looked much simpler. She returned from work, prepared dinner, checked school bags and tried to get everyone into bed at a reasonable hour. One night, while folding laundry, she asked her six-year-old what his favourite part of the week had been.
She expected him to mention the birthday party they had attended. Instead, he said:

“When we made rotis together and mine looked like a cloud.” She had almost forgotten that moment. He had stood beside her for ten minutes, covered in flour, while she tried to finish dinner. There had been no special plan, no photograph and certainly no perfect round roti. But he remembered it because, for those few minutes, they had laughed together. Children do not measure connection by the cost of an experience or the number of hours we spend entertaining them.
They feel it in simpler ways:
“You noticed me.”
“You enjoyed being with me.”
“I can come to you when I need you.”
“Even when we have a difficult moment, we find our way back to each other.” That is connection. And most of it is built in the middle of ordinary family life.
What Connection Really Means

Connection is the sense of safety and belonging that grows when a child is repeatedly treated as someone worthy of attention, affection and respect. It does not require a parent to be available every minute. It does not mean agreeing with everything a child wants. It does not mean preventing every disappointment, removing every boundary or becoming the child’s constant entertainer. A connected parent can still say:
“I love being with you, and the television is still going off now.”
“I understand that you’re angry. I won’t let you hit me.”
“I can’t play at this moment. I’ll sit with you after I finish cooking.”
Connection and boundaries are not opposites. In a healthy relationship, they strengthen each other. Warmth helps a child trust the parent, while dependable boundaries help the child feel safe.
Connection Is Built Through Responsiveness, Not Perfection

Young children regularly send small invitations for connection. A toddler points at a bird. A preschooler brings you a drawing. A child calls from the other room, “Look what I made!” These invitations may appear unimportant to an adult. But when we sometimes pause, look and respond, a simple back-and-forth exchange begins. The child points. The parent looks. The child smiles. The parent says, “You spotted a tiny bird on the wall.”

The child adds another sound or idea. This responsive exchange is sometimes called serve and return . The child “serves” an expression, gesture, sound or question, and the adult responds in a meaningful way. These repeated interactions support communication, learning and emotional development. [Harvard Center on the Developing Child](https://developingchild.harvard.edu/science/key-concepts/serve-and-return/) and the [World Health Organization’s nurturing-care guidance](https://www.who.int/teams/maternal-newborn-child-adolescent-health-and-ageing/child-health/nurturing-care) both emphasise the importance of responsive caregiving in early childhood.

This does not mean you must respond to every invitation immediately. Sometimes you are driving. Sometimes another child needs you. Sometimes you are exhausted. Sometimes you simply miss the signal. A secure relationship is not created by perfect responses. It is created by a reliable pattern:
“Most of the time, when I genuinely need you, I can find my way back to you.”
The Power of Small, Predictable Moments
Many parents wait for enough free time to “properly” connect with their child. That free time rarely arrives. The good news is that connection can be woven into moments that already exist.

rows: Make eye contact, smile and greet the child warmly
Let the child choose between two suitable options
Invite the child to wash vegetables, stir or carry napkins
Use a predictable goodbye phrase or gesture
Pause briefly, greet the child and acknowledge that you are together again
Notice something outside or sing a familiar song
Share one small part of your day without turning dinner into an interview
Work side by side, add music or make the task playful
Follow the child’s pretend play for a few minutes
Read, talk, cuddle if welcomed or recall one moment from the day
Return, repair and reassure the child that the relationship is safe

blocks.text: Not every routine needs to become a lesson or a performance. Sometimes connection is simply allowing the child to be beside you while you live your ordinary life.
The Difference Between Attention and Connection

A child can receive a great deal of attention without feeling deeply connected. We can remind, instruct, question and correct them throughout the day: “Put your shoes on.” “Did you finish your lunch?” “Why is your room untidy?” “Have you done your homework?” “Don’t touch that.” All of these may be necessary. But if most of our communication is managerial, the child may begin to experience the parent mainly as the person who directs and corrects.
Connection gives the relationship a little breathing space. Instead of asking another question, we might observe:
“You used so many colours in that picture.”
Instead of immediately teaching, we might join:
“You’re making the dinosaur climb the cushion.”
Instead of correcting an imaginative story, we might say:
“In your story, the dog can drive the bus!”
For a few minutes, we enter the child’s world without trying to improve it. That can be surprisingly powerful.
Child-Led Play

Play is one of the clearest ways young children communicate. Through play, children experiment with ideas, replay experiences, practise relationships and express feelings they may not yet have words for. Child-led play means allowing the child to choose a safe activity and decide how the play develops while the parent follows with interest. You do not need expensive toys. A child may choose:

items: or an imaginary game with no materials at all.

blocks.text: Even five or ten minutes of regular child-led play can create a dependable point of connection. The [CDC’s guidance on special playtime](https://www.cdc.gov/parenting-toddlers/communication/special-playtime.html) encourages parents to use skills such as praise, imitation, description and active listening while allowing the child to lead.
Five Helpful Child-Led Play Skills
1. Follow

Allow the child to choose what happens, provided the play remains safe. If the child says the blue block is a boat, it is a boat. You do not need to turn the activity into a lesson about colours, shapes or engineering.
2. Describe
Describe what you notice without taking over.
“You’ve put all the animals inside the bus.”
“You’re pressing the clay very carefully.”
“That tower is getting taller.”
Description tells the child:
“I am paying attention to what matters to you.”
3. Imitate

Join the child’s play in a similar way. If the child rolls a car, you can roll another car. If the child draws circles, you might draw nearby—without copying so closely that it feels intrusive. Imitation communicates interest and gives the child the unusual experience of leading an adult.
4. Reflect

Repeat or gently expand the child’s words. Child: “The baby is sad.” Parent: “The baby is feeling sad and wants someone close.” Child: “This is a rocket.” Parent: “A very fast rocket going into space.” Reflection supports language while showing that you heard the child.
5. Offer Specific Appreciation
Notice effort, cooperation or imagination.
“You kept trying until those pieces fitted.”
“You made room for my animal too.”
“You had a creative idea for that box.”
Specific appreciation is more informative than repeating “Good job” throughout the activity.
What to Reduce During Child-Led Play
Try to reduce:

items: or changing the game because the child’s version seems boring.

blocks.text: Instead of:
“What colour is this? How many blocks are there? What shape is that?”
try:
“You chose the red block for the roof.”
Questions are not harmful. But too many can make play feel like an examination.
Safety Still Comes First
Following the child’s lead does not mean abandoning boundaries. You can intervene calmly when play becomes unsafe or destructive.
“The clay stays on the table.”
“I won’t let you throw the wooden block.”
“You may make the dolls fight, but you may not hit your brother.”
If the unsafe behaviour continues, end or change the activity. Connection does not require permitting harm.
Connection Before Correction

You may hear the phrase “connect before you correct.” It can be helpful, but it is sometimes misunderstood. Connection before correction does not mean giving a long emotional speech before every instruction. It means briefly recognising the child’s experience so that the boundary is easier to receive. Instead of:
“Stop complaining and put your shoes on.”
try:
“You wish you could keep playing. It’s hard to stop. Shoes on now—we need to leave.”
Instead of:
“Don’t snatch!”
try:
“You really wanted that toy. I won’t let you take it from her. Ask for a turn.”
The limit remains clear. However, in an urgent situation, safety comes before connection:
“Stop. Stay on the pavement.”
You can acknowledge the child’s feelings after everyone is safe. Connection is not a technique for making children obey. It is the relationship within which guidance happens.
Reunions Matter

When children return from school, childcare or time with another caregiver, parents may be eager for information. “How was school?” “What did you learn?” “Did you finish your lunch?” “Who did you play with?” “Why are your clothes dirty?” The questions come from care. But a child who has spent hours managing instructions, noise and social demands may need time before talking. Some children run into a parent’s arms. Some appear indifferent.

Some become silly, demanding or tearful as soon as they reach home. This does not always mean the day went badly. A child may have held themselves together in a structured setting and released their feelings in the place where they feel safest. A gentler reunion might sound like:
“I’m happy to see you.”
“Your snack is ready when you want it.”
“You don’t have to tell me everything yet.”
You might create a small reunion ritual:

items: or one familiar song on the way home.

blocks.text: Predictability helps children move from separation back into connection.
Affection Must Respect the Child’s Body

Affection can be an important part of connection, but it should not be forced. Some children love hugs and kisses. Others prefer sitting nearby, holding hands, a high-five, a playful wink or hearing affectionate words. A child should be allowed to decline physical affection—even from people they love. Instead of:
“Go and hug Uncle. Don’t be rude.”
try:
“Would you like to say hello with a hug, a wave or a high-five?”

This does not teach disrespect. It teaches bodily autonomy and helps children understand that affection should be freely given. It is especially important that adults do not ask children to keep affectionate contact, gifts or conversations secret from their parents. Healthy connection never requires secrecy, fear or the surrender of personal boundaries.
One-to-One Time in a Busy Family
