In families with more than one child, parents often worry that each child is not receiving equal attention. Perfect equality is rarely possible. A baby may need more physical care. An older child may need homework help. A sick child may temporarily receive most of the family’s attention. Children do not necessarily need identical minutes. They need reliable experiences of individual belonging. One-to-one connection could be:

items: accompanying one child to buy vegetables;
reading together after the younger sibling sleeps;
inviting one child to help prepare tea;
spending ten minutes with their chosen game;
or sitting beside them while they show you something important.

blocks.text: You might say:
“Your brother needs my help now. After his bath, I want ten minutes with just you.”
Make promises carefully. If you cannot keep the exact plan, return and repair:
“I said we would read after dinner, but the evening became difficult. I’m sorry. Let’s choose another time I can genuinely protect.”
Reliable attention matters more than grand promises.
Connection When Time Is Limited

Working parents and parents carrying heavy household responsibilities may feel guilty when advice about connection sounds like another impossible task. Connection does not require hours of uninterrupted play. It benefits from small moments in which the child receives your genuine attention. Consider creating two or three protected pockets:

items: the first two minutes after reunion;
or a few minutes of child-led play on selected days.

blocks.text: A brief, dependable ritual is often more sustainable than an ambitious plan that disappears after one week. Children also benefit from loving relationships with other dependable caregivers. A grandparent, aunt, uncle, teacher or childcare provider can become part of the child’s circle of security. A child’s attachment to another safe adult does not reduce the parent’s importance. It expands the child’s village.
Devices and Divided Attention

Phones are part of modern family life. Parents use them for work, banking, school messages, medical appointments, family communication and much more. The goal does not need to be a completely phone-free home. A more realistic goal is to create some moments in which the child can reliably reach your face and attention. You might choose:

items: no phone during the first few minutes after reunion;
the phone outside the bedroom during the bedtime story;
or telling the child what you are doing when the phone is necessary.

blocks.text: “I’m sending one work message. When I finish, I’ll look at your drawing.”
Then follow through. If you realise you have been distracted, repair simply:
“You were talking and I kept looking at my phone. I’m sorry. Please tell me again.”
Children do not need parents who never become distracted. They benefit from parents who notice disconnection and return.
When Your Child Does Not Want to Connect

Sometimes a parent offers closeness and the child turns away. The child may be angry. They may need quiet. They may be absorbed in an activity. They may have a temperament that requires more personal space. Do not force the moment. You can say:
“You don’t want a hug. I’ll stay nearby.”
“You’re not ready to talk. You can come to me when you are.”
“You want to play alone. I’ll check on you later.”

Remaining available without chasing teaches the child that connection is an invitation, not a demand. A child who repeatedly avoids all interaction, appears persistently withdrawn or shows a sudden major change in behaviour may need additional support. But an occasional wish for space is healthy.
Connection and Independence
Being connected does not mean doing everything together. Children also need opportunities to:

items: spend time with other safe adults;
and discover interests that do not involve the parent.

blocks.text: You can communicate both closeness and confidence:
“I’m nearby, and I believe you can work on this.”
“I can help with the first part. You try the next part.”
“I’m cooking now. You may play here beside me or choose something in your room.”
Healthy connection gives a child a secure place to return to—not a reason to fear exploring.
When Parent and Child Are Very Different

Connection sometimes feels harder when the parent and child have different temperaments. A quiet parent may feel overwhelmed by a highly energetic child. A sociable parent may worry about a child who needs time before joining others. A fast-moving parent may become impatient with a cautious child. A parent who loves physical affection may feel rejected by a child who prefers more space. Connection does not require sameness. It requires curiosity.
Instead of:
“Why are you always so slow?”
try:
“You like time to understand what is happening before you join.”
Instead of:
“Why don’t you ever tell me anything?”
try:
“You often talk when we’re doing something side by side. I’ll make space for that.”
Understanding the child you have is more connecting than insisting they relate exactly as you do.
Common Mistakes
1. Turning Special Time Into Another Lesson

If every game becomes an opportunity to teach letters, numbers, manners or performance, the child rarely experiences the pleasure of simply being enjoyed. Learning will happen. It does not have to happen in every minute.
2. Asking Too Many Questions
Questions can make some children feel pressured. Use observations, reflections and pauses too.
3. Taking Over the Play
Adults are usually faster and more skilled. But when we build the tower, fix the drawing and decide the story, the child loses ownership.
4. Using Gifts as a Substitute for Presence
Gifts can express love, but they cannot replace attention, reliability or emotional safety.
5. Forcing Affection
A forced hug may please an adult while teaching the child to ignore discomfort. Offer respectful alternatives.
6. Connecting Only After Difficult Behaviour

If the child receives undivided attention only after screaming or hitting, difficult behaviour may become one of the most reliable ways to reach the parent. Offer small positive moments at ordinary times too.
7. Comparing Siblings
“Your sister always tells me about her day.”
“Your brother is much more affectionate.”
Comparison creates competition where each child needs belonging.
8. Expecting Yourself to Be Constantly Available
Parental exhaustion does not create deeper connection. Rest, boundaries and help from other safe adults support the relationship.
Red Flags
Connection should never be used to cross a child’s boundaries. Take concerns seriously if:

items: an adult demands hugs, kisses or physical closeness after the child says no;
an adult asks the child to keep gifts, conversations or touch secret;
someone uses a “special relationship” to isolate the child from parents or other trusted adults;
a child appears unusually fearful of a particular person;
there is a sudden change in sleep, toileting, mood, behaviour or willingness to be near someone;
a caregiver humiliates, threatens or frightens the child in the name of closeness or discipline;
or parental stress has reached a point where the adult fears they may harm the child or themselves.

blocks.text: Seek immediate help when a child’s safety may be at risk.
When Additional Support May Help
Consider speaking with a paediatrician, mental-health professional or qualified child-development specialist when:

items: the relationship feels persistently dominated by fear, rejection or conflict;
the child is consistently withdrawn and shows little interest in interaction;
there has been a frightening event, loss or major family disruption;
communication or developmental differences make connection especially difficult;
the parent is experiencing depression, anxiety, trauma symptoms or severe burnout;
the child’s behaviour changes suddenly and significantly;
or attempts at repair repeatedly seem impossible.

blocks.text: Seeking support is not evidence that the relationship has failed. It is one way of protecting it.
Your Connection Map
Choose five moments that already happen on most days.

rows: One small way I could connect

blocks.text: Do not redesign the whole day. Choose one moment first.
Your Ten-Minute Child-Led Play Plan
Where I will place my phone: ____________________________________ During these ten minutes, I will try to:

items: Let my child choose a safe activity.
Reflect my child’s words or feelings.

blocks.text: At the end, I can say:
“I enjoyed playing with you. We’ll do this again.”
If the child protests when the time ends:
“You wish we could keep going. I enjoyed it too. Playtime with me is finished for now.”
Connection does not require an unlimited ending.
Remember This
Connection is not created by spectacular experiences or constant attention.

It grows through ordinary moments in which your child feels noticed, respected and welcomed back. You can be connected and still have boundaries. You can be loving and still need rest. You can encourage independence and still remain a secure place to return to.
Try This Today

Set aside five or ten minutes. Let your child choose one safe activity. Put your phone away. For those few minutes, do not teach, test or correct unless safety requires it. Follow. Describe. Imitate. Listen. When the time ends, notice what changed—not only in your child, but in you.
From Mom’s Village
Some days will contain long stories, laughter and easy affection. Other days, connection may look like placing a glass of water beside an upset child and saying:
“I’m here when you’re ready.”

Both count. The relationship is not built in one perfect afternoon. It is built through thousands of small returns: After distraction. After separation. After impatience. After disagreement. We notice the distance, and we find our way back. That is how ordinary moments become a secure childhood.
Authoritative References

items: [Harvard Center on the Developing Child — Serve and Return](https://developingchild.harvard.edu/science/key-concepts/serve-and-return/)

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition

Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed with examples representing varied Indian family structures, including nuclear families, joint families, single-parent households and families in which parents work away from home. The final design could include:

items: a small box explaining affectionate boundaries to grandparents and relatives respectfully;
and a reminder that dependable grandparents and extended-family caregivers can be valuable members of a child’s secure relational network.

blocks.text: The chapter should retain its central message: children benefit from warm, responsive relationships, but parents do not need unlimited time, money or energy to build them.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure
pages.epigraph: “A routine does not have to make every day perfect. It simply helps a child know what comes next.”

blocks.text: The Morning That Went Differently

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: One morning, a mother woke twenty minutes late. Her older child could not find a shoe. Her younger child suddenly decided that the blue cup—the same cup he had happily used all week—was completely unacceptable. Breakfast remained unfinished. The school bag was missing a notebook. Everyone seemed to need something at the same time. By the time they reached the front door, the mother was already exhausted. “Why does every morning have to become such a battle?” she wondered.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: That evening, she tried something small. Together, she and her children placed their shoes near the door. They packed the school bag and filled the water bottle. She drew four simple pictures on a sheet of paper:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: The following morning was not magical. One child still moved slowly. Someone spilled milk. There was still a disagreement about socks. But the mother noticed an important difference. She no longer had to repeat every instruction from the beginning. She could point to the pictures and say:
“You’re dressed. What comes next?”

Little by little, the routine began carrying some of the responsibility that had previously rested entirely on her voice. That is one of the quiet benefits of family routines. They do not remove childhood from the day. They give it a dependable shape.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Why Routines Matter
Young children have limited control over their lives. Adults decide:

items: and when it is time to sleep.

blocks.text: A predictable rhythm helps children understand these decisions and prepare for transitions. When a child knows what usually comes next, less energy is spent wondering:
“What is happening?”
“When will this end?”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: “Will I get another turn?”
“Is my parent coming back?” Routines can support:

items: and a more manageable mental load for parents.

blocks.text: But routine should not be confused with rigidity. A family routine is a familiar pathway—not a rule that every day must unfold at exactly the same minute.
Routine, Schedule and Ritual

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: These words are related, but they are not identical.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

rows: Toilet, brush teeth, pyjamas, story, sleep
A plan connected to particular times
A repeated action carrying emotional meaning
A special goodbye phrase before school
A clear expectation or safety boundary
We hold an adult’s hand while crossing the road

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: A routine tells the child what happens. A ritual helps the child feel connected while it happens. A rule explains what must or must not happen. Families often use all three together.
Predictability Is More Important Than Perfection

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Some families follow precise schedules. Others cannot. A parent may work shifts. School transport may be unreliable. A baby may wake frequently. A grandparent may share caregiving responsibilities. Religious events, family visits and festivals may change the usual rhythm. Children can still benefit from routines when the clock changes. For example, bedtime may not always begin at exactly 8:00 p.m. But the sequence can remain familiar:
Wash → pyjamas → brush teeth → story → cuddle or goodnight phrase → lights out.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: The familiar order helps the child recognise that the day is ending. This is especially useful for children who find transitions difficult.
Begin With the Most Difficult Parts of the Day
You do not need to organise every hour. Begin by asking:
“Which part of our day causes the most repeated stress?”
It may be:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Choose one. A simple routine practised consistently is more useful than an elaborate household plan no one can maintain.
Building a Child-Friendly Routine
Step 1: Choose the Essential Actions

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Keep the routine short. A morning routine might include:
Avoid placing every possible detail on the list. Too many steps can make the routine harder to follow.
Step 2: Put the Actions in a Familiar Order
Whenever possible, use the same basic sequence. Repeated order helps the child gradually remember the next step without continuous adult direction.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Step 3: Make It Visible
Young children may not yet read fluently. Use:

items: or a chart with one short phrase beside each image.

blocks.text: Place it where the routine happens. A bathroom routine belongs near the bathroom—not hidden in a parent’s notebook.
Step 4: Practise When Everyone Is Calm
Do not introduce a complicated new plan in the middle of a rushed morning. Walk through it beforehand.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: “Tomorrow, after you get dressed, you can look at the chart and see what comes next.”
Let the child practise pointing to or moving through the steps.
Step 5: Notice Progress
You do not need to reward every step. Offer specific acknowledgement:
“You checked the chart and packed your bottle without another reminder.”
“You started brushing after putting on your pyjamas. You remembered the order.”
This helps the child recognise growing independence.
Use Fewer Words

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: When parents are stressed, instructions often become longer.
“How many times have I told you? Go upstairs, change your clothes, put your uniform in the basket, wash your hands properly and come back because your food is getting cold.”
A young child may hear only fragments. Shorter directions are easier to process:
“Uniform in the basket.”
Wait.
“Now wash your hands.”
As the routine becomes familiar, you can ask:
“What comes after your uniform goes in the basket?”
The goal is not instant independence. It is gradual participation.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Routines for Different Parts of the Day
The Morning Routine
Mornings are often difficult because adults are watching the clock while children are still developing their sense of time. Helpful preparations may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: laying out clothes the night before;
keeping shoes and bags in a consistent place;
offering only two breakfast or clothing choices;
building in more time than the adult alone would need;
and keeping screens off if they regularly interrupt preparation.

blocks.text: A Simple Morning Script
“Good morning. First we use the toilet and get dressed. Then breakfast.”
“You may wear the green shirt or the yellow one.”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: “We leave after shoes and bag. Check your chart.”
If the child is moving slowly:
“Your body wants a slow morning. We still need to leave. Would you like to hop to the bathroom or hold my hand?”
Playfulness can help, but the expectation remains.
The After-School Routine
Many children need time to decompress after school. A routine might be:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: Shoes and bag in their places.

blocks.text: Avoid beginning with several questions and demands.
“I’m happy to see you. Wash your hands, and your snack is ready.”
Some children talk immediately. Others share more after eating, moving or playing.
The Mealtime Routine

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Mealtimes can support connection and predictability without becoming a daily argument. The adult is generally responsible for:
The child can listen to their body and participate within developmentally appropriate limits. A simple routine might include:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: keeping screens away for part or all of the meal;
and carrying the plate to a designated place afterwards.

blocks.text: Avoid making affection, dessert or parental approval dependent on clearing the plate. Persistent feeding difficulties, restricted eating, poor growth, pain, choking or intense distress should be discussed with a paediatrician or qualified feeding professional.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: The Screen-Ending Routine
Children often struggle when a highly engaging activity ends abruptly. Use a predictable sequence:

items: Explain the screen limit before it begins.
Give a brief warning before the end.

blocks.text: “One episode. When it ends, the television goes off and we have a bath.”
Later:
“The episode is ending. Television off, then bath.”
If the child protests:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: “You wanted another episode. It is hard to stop. Television is finished for today.”
Do not restart the programme merely to end the protest. That teaches the child that prolonged resistance may change the limit.
The Bedtime Routine
Sleep cannot be forced, but the conditions around sleep can be supported. A calming routine may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: The final sequence should be manageable enough to repeat. A bedtime routine involving six stories, three snacks, multiple trips out of bed and lengthy negotiations may unintentionally delay sleep. Offer limited choices:
“Would you like the animal story or the train story?”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: “Would you like one song or a back rub?”
The parent chooses the boundary. The child chooses within it.
Transitions: The Spaces Between Activities
Many difficult behaviours happen not during an activity, but when moving from one activity to another. A child may resist:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Transitions ask the developing brain to stop something engaging, shift attention and accept less control. Young children often need support with this.
A Simple Transition Formula
1. Prepare
“We will leave the park after two more turns on the slide.”
2. Remind
“This is your last turn.”
3. Acknowledge
“You wish we could stay longer.”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: 4. State the Next Step
“It’s time to walk to the car.”
5. Offer Limited Control
“Would you like to hold my hand or walk beside the pushchair?”
Do not offer a choice when there is no choice. Avoid:
“Shall we go home now?”
if the family must leave. Say:
“It is time to go. Would you like to carry the water bottle or the ball?”
First–Then Language
“First–then” language makes expectations easier to understand.
“First shoes, then outside.”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: “First toys in the basket, then story.”
“First medicine, then you may choose the song.”

This should not become a threat or a bargain for every ordinary responsibility. It is simply a clear way of showing sequence. Keep the “then” part realistic and relatively close. A four-year-old may not be motivated by something happening several days later.
Timers and Visual Cues
Timers can help because the transition signal comes from something outside the parent’s repeated voice. You might say:
“When the timer rings, bath time begins.”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: But timers are not magic. A child may still protest. The timer helps make the transition predictable; it does not remove every feeling about it. Other useful cues include:

items: switching off one light before bedtime;
or placing finished activities in a “done” box.

blocks.text: Choose cues that reduce confusion rather than adding stimulation.
When the Routine Changes

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Children need predictability, but they also need help learning that plans sometimes change. Tell them as early and simply as possible:
“Usually Grandmother collects you today. She is unwell, so I will come instead.”
“We normally go to the park after school. Today we have a doctor’s appointment.”
A visual change can help:
“The park picture is coming off today. Here is the doctor picture.”
Allow disappointment:
“You were expecting the park. It’s disappointing when a plan changes.”
Then state what will happen. Do not promise that routines will never change. Teach the child that changes can be explained and managed.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Routines in Shared-Care and Joint Families

Children may be cared for by parents, grandparents, relatives, childcare providers or domestic caregivers. Every adult does not need to behave identically. However, it helps when the adults agree on a few important anchors:

items: and how serious behaviour will be handled.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: A child can understand:
“At Nani’s house, you sleep in her room. At home, you sleep in your bed.”
But frequent contradiction about essential boundaries can create confusion. Adults should discuss disagreements away from the child whenever possible. Avoid placing the child between caregivers:
“Your father allows everything.”
“Don’t tell your mother I gave you the phone.”
Secrecy and competition weaken the security routines are meant to create.
When a Child Refuses the Routine

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: A routine is not a guarantee of cheerful cooperation. Children may refuse because they are:

items: or testing whether the expectation remains firm.

blocks.text: Begin with curiosity, but do not abandon necessary limits.
“You don’t want to put on these socks. Is the seam hurting, or do you want a different colour?”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: “You wanted to keep building. The blocks will stay here. It’s time for dinner.”
If the child cannot complete a necessary step independently, help calmly:
“You’re having trouble getting started. I’ll help you put on the first shoe.”
Support is not the same as surrender.
Flexibility for Neurodivergent and Highly Sensitive Children
Some children need more preparation, visual support or sensory accommodation. A child may struggle with:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Helpful adaptations may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: and using the same key language across caregivers.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: The goal is not to force the child to tolerate every difficulty without support. It is to help the child participate while understanding their needs. Seek professional guidance when routine difficulties substantially interfere with eating, sleep, school attendance, hygiene, safety or everyday family life.
Common Routine Mistakes
1. Changing the Expectation Every Day

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: If screen time ends after one episode on Monday but continues after prolonged crying on Tuesday, the child must keep testing to discover where the boundary is. Aim for reasonable consistency.
2. Creating Too Many Rules at Once
An extensive chart may impress adults and overwhelm children. Begin with three to five important steps.
3. Expecting Immediate Independence
A routine must be taught, practised and supported before the child can manage it alone.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: 4. Giving Instructions From Another Room
Move closer, gain the child’s attention and give one clear direction.
5. Using the Routine to Shame
Avoid:
“Even a baby could do this.”
“Why are you always the last one?”
A routine should support competence, not attack dignity.
6. Turning Every Delay Into Defiance
A child may be distracted, confused, tired or struggling with the task. Look for the obstacle before assuming deliberate disrespect.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: 7. Making the Routine So Elaborate That the Parent Cannot Sustain It
The best routine is not the most attractive chart. It is the one the family can actually repeat.
8. Removing All Joy
Routines can include songs, humour, affection and choice. Predictability does not need to feel cold.
Red Flags
A routine needs reconsideration if:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: it regularly involves threats, humiliation or physical punishment;
a child is denied food, sleep, toileting or affection as punishment;
the child shows intense distress linked to a particular caregiver or part of the routine;
toileting routines involve force, restraint or shame;

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: sleep practices place the child in an unsafe situation;
adults use medication to make a child sleep without appropriate medical guidance;
a child’s distress is dismissed despite signs of pain, sensory discomfort or developmental difficulty;
or family conflict about routines is exposing the child to repeated intimidation or violence.

blocks.text: Routines should create safety—not fear.
When to Seek Additional Support
Speak with a paediatrician or appropriate professional when:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: sleep difficulties are severe, persistent or affecting daytime functioning;
the child snores heavily, appears to stop breathing or has other concerning sleep symptoms;
eating is extremely restricted or associated with choking, pain or poor growth;
transitions routinely produce distress so intense that family life or school attendance is significantly disrupted;

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: toileting involves persistent pain, constipation, regression or intense fear;
the child seems unable to understand or complete familiar routines despite substantial support;
sensory difficulties strongly interfere with daily care;
or parental exhaustion has reached an unsafe level.

blocks.text: Practical support can make routines more manageable for both the child and the family.
Build One Routine
Choose one difficult part of the day.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: What usually makes it difficult: ____________________________________
The three to five essential steps:
A visual or verbal cue I can use: ____________________________________
One choice my child can safely make: ____________________________________

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: What I will say when my child protests: ____________________________________
How I will acknowledge progress: ____________________________________ Practise this routine for at least several days before deciding it has failed.
A Family Routine Check
Ask yourself:

items: Is the routine short enough for my child to understand?
Does my child know what comes next?
Am I giving one instruction at a time when necessary?
Is there a small amount of appropriate choice?

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: Have I allowed enough time for a young child?
Is the routine flexible when genuine needs arise?
Do the main caregivers agree on essential safety boundaries?
Am I supporting independence rather than demanding it too early?
Does the routine protect sleep, food, hygiene and connection?
Can our family realistically maintain it?

blocks.text: Remember This
A routine is not meant to control every moment of childhood.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: It is meant to make important parts of the day understandable, repeatable and safer. The child may still complain. Plans may still change. Milk may still spill. But when the pathway is familiar, both parent and child have fewer things to negotiate from the beginning.
Try This Today
Choose the most difficult transition in your day. Write down only three steps. Tell your child what will happen using simple language:
“First this, then this, and finally this.”

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: If helpful, draw a quick picture for each step. Tomorrow, use the same words again. Do not aim for a perfect routine. Aim for a familiar one.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: A peaceful family is not a family in which everyone moves through the day without protest. It is a family that has dependable ways to begin again. The shoes will sometimes disappear. A child will occasionally refuse the carefully chosen breakfast. Bedtime will not always unfold according to the chart. That does not mean the routine is useless or that you have failed. Return to the next familiar step. Offer help where it is needed.
Hold the important boundary. Leave room for humour. Then try again tomorrow. That steady return is what makes a routine feel safe.

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
The final edition should include routines reflecting varied Indian households, including:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: parents working shifts or long commutes;
grandparents and other relatives as regular caregivers;
and families with more than one school schedule.

blocks.text: Useful visual resources could include:

pages.chapterTitle: Routines That Help Children Feel Secure

items: and a blank routine template families can photocopy.

blocks.text: Any medical guidance concerning sleep, nutrition, toileting or development should be reviewed by appropriately qualified Indian paediatric and child-development professionals before publication.

pages.epigraph: “Sleep cannot be forced, but we can create the rhythm, safety and conditions that help it arrive.”

blocks.text: The Evening That Never Seemed to End

At seven-thirty, a mother announced that it was bedtime. Her five-year-old suddenly became thirsty. Then hungry. Then deeply concerned about a toy he had not touched for several weeks. After one story, he requested another. After the second, he needed the toilet. When she finally turned off the light, he remembered an important question:

“Where do clouds sleep?” By nine o’clock, the mother was no longer calm. “Why won’t you just sleep?” she snapped. Her child began to cry. Now both of them were tired, upset and even further from sleep. If this feels familiar, you are not doing anything unusually wrong. Bedtime combines several difficult things for a young child:

items: accepting that the day is over;
becoming still after hours of movement;
and allowing sleep to happen without being able to control exactly when it arrives.

blocks.text: Parents are usually tired too. Bedtime resistance is therefore not simply a child refusing one instruction. It often occurs when two exhausted nervous systems meet at the end of a demanding day. The solution is rarely one perfect sentence. It is a calmer, clearer pattern practised over time.
Why Sleep Matters
Sleep supports a child’s physical health, learning, memory, attention, emotional regulation and daily functioning. A child who is not sleeping well may not simply look sleepy. They may appear:

items: or less able to cope with frustration.

blocks.text: Sleep needs vary between children and across development. Total sleep includes daytime naps as well as night-time sleep. Published guidance provides useful age-based ranges, but these should be treated as general guides rather than a test every family must pass perfectly. The child’s mood, alertness, health and daytime functioning also matter. The [American Academy of Pediatrics’ parent guidance](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/healthy-living/sleep/Pages/healthy-sleep-habits-how-many-hours-does-your-child-need.aspx) explains age-related sleep needs and the value of consistent daily rhythms. The [World Health Organization](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241550536) also includes sleep within its guidance for healthy movement and rest in children under five.
General Sleep Ranges
The following ranges refer to total sleep across twenty-four hours, including naps:

These ranges are not prescriptions for an individual child. A child at one end of the range is not automatically healthier than a child at the other. If you are concerned about your child’s sleep, growth, breathing, behaviour or daytime alertness, speak with a paediatrician.
Sleep Cannot Be Commanded
Adults can require a child to:
But we cannot order the brain to fall asleep on demand. Repeatedly saying:
“Sleep now!”
may increase tension without producing sleep. A more realistic parental role is to create the conditions for rest:
You are responsible for the structure. The child’s body completes the process.
The Foundations of Healthy Sleep
1. A Reasonably Consistent Rhythm

Children often settle more easily when waking, meals, activity and bedtime follow a broadly familiar rhythm. This does not require military precision. A family wedding, festival, journey or occasional late night will not destroy a healthy sleep pattern. But very large daily changes can make it harder for the child’s body to recognise when it is time to become alert and when it is time to wind down. When possible, keep wake-up and bedtime within a fairly predictable range—including weekends.
2. Daylight and Physical Activity

Children need opportunities to move during the day. Outdoor light and active play help distinguish daytime from night-time and allow children to use their bodies fully. Avoid using intense physical activity as a punishment for not sleeping:
“You have too much energy, so run until you’re tired.”

Instead, make movement a normal part of the day. Some children become more alert after vigorous play immediately before bed. Notice your child’s pattern and shift highly stimulating activity earlier if necessary.
3. A Wind-Down Period
Moving directly from exciting play, loud television or a family gathering into bed can be difficult. A short wind-down period tells the child’s body:

“The active part of the day is ending.” The [NHS guidance for young children](https://www.nhs.uk/baby/health/sleep-and-young-children/) suggests beginning a calming routine before the child’s usual sleep time and keeping it predictable. Wind-down activities may include:
A long routine is not necessarily better. Twenty to thirty manageable minutes may be more sustainable than an elaborate hour-long performance.
4. A Sleep-Supportive Environment

Families live in many different kinds of homes. A child may share a room with siblings or parents. There may be street noise, warm weather or limited private space. A perfect bedroom is not required. Work with what is available:

items: keep the sleep area at a comfortable temperature;
move exciting toys away from immediate reach;
and use a familiar comfort item when safe and age-appropriate.

blocks.text: For children in this book’s age range, a favourite soft toy or blanket may help with separation. Ensure that any sleep environment is appropriate for the child’s age, health and individual safety needs.
5. Reduced Screen Stimulation

Television, games, short videos and messaging can keep the brain engaged and delay the transition into sleep. A practical family goal is to stop recreational screens before bedtime and replace them with a quieter sequence. The exact timing must fit family life, but a consistent screen-off point is helpful. The American Academy of Pediatrics advises keeping screens out of children’s bedrooms and turning them off before bedtime as part of healthy sleep habits. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/healthy-living/sleep/Pages/healthy-sleep-habits-how-many-hours-does-your-child-need.aspx)
If screens currently remain on until the child falls asleep, change gradually if necessary:

items: Move the device farther from the bed.
Introduce a story, song or audio alternative.

blocks.text: Avoid secretly removing a device after the child falls asleep if the child expects it to remain. Explain the new plan beforehand.
Creating a Bedtime Routine
A helpful bedtime routine is:

items: and guided by an adult boundary.

blocks.text: A simple routine might be:
The same steps do not have to occur in every family. The sequence matters more than copying someone else’s version.
Give Choices Inside the Routine
Small choices can reduce power struggles without handing over the entire bedtime. Offer:
“Would you like the red night clothes or the blue ones?”
“Would you like the animal story or the train story?”
“Would you like a hug, a back rub or a quiet goodnight?”
Avoid:
“Do you want to go to bed?”
when bedtime is not optional. The adult decides that bedtime is happening. The child receives some control over how the routine unfolds.
Common Bedtime Requests

Young children quickly discover that bedtime can be extended. This is not always deliberate manipulation. The child may genuinely remember needs, feel anxious about separation or simply wish to continue receiving attention. The response should be kind and finite.

rows: “We read our one story. We can read another tomorrow.”
“Here is one drink. Then the cup stays beside your bed.”
“Dinner and the bedtime snack are finished. Breakfast comes in the morning.”
“You may use the toilet. Then we return directly to bed.”
“I’ll sit here for two minutes, then I’m leaving. You are safe.”
“You don’t have to force sleep. It is time for your body to rest quietly.”

blocks.text: Address genuine needs. Then avoid reopening the whole routine.
When the Child Keeps Leaving the Bed
If the child repeatedly comes out, long explanations can turn each return into additional attention. Keep the response calm and brief:
“It is sleep time.”

Guide the child back. Repeat as needed. The first few nights of a changed boundary may be difficult. A child may test whether the new pattern is real. Consistency does not mean becoming cold. You can remain gentle without adding another story, snack or play session. If the child is frightened or distressed, respond to the fear while retaining the structure:
“That sound frightened you. I checked the room. You are safe. I’ll tuck you in again, and then it is sleep time.”
Bedtime Fears
Fear of darkness, imagined creatures, noises and separation can appear during early childhood. Avoid mocking the fear:
“Don’t be silly. There is nothing there.”
The feeling is real even when the danger is not. Try:
“The shadow looked frightening. Let’s see what is making it.”
“Your imagination is making a scary picture. You are safe here.”
Helpful supports may include:

items: or talking about the fear earlier in the day.

blocks.text: Avoid building an elaborate monster-checking ritual that accidentally confirms that monsters may genuinely need to be hunted every night. A simple check and calm reassurance are enough.
Nightmares and Night Terrors Are Different
Nightmares
A nightmare is a frightening dream. The child may:

items: and have difficulty returning to sleep.

blocks.text: Offer reassurance:
“That was a frightening dream. You are awake now, and you are safe.”
Avoid pressing for a complete account in the middle of the night. The child can talk more in daylight if they wish.
Night Terrors

During a night terror, a child may scream, appear terrified, sit up or move while still not fully awake. They may not recognise the parent and may remember little or nothing the next morning. Do not try to force the child fully awake. Focus on physical safety and allow the episode to pass. Frequent, prolonged or dangerous episodes should be discussed with a paediatrician, particularly if you are unsure whether the events are night terrors, nightmares, seizures or another sleep problem.
Naps and the Changing Need for Daytime Sleep
Some young children still need a nap. Others begin resisting it or take so long to fall asleep at night that the timing may need review. Signs that a nap may still help include:

items: regularly falling asleep during the day;
becoming extremely distressed by late afternoon;
struggling to function without daytime rest;
or sleeping quickly when given the opportunity.

blocks.text: If a late or long nap repeatedly delays bedtime, consider adjusting its timing or length gradually. Do not remove a needed nap merely to force an earlier bedtime. When naps fade, a quiet rest period can remain:
“You do not have to sleep. This is quiet time for books, drawing or resting.”
Sleep in Shared Rooms
Many children share a room with siblings or parents. The goal is not to reproduce advice designed for a large private bedroom. Practical options include:

items: giving each child a separate comfort item;
settling the younger child first when possible;
creating a quiet activity for the child who is not yet sleepy;
using soft, steady background sound if appropriate;
and keeping late-night adult conversations or devices away from the sleep area.

blocks.text: If siblings repeatedly stimulate each other, adjust the sequence rather than assuming both children are deliberately misbehaving. Different children may need different bedtimes. Fairness does not always mean identical lights-out times.
Sleep and Separation
Bedtime is a daily separation. A child may become especially clingy when:

items: or spending less time with a parent.

blocks.text: Offer extra connection before the final boundary:
“We had a busy day. Let’s sit together for five minutes before the story.”
Then make the goodbye predictable:
“One hug, one kiss and our goodnight words. I’ll see you in the morning.”

Do not disappear secretly while the child is distracted. A trustworthy goodbye may produce protest, but it protects the child’s confidence that adults say when they are leaving and return as promised.
When Parents Use Different Bedtime Approaches

One caregiver may allow another programme. Another may insist on immediate lights out. A grandparent may lie beside the child until sleep, while the parent wants the child to settle more independently. Different relationships can contain different rituals. However, frequent conflict between adults may make bedtime more stressful than the routine itself. Agree privately on:

items: how many stories or requests are included;
how night waking will be handled;
and which parts may differ between caregivers.

blocks.text: Avoid arguing beside the child:
“Your father has spoiled the whole routine.”
“Your mother is too strict.”
The child should not have to choose between adults in order to sleep.
Sleep Independence Is Not a Moral Achievement

Some children settle alone. Some need a parent nearby for longer. Some families share sleeping spaces by preference, culture or necessity. There is no single sleeping arrangement that proves a parent is loving or that a child is independent. The important questions are:

items: Is the arrangement safe for the child’s age?
Is the child receiving sufficient, good-quality sleep?
Is the family functioning reasonably well?
Is anyone feeling trapped, unsafe or severely sleep-deprived?
Does the arrangement respect the needs and boundaries of everyone involved?

blocks.text: If your family wants to change an established pattern, do so gradually and consistently. For example:

items: Move the chair slightly farther away.
Continue until the child can manage the agreed arrangement.

blocks.text: Progress may not be perfectly linear.
Common Sleep Mistakes
1. Beginning Bedtime Only After the Child Is Exhausted
An overtired child may become more active, irritable or distressed. Begin winding down before the child reaches that point.
2. Using Sleep as a Threat
Avoid:
“If you don’t behave, I’m sending you to bed.”
Sleep should feel like a normal physical need, not a punishment.
3. Negotiating the Entire Routine Every Night
Make the sequence clear before bedtime. Offer limited choices, not unlimited extensions.
4. Expecting Immediate Results
A new sleep pattern may take repeated practice. One difficult evening does not mean the routine has failed.
5. Giving Exciting Screens to Settle the Child
The screen may quiet the body while keeping the brain engaged and making the device part of the sleep association.
6. Comparing Children
“Your sister slept alone at three.”
Children differ in temperament, sleep needs and sensitivity.
7. Ignoring Daytime Factors
A child’s sleep may be affected by naps, activity, anxiety, caffeine, illness, medication, pain or family stress.
8. Treating Every Problem as Behavioural
Not all sleep problems are solved through firmer boundaries. Breathing problems, discomfort and medical or developmental conditions require appropriate assessment.
Red Flags
Speak with a paediatrician if your child:

items: gasps, chokes or appears to stop breathing during sleep;
has persistently noisy or difficult breathing;
is extremely sleepy during the day;
regularly falls asleep at inappropriate times;
experiences frequent or unusual movements that concern you;
has sleep problems associated with pain, reflux, itching or breathing difficulty;
shows a significant change in sleep without an obvious explanation;
or has persistent sleep difficulties that seriously affect the child or family.

blocks.text: Snoring is not always harmless. It can sometimes be associated with obstructed breathing during sleep, so frequent or concerning snoring deserves medical discussion. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/healthy-living/sleep/Pages/A-Lullaby-for-Good-Health.aspx) Seek urgent medical help if a child has severe difficulty breathing, becomes blue or unresponsive, has a prolonged seizure-like event or appears seriously unwell.
Medication and Sleep Products
Do not give a child:

unless an appropriately qualified clinician has specifically advised their use for that child. “Natural” does not automatically mean safe, suitable or correctly dosed. A product may also hide an underlying problem that needs assessment. Sleep medicines should never replace an evaluation of breathing, pain, anxiety, routine, environment or developmental needs.
Your Bedtime Review
Does my child still nap? If so, when and for how long? ____________________________________
What happens during the hour before bed? ____________________________________
The most difficult part of bedtime is: ____________________________________
Build a Five-Step Routine
Keep it simple enough to repeat.
What I will say if my child asks for more: ____________________________________
How I will respond if my child leaves the bed: ____________________________________ Practise the routine consistently before adding more changes.
A Seven-Day Sleep Note
If sleep is difficult, keep a brief record for one week.

This may reveal patterns and can be useful when discussing concerns with a clinician. Do not become so focused on exact tracking that it creates more anxiety. The purpose is understanding, not perfect measurement.
Remember This
Your job is not to force sleep.

Your job is to create a safe, predictable opportunity for rest—and to hold its boundaries with calm consistency. A child may still ask for another story. They may still dislike saying goodnight. The routine is working when the pathway becomes familiar, not when the child never protests.
Try This Today

Write down a bedtime routine containing no more than five steps. Tell your child the plan before the evening begins. Offer two small choices within it. When the final step is complete, use one warm and consistent sentence:
“The day is finished. You are safe, you are loved, and now it is time to rest.”
Then resist the temptation to create an entirely new bedtime after the lights go out.
From Mom’s Village

There will be nights when bedtime feels peaceful. There will also be nights when the child is restless, the parent is impatient and no carefully chosen phrase seems to work. One difficult night does not erase the rhythm you are building. Return to the familiar sequence. Respond to genuine needs. Keep unnecessary excitement low. Hold the boundary without turning bedtime into a battle over love. Sleep arrives in its own time. Our role is to make room for it—and to help the child trust that morning will come, the parent will return and tomorrow will begin again.
Authoritative References

items: [World Health Organization — Guidelines on Physical Activity, Sedentary Behaviour and Sleep for Children Under Five](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241550536)
[Great Ormond Street Hospital — Sleep Hygiene in Children and Young People](https://www.gosh.nhs.uk/conditions-and-treatments/procedures-and-treatments/sleep-hygiene-children/)

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by an Indian paediatrician or paediatric sleep specialist. The final edition should represent:

items: children sharing rooms with parents, siblings or grandparents;
homes affected by heat, traffic and neighbourhood noise;
parents working late or changing shifts;
and families without a separate bedroom for the child.

blocks.text: A final design package could include:

items: a comparison of nightmares and night terrors;
and a clearly displayed medical-warning box for snoring, breathing pauses and severe daytime sleepiness.

blocks.text: The chapter should avoid presenting one culturally specific sleeping arrangement as universally correct. Its emphasis should remain on safety, adequate rest, dependable routines and family wellbeing.

pages.epigraph: “Our role is to offer food with care. A child’s role is to learn, little by little, how to listen to their own body.”

blocks.text: The Uneaten Dinner

A mother had spent nearly an hour preparing dinner. There was dal, rice, vegetables and fresh rotis. She had made the vegetable mild because her four-year-old disliked anything spicy. The child arrived at the table, looked at the plate and said:

“I don’t like it.” “You haven’t even tasted it,” the mother replied. The child turned away. What began as encouragement soon became negotiation. “Just five bites.” “No.” “Three bites, and then you can have something sweet.” “No!” “At least eat the dal. I made it specially for you.” The child began to cry. The mother became angry. By the end of the meal, the food remained uneaten and both of them felt defeated. This happens in many caring homes.

A parent sees food as nourishment, effort, culture and love. A young child sees a plate containing unfamiliar smells, mixed textures, changing appetite and one of the few decisions they can physically control. The parent thinks:
“My child must eat.” The child’s body says:

“You cannot make me swallow.” The harder both sides push, the more the table becomes a battleground. Helping children develop a healthy relationship with food requires structure—but also patience, repeated exposure and respect for the signals coming from the child’s body.
What Responsive Feeding Means

Responsive feeding is an approach in which the adult provides appropriate food and pays attention to the child’s signs of hunger, fullness, interest and distress. The adult encourages eating but does not force it. The [World Health Organization’s feeding guidance](https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/infant-and-young-child-feeding) recommends patient, responsive feeding: helping children eat, encouraging them without force and interacting warmly during meals.
For children between two and seven, this generally means:
The adult is responsible for:

items: offering meals and snacks at broadly predictable times;
and creating a respectful mealtime environment.

blocks.text: The child is responsible for:

items: deciding whether to eat from what is offered;
deciding which offered foods to eat;
and responding to their body’s hunger and fullness signals.

blocks.text: This is not permissive feeding. The child does not decide that chocolate will replace dinner or that food must be prepared on demand throughout the evening. The adult still provides the structure. But the adult does not control what happens inside the child’s body.
Appetite Is Not the Same Every Day
Young children do not always eat predictable amounts. A child may eat generously one day and very little the next. Appetite can change because of:

It is usually more useful to notice patterns across several days than to judge nutrition by one meal. A child who barely touches dinner may have eaten more at breakfast, school or a grandparent’s home than the parent realises. Ask calmly:
“What did you have after school?”
Avoid turning the question into an investigation or accusation.
The Mealtime Partnership
A healthy mealtime has both structure and flexibility.

rows: At least one familiar component when practical
Remaining at the table for a developmentally reasonable period
Learning that unfamiliar food may become familiar

blocks.text: The goal is not to make the child eat a perfect plate today. It is to help the child build skills and confidence that can support eating over many years.
Selective Eating Is Common

Between the toddler and preschool years, many children become more cautious about food. A child who once ate everything may suddenly reject vegetables, mixed dishes or foods that look slightly different. This can feel alarming, but ordinary selective eating is common in early childhood. The American Academy of Pediatrics notes that choosy eating is particularly common between ages two and four and recommends a low-pressure approach with regular meals, repeated exposure and familiar foods alongside other offerings. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/tips-tools/ask-the-pediatrician/Pages/How-Do-I-Help-My-Picky-Eater-Try-More-Foods.aspx)
A selective eater may:

items: reject a food because it looks different;
eat the same meal repeatedly for a period;
or accept a food one day and reject it the next.

blocks.text: These behaviours can be frustrating without necessarily indicating a serious feeding disorder. However, some children’s eating difficulties extend beyond an ordinary selective phase. We will examine those signs later in the chapter.
Familiarity Comes Before Eating

Adults sometimes assume that if a child refuses a food once, the child permanently dislikes it. Children often need repeated, pressure-free experiences before a food feels safe enough to taste. Progress may look like:

items: Tolerating the food on the table.
Eating a larger amount on another occasion.

blocks.text: Not every step must occur during the same meal. A child who touches a new vegetable and then leaves it has still gathered information about it. Avoid demanding:
“You must take one bite.”
For some children, even a “no-thank-you bite” creates pressure and increases fear. Instead, you might say:
“You don’t have to eat it. It can stay on the side of your plate.”
“That is roasted pumpkin. You can explore it if you want.”
Then continue with the meal.
Offer Familiarity Without Becoming a Short-Order Cook
Parents are often caught between two extremes:

items: forcing the child to eat the family meal;
or preparing an entirely separate preferred meal after every refusal.

blocks.text: A middle path is to offer the family’s food with at least one component the child usually accepts when practical. For example:

items: dal, rice, vegetable and plain curd;
roti, paneer dish, cucumber and fruit;
idli, sambar and a familiar chutney;
or khichdi with a familiar side food.

blocks.text: The familiar item helps the child approach the table without fearing that everything will be impossible to eat. It is not a reward for refusing. It is part of the original meal. If the child chooses only the familiar food, remain neutral. Continue offering variety over time.
Family Food Does Not Have to Be Identical
A child does not necessarily need a completely separate meal, but family food may require developmentally appropriate adaptation. You might:

items: remove the child’s portion before adding strong chilli;
cook vegetables to a manageable texture;
provide a less spicy dal alongside a family curry;
or allow sauces and chutneys to remain optional.

blocks.text: This is not “spoiling” the child. It is making shared food accessible while the child’s eating skills develop. At the same time, avoid allowing the child to control the entire household menu indefinitely. The family’s food culture belongs to everyone.
Structure Meals and Snacks
Children who graze continuously may arrive at meals without enough appetite to eat. A predictable rhythm of meals and snacks helps the child learn:
“Food is offered regularly. I do not need to panic, and I will not eat continuously throughout the day.” A family pattern might include:

The exact timing depends on school, sleep, activity and medical needs. Water can generally remain available. Frequent milk, juice or calorie-containing drinks close to meals may reduce appetite for food in some children. Children with diabetes, metabolic conditions, growth concerns or other medical needs require individual guidance from their healthcare team.
Mealtime Without Screens

Many parents use a phone or television because it seems to help the child eat more. The adult distracts. The child opens their mouth. The meal is completed. In the short term, this may reduce conflict. But the child may pay less attention to the sensory experience of eating or to internal signs of hunger and fullness. If a child currently eats only with a screen, an abrupt removal may produce distress and very little food intake.
Change gradually:

items: Move from continuous video to music or audio.
Turn the screen off for the first few minutes.
Increase the screen-free period over time.

blocks.text: If screen dependence is severe or the child’s intake falls significantly without distraction, consult a paediatrician or feeding professional rather than beginning a power struggle.
Do Not Use Pressure Disguised as Praise
Pressure does not always sound harsh. It can sound loving:
“One more bite for Mama.”
“Good children finish their food.”
“Look how happy Grandma is when you eat.”
“You ate all your rice! I’m so proud of you.”
The child may begin eating to manage adult emotions rather than responding to their own body. Praise skills and participation instead:
“You served yourself carefully.”
“You touched a food that felt unfamiliar.”
“You listened when your stomach said it was full.”
“You helped carry the bowls.”
This keeps approval separate from the amount consumed.
Hunger and Fullness Language
Help children notice body signals without telling them what they must feel. Instead of:
“You cannot be full. You hardly ate anything.”
try:
“Your stomach is telling you it has had enough.”
Instead of:
“You must be hungry.”
try:
“Dinner is available. You can check what your body needs.”
Children will not interpret these signals perfectly every time. Adults do not either. The purpose is gradual awareness—not flawless self-regulation.
Sweets, Desserts and “Treat” Foods
When sweets are presented as the grand prize for eating vegetables, they become more desirable while ordinary food becomes the unpleasant task required to earn them. Avoid:
“Finish your dinner and then you can have ice cream.”
This may encourage the child to eat beyond fullness to obtain dessert. Possible approaches include:

items: serving dessert occasionally without requiring a cleaned plate;
offering a small dessert portion alongside or after the meal according to family custom;
and maintaining calm limits about when and how much is available.

blocks.text: You can say:
“There is one piece of mithai for each person today.”
If the child asks for more:
“You enjoyed it. That is the portion available today.”
Do not describe foods as morally “good,” “bad,” “clean” or “guilty.” Food differs in nutritional value and purpose, but eating does not determine a child’s moral worth.
Do Not Use Food as the Main Emotional Solution

Food naturally accompanies comfort, celebration and culture. There is nothing wrong with enjoying birthday cake or offering warm food after a difficult day. The concern arises when eating becomes the only response to every feeling:
“Don’t cry—have a chocolate.”
“You were brave at the doctor, so you deserve chips.”
“If you behave, I’ll buy you ice cream.”
Children also need other ways to experience comfort and celebration:
Food can be part of care without carrying the entire emotional job.
Helping Children Participate
Participation creates familiarity without requiring consumption. A child can help:

items: choose between two vegetables at the market;
or grow a herb in a pot.

blocks.text: Do not immediately demand:
“You made it, so now you must eat it.”
Preparing food is valuable even if the child is not ready to taste it.
Making Mealtimes Social

Family meals do not need to involve every family member at every meal. Work hours, school schedules and commuting can make that impossible. Aim for shared eating when realistically available—even if it is breakfast, an evening snack or one weekend meal. Keep conversation suitable for the child. Avoid using the table for:

items: discussing the child’s eating in front of others;

blocks.text: A child is less likely to explore food while feeling watched, judged or discussed.
Feeding in Joint and Multigenerational Families
Grandparents and relatives may express love through food. A refusal can feel personal:
“I made this specially for you.”

Caregivers may also disagree about how much the child should eat. One adult insists on a clean plate. Another follows the child with a bowl. A third offers biscuits after a refused meal. Discuss the approach away from the child. A respectful family agreement might be:

items: adults decide the food and timing;
no one forces or chases the child;
at least one familiar food is available;
dessert is not used as a bribe;
and genuine growth concerns go to the paediatrician rather than being debated at every meal.

blocks.text: You might say to a relative:
“We value the food you prepare. We are helping her listen to her hunger, so we will offer and encourage without forcing.”
Body Talk at the Table
Children absorb adults’ attitudes towards bodies and dieting. Comments such as:
“I was bad today—I ate cake.”
“You’re getting chubby.”
“Girls should not eat so much.”
“Your brother is too thin.”

can create shame and confusion. Avoid commenting frequently on the child’s weight, shape or appetite. Talk instead about what bodies can do and how varied food supports growth, energy and health. If a child’s weight or growth needs attention, discuss it privately with an appropriate healthcare professional—not as public criticism.
Choking Safety
Young children need food prepared in developmentally safe forms and should be supervised while eating. Teach children to:

items: and eat without small objects or toys in the mouth.

blocks.text: Certain foods may need to be cut, cooked, grated, spread thinly or otherwise modified depending on the child’s age and chewing ability. Families should obtain current, age-specific choking-prevention and first-aid guidance from qualified healthcare sources. Adults caring for children should know how to respond to choking. If a child coughs, gags or struggles frequently during meals, do not assume it is ordinary fussiness. Seek professional assessment.
Allergy Is Not the Same as Dislike
A child refusing a food does not automatically indicate an allergy. But possible allergic reactions require attention. Symptoms may include:

items: or other rapid symptoms after eating.

blocks.text: Breathing difficulty, swelling of the tongue or throat, collapse or a severe rapidly developing reaction requires emergency medical care. Do not deliberately reintroduce a food suspected of causing a serious allergic reaction without medical guidance. Families managing a diagnosed allergy should provide clear written instructions to schools, relatives and other caregivers.
Ordinary Selectivity or a Feeding Concern?

rows: Often seen in ordinary selective eating
Eats a very narrow and shrinking range
Shows intense fear of eating or choking
Regularly coughs, chokes or struggles to swallow
Often seen in ordinary selective eating
Eats at least some foods from several groups
Avoids entire textures or nutritional groups
Has weight loss, poor growth or nutritional deficiency
Can usually eat in ordinary settings
Cannot participate in meals outside a very specific routine
Often seen in ordinary selective eating
Meals regularly involve panic, gagging or severe distress

blocks.text: Only a qualified professional can assess the cause. Feeding difficulties may relate to:
These concerns are not solved by greater hunger, punishment or force.
Red Flags
Seek medical or feeding support if your child:

items: is losing weight or not growing as expected;
regularly coughs, chokes or has difficulty breathing during meals;
has pain when eating or swallowing;
has persistent diarrhoea, severe constipation or blood in the stool;
eats only a very small number of foods;
appears fearful of swallowing or choking;
takes an unusually long time to complete meals;
depends on distraction for almost all intake;
shows signs of dehydration or nutritional deficiency;
or experiences severe family distress around eating.

blocks.text: Seek urgent medical care for severe allergic symptoms, breathing difficulty, choking that obstructs breathing, unusual unresponsiveness or signs of serious dehydration.
Common Mealtime Mistakes
1. Forcing the Spoon Into the Child’s Mouth
Force can increase distress and reduce trust in eating.
2. Chasing the Child Around the House With Food
Create a reasonable eating place and rhythm instead of turning meals into a pursuit.
3. Offering Unlimited Alternatives After Refusal
Include something familiar in the original meal, but avoid opening a made-to-order kitchen throughout the evening.
4. Bribing With Dessert
This places dessert above all other food and may encourage eating beyond fullness.
5. Serving Overwhelming Portions
Begin with small amounts. More can be offered.
6. Hiding Every Nutritious Food
Adding nutrition to familiar recipes can be useful, but complete secrecy does not help a child learn to recognise and accept the food itself.
7. Discussing the Child’s Eating Constantly
Children can feel watched and pressured even when adults speak about them to someone else.
8. Assuming the Child Is Manipulating You
Look for hunger patterns, sensory needs, discomfort, fear and developmental skill before assigning negative motives.
9. Ignoring Cultural and Family Preferences
Healthy eating can include the household’s familiar grains, pulses, vegetables, fruits, dairy products, eggs, fish or meats according to its culture, beliefs, access and dietary practices.
A Pressure-Free Mealtime Script
Before the meal:
“Dinner is ready. We have rice, dal, beans and curd.”
At the table:
“You may choose from what is here.”
If the child refuses:
“You don’t want it. It can stay on your plate.”
If the child wants a different meal:
“This is what we are serving tonight. There is rice and curd if you want something familiar.”
If the child says they are finished:
“Listen to your stomach. If you are finished, place your plate here.”

If the child asks for food shortly afterwards: Respond according to the child’s age, health and family routine. You might offer the remaining meal or explain when the next planned snack will be. The goal is not to punish the child for refusing. It is to keep food opportunities predictable.
Your Mealtime Review
The most difficult meal in our home is: ____________________________________
What usually happens before that meal: ____________________________________
Foods my child reliably accepts: ____________________________________
Foods my child is learning about: ____________________________________
Possible sensory concerns—texture, smell, temperature or foods touching: ____________________________________
Drinks or snacks that may be affecting appetite: ____________________________________
One form of pressure I can reduce: ____________________________________
One way my child can participate: ____________________________________
A Simple Meal-Planning Pattern
For each meal, consider including:

items: A familiar food: something the child usually manages.
A family food: what the household is eating.
A learning food: something less familiar, offered in a small amount.
Water or the family’s planned drink.

blocks.text: This is a planning guide, not a nutritional prescription. Children with allergies, medical conditions, growth concerns or specialised diets need individual advice from a paediatrician and qualified dietitian.
A Mealtime Check
Ask yourself:

items: Are meals and snacks offered at broadly predictable times?
Does the child arrive with some opportunity to feel hungry?
Is at least one manageable food available?
Are portions small enough not to overwhelm?
Can the child stop when comfortably full?
Are screens and distractions being reduced realistically?
Are adults avoiding body shame and food-based moral language?
Are caregivers following the same essential safety guidance?
Have we sought help for pain, choking, poor growth or severe restriction?

blocks.text: Remember This
You can decide what, when and where food is offered.

You cannot—and should not—force a child’s body to feel hungry, enjoy a taste or swallow on command. Structure the opportunity. Offer variety. Include familiarity. Model eating. Allow learning to take time.
Try This Today

At the next meal, remove one form of pressure. Do not ask for a particular number of bites. Do not bargain with dessert. Do not comment repeatedly on how much the child has eaten. Serve a small portion of the family food with something familiar. Then say:
“You can choose what your body needs from what is here.”
Notice whether the atmosphere changes, even if the child’s eating does not change immediately.
From Mom’s Village

Food carries so much of family life. It carries memory, tradition, effort and love. That is why rejection can hurt. But when a child refuses the vegetable, the child is not rejecting the parent who cooked it. They are still learning about appetite, taste, texture, trust and control. Keep bringing the family to the table. Keep offering food with care. Keep the child’s dignity beside the plate. Some meals will be eaten eagerly. Some will barely be touched.
The long-term lesson is larger than one dinner:
Food is available. My body belongs to me. I can explore without fear. The people at this table will guide me without forcing me. That is a nourishing relationship with food.
Authoritative References

items: [World Health Organization — Infant and Young Child Feeding](https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/infant-and-young-child-feeding)
[World Health Organization — Feeding an Infant or Young Child](https://www.who.int/tools/your-life-your-health/life-phase/newborns-and-children-under-5-years/feeding-an-infant-or-young-child)
[HealthyChildren.org — Helping a Selective Eater Try More Foods](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/tips-tools/ask-the-pediatrician/Pages/How-Do-I-Help-My-Picky-Eater-Try-More-Foods.aspx)

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by an Indian paediatrician and paediatric dietitian. The final edition should include varied examples representing:

items: children fed by grandparents or domestic caregivers;
food insecurity and limited kitchen resources;
and children with developmental or sensory feeding differences.

blocks.text: Suggested final resources include:

items: an allergy information sheet for caregivers;
an illustrated choking-safety page reviewed by a clinician;
and a clear comparison between ordinary selective eating and feeding concerns requiring assessment.

blocks.text: The book should not prescribe calorie amounts, portion sizes, supplements or restrictive diets for individual children. Such guidance belongs with appropriately qualified professionals who can assess the child’s growth, health and family circumstances.

pages.epigraph: “Toileting is not a test of obedience. It is a physical and developmental skill learned through readiness, practice and trust.”

blocks.text: The Accident at the Door

A father had just finished dressing his three-year-old for a family function. The child was wearing new clothes. Everyone was already late. As they reached the front door, the child had an accident. The father stared at the wet clothes and said:

“Why didn’t you tell me? You know how to use the toilet!” The child lowered her head. She had been busy watching everyone prepare. She had noticed the feeling in her bladder—but too late. Then she had been afraid to interrupt. What looked like carelessness was actually a skill still developing. Using the toilet requires a child to:

Adults perform this sequence without thinking. For a young child, it is a complicated chain. Accidents are not proof that the child does not care. They are information about where support is still needed.
Toilet Learning, Not Toilet Performance
Families use different terms: potty training, toilet training or toilet learning. Whatever term you prefer, the aim is the same:
To help the child gradually recognise body signals and manage toileting with increasing independence.

The process should not become a contest between families. A child who uses the toilet earlier is not more intelligent, disciplined or loved. A child who learns later is not lazy or deliberately difficult. Development, health, temperament, sensory needs, communication, caregiving arrangements and previous experiences all influence the process.
Signs That a Child May Be Ready to Participate
Readiness is not one magical moment. A child may be ready in some ways but still need support in others. Helpful signs can include:

items: noticing or communicating that they are wet or soiled;
showing awareness before or during urination or a bowel movement;
staying dry for somewhat longer periods;
helping pull clothing up or down;
and showing interest in doing things independently.

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics explains that readiness involves several areas, including physical awareness, motor skills, communication, memory and emotional development. [HealthyChildren.org](https://healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/toddler/toilet-training/Pages/How-to-Tell-When-Your-Child-is-Ready.aspx) A child does not need to demonstrate every skill before being introduced gently to the toilet. But if the child is frightened, physically uncomfortable or completely unable to participate, more preparation may be needed.
Preparing Before Removing Nappies
Toilet learning begins before expecting the child to remain dry. You can:

items: use simple words for urine, bowel movements and body parts;
allow the child to see the bathroom routine in an age-appropriate way;
explain what the toilet is for;
help the child manage easy clothing;
keep a potty or secure toilet seat available;
and establish handwashing as part of the sequence.

blocks.text: Use clear, neutral language. The family may say “wee,” “pee,” “potty,” “toilet,” “poo” or familiar words in its home language. The exact term matters less than using it without shame.
Potty or Toilet?
Either can work.
A floor potty may:

items: allow the child’s feet to rest securely;

blocks.text: A toilet-seat reducer may:

items: reduce the later transition from potty to toilet;
and allow the child to imitate family members.

blocks.text: If using an adult toilet, provide a stable step so the child’s feet are supported. Dangling legs can make the child feel insecure and may make bowel movements more difficult. Choose an arrangement that is:
Begin During a Manageable Period
Avoid beginning an intensive toileting change immediately before:

when there is flexibility to wait. Life does not always provide a perfectly calm period. But a few relatively predictable days can make it easier to observe patterns and respond consistently. Tell all regular caregivers about the approach. A child will be confused if one adult encourages toileting calmly while another forces, shames or repeatedly returns to nappies for convenience without explanation.
A Simple Toilet-Learning Process
Step 1: Notice the Child’s Pattern
