For several days, observe when the child usually urinates or has a bowel movement. Common opportunities include:
The goal is not constant surveillance. It is learning the child’s natural rhythm.
Step 2: Offer Regular Opportunities
Invite rather than repeatedly interrogate. Instead of:
“Do you need to go?”
which often receives an automatic “No,” try:
“It’s toilet time before we leave.”
“Your body is doing the toilet dance. Let’s go and check.”
Keep visits brief unless the child is comfortable.
Step 3: Use Easy Clothing
Choose clothing the child can manage:

items: and dresses or garments that do not create complicated fastenings.

blocks.text: Independence is harder when clothing requires adult-level coordination.
Step 4: Respond Neutrally to Accidents
Say:
“Your clothes are wet. The urine came before you reached the toilet. Let’s clean up.”
Invite age-appropriate participation:
“Please place the wet clothes in this basket.”
Do not make cleanup humiliating or excessively burdensome.
Step 5: Notice the Process
Acknowledge skills rather than turning bodily functions into a performance.
“You noticed the feeling and told me.”
“You pulled your clothes down by yourself.”
“You sat and tried.”
The child can succeed in part of the sequence even if no urine or stool reaches the toilet.
Rewards: Use Carefully
Some families use stickers or a simple chart. These can provide encouragement, especially for participating in the routine. If using rewards, focus on actions within the child’s control:
Avoid punishing the absence of urine or stool. A child cannot always produce a bowel movement on command. Do not use:

items: or escalating prizes that turn toileting into a negotiation.

blocks.text: Warm acknowledgement and growing independence may be enough for many children.
Accidents Are Part of Learning
Even after a child appears trained, accidents may happen because they are:

items: unable to find a clean toilet;
or coping with a major change.

blocks.text: Daytime accidents should generally become less frequent as the skill strengthens, but the process is rarely perfectly linear. The American Academy of Pediatrics notes that accidents may continue for a period after toilet learning and recommends medical discussion when they are frequent, persistent or not improving. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/tips-tools/ask-the-pediatrician/Pages/Are-accidents-normal-or-the-sign-of-a-problem.aspx)
Avoid saying:
“You did this on purpose.”
“Only babies wet themselves.”
“Everyone will laugh at you.”
Shame does not improve bladder awareness. It may teach the child to hide wet clothing or bodily needs.
Regression After Progress
A child who had been using the toilet may begin having accidents again. Possible reasons include:
Respond with curiosity:
“Your body has been having more accidents. We’re going to help you.”

Return temporarily to more reminders and support. If the change is sudden, persistent or accompanied by pain, thirst, frequent urination, fever or other symptoms, consult a paediatrician. Regression is a signal to investigate—not an invitation to punish.
Constipation: A Common Hidden Difficulty

Constipation can make toilet learning painful and frightening. A child may begin withholding stool because passing it once caused pain. The longer the child holds it, the harder and more uncomfortable it may become. Signs can include:

items: hiding, stiffening or standing on tiptoe;
small smears or loose-looking stool in underwear;

blocks.text: Soiling can sometimes occur when softer stool leaks around a mass of retained hard stool. It should not automatically be interpreted as laziness or deliberate behaviour. The [US National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases](https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information/digestive-diseases/constipation-children/symptoms-causes) explains that withholding, painful stools and leakage can all be associated with childhood constipation.

If you suspect constipation, speak with a paediatrician. Do not give laxatives, enemas, suppositories or other bowel medicines without appropriate medical guidance for that child. Constipation treatment may take time and should not be stopped or changed casually.
Supporting Comfortable Bowel Movements
General habits that may support bowel health include:

items: fibre-containing foods appropriate to the child;
an unhurried toilet opportunity after meals;
and prompt medical attention for persistent pain or withholding.

blocks.text: Food changes alone may not be enough once constipation is established. Do not repeatedly tell a child:
“Just push harder.”
A frightened or constipated child may be physically unable to relax. You might say:
“Your body is having trouble letting the stool come out. This is not your fault. We will get help.”
Sitting Position Matters
For a bowel movement, the child should feel physically secure. Helpful positioning often includes:

items: feet resting firmly on a floor or stable step;
and the child leaning slightly forward if comfortable.

blocks.text: A child who fears falling into the toilet may hold their body tense. Never restrain a frightened child on the toilet. Help them become familiar gradually or use a more secure setup.
Daytime Dryness and Night-Time Dryness Are Different

Staying dry during the day and staying dry while asleep are different developmental processes. A child may reliably use the toilet while awake and continue wetting at night. Night-time dryness depends partly on:
It is not simply a matter of motivation. Do not punish bedwetting. Do not announce it to relatives or siblings. Use practical protection such as:

The [NHS guidance on bedwetting](https://www.nhs.uk/symptoms/bedwetting/) emphasises that bedwetting is not the child’s fault and advises against punishment. Seek medical guidance when bedwetting is concerning, persists at an older age, begins again after a long dry period or occurs alongside pain, unusual thirst, constipation, daytime accidents or other symptoms.
Should You Wake a Child to Use the Toilet?

Taking a sleeping child to the toilet may reduce wet bedding on a particular night, but it does not necessarily teach the child’s body to wake independently. If families use this temporarily for practical reasons, it should not involve fear or punishment. Discuss persistent bedwetting with a healthcare professional rather than relying indefinitely on repeated night waking. Make sure the path to the toilet is safe and reasonably lit.
Public and School Toilets
Some children avoid toilets outside home because of:
Preparation can help:

items: visit the toilet with the child;
explain automatic flushing or hand dryers;
carry tissues or necessary hygiene supplies;
teach the child whom to ask;
practise a sentence such as, “I need to use the toilet”;
and inform the teacher privately when the child needs support.

blocks.text: Never instruct a child to hold urine or stool for the entire school day. Persistent avoidance may contribute to accidents, urinary difficulties or constipation.
Toileting and Bodily Privacy
Toileting is also an opportunity to teach respectful body boundaries. Use correct or clear family words for private body parts. Teach:

items: caregiving touch should have a clear hygiene or health purpose;
the child can ask what is happening;
no one should take photographs of private toileting;
and the child should tell a trusted adult if anyone makes them uncomfortable.

blocks.text: A young child may still need help wiping or changing. Explain before helping:
“You need help cleaning your bottom. I’m going to use a wet cloth now.”
Do not tease, photograph or share embarrassing toileting stories for entertainment. Dignity matters even when the child is small.
Learning to Wipe
Independent wiping develops gradually. Children may need help because the task requires:

items: understanding how much paper or water to use;
and recognising when they are finished.

blocks.text: Teach step by step. Depending on the household’s practice, the child may learn to use water, toilet paper or both. Ensure the method is hygienic and safe. Children with vulvas should be taught to wipe from front to back to reduce the movement of germs towards the urinary opening. Continue checking or helping until the child can manage reliably. Independence should not come at the cost of hygiene or discomfort.
Handwashing
The toileting routine finishes with washing and drying hands. Teach the child to:

items: Rub palms, backs of hands, between fingers and around nails.

blocks.text: Provide a stable step if the sink is too high. Supervise until the child can use the tap, soap and water safely. Keep cleaning products and medicines out of reach.
When a Child Refuses the Toilet
Refusal may occur because the child:

items: is not ready for the expectation;
is frightened of the seat or flush;

blocks.text: Find the obstacle.
“The flush is too loud. You may leave the room before I flush.”
“You want your nappy for a bowel movement. We will help your body feel safe and talk with the doctor about the pain.”
“You don’t want to stop playing. The toys will wait here while we visit the toilet.”
If every attempt produces a major struggle, pause the pressure while maintaining gentle familiarity and investigating possible physical causes. A break is not failure.
Toileting and Additional Needs
Children with developmental, communication, motor or sensory differences may need:

Do not assume that a child who cannot speak cannot learn toileting signals. Watch for gestures, posture, facial expressions and behavioural patterns. Seek an individual plan from the child’s healthcare and developmental team when needed. The goal is participation and dignity—not comparison with peers.
Common Toileting Mistakes
1. Beginning Because of Social Pressure
Another child’s timeline does not determine your child’s readiness.
2. Asking Every Five Minutes
Constant reminders can create resistance and make the whole day revolve around toileting. Use likely times and observed signals.
3. Punishing Accidents
Punishment cannot accelerate nervous-system maturation or bladder awareness.
4. Keeping the Child on the Toilet for a Long Time
Short, calm opportunities are usually more respectful and manageable.
5. Ignoring Constipation
Painful stool can turn toileting into fear.
6. Removing All Clothing Independence
If adults always manage every step, the child has fewer opportunities to practise.
7. Expecting Night Dryness Immediately
Night-time bladder control may develop later than daytime control.
8. Publicly Discussing Accidents
Protect the child’s privacy.
9. Assuming Soiling Is Deliberate
Leakage can be associated with constipation and requires proper assessment.
10. Using Fear
Never threaten doctors, injections, school humiliation or abandonment because of accidents.
Red Flags
Consult a paediatrician if your child has:

items: a weak urinary stream or difficulty passing urine;
repeated daytime wetting that is not improving;
a sudden return of accidents after a long dry period;
weakness, numbness or unusual changes in walking;
or intense fear or distress that prevents toileting.

blocks.text: The NIDDK advises medical review when constipation persists and urgent review when it occurs with symptoms such as rectal bleeding, blood in the stool, constant abdominal pain, vomiting or weight loss. [NIDDK](https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information/digestive-diseases/constipation-children/symptoms-causes) Seek urgent care if a child cannot pass urine, appears seriously unwell, has severe abdominal pain, has significant bleeding or shows signs of severe dehydration.
Your Child’s Toileting Map
My child currently notices:

items: The need for a bowel movement

blocks.text: My child can currently:
Possible signs of pain or constipation: ____________________________________
Times my child usually urinates or passes stool: ____________________________________
One skill we will practise next: ____________________________________
A Simple Visual Routine
Create pictures for:
A child may need adult help at several stages. The chart is a guide—not a demand for complete independence.
An Accident Script
When an accident happens:
“Your clothes are wet/soiled. Accidents happen while bodies are learning.”
“Let’s go somewhere private and clean you.”
“The wet clothes go here.”
“Next time, we’ll try to notice the feeling sooner.”
Then move on. Avoid turning the accident into a lecture. The child has already experienced the natural inconvenience of changing and cleaning.
Remember This
A child’s body is not disobedient because it is still learning. Toilet independence grows through:
Accidents are a need for help—not a character flaw.
Try This Today
Look at your bathroom from your child’s height. Ask:

items: Can the child reach the toilet or potty?
Can they reach soap and water safely?
Is there a sound, smell or surface that may frighten them?
Do they know how to ask for help?

blocks.text: Change one part of the environment before asking the child to change their behaviour.
From Mom’s Village

There will be accidents at inconvenient times. There may be a wet bed after several dry nights. A child may announce an urgent need just after the family leaves home. These moments are frustrating—but they are not failures. Respond to the body first. Clean what needs cleaning. Protect the child’s privacy. Investigate pain and constipation. Then continue teaching. One day, the sequence that required reminders, spare clothes and patient assistance will become an ordinary part of the child’s independence.
The child may not remember every accident. But they may remember whether, while learning something deeply personal, they were treated with dignity.
Authoritative References

items: [HealthyChildren.org — How to Tell When Your Child Is Ready](https://healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/toddler/toilet-training/Pages/How-to-Tell-When-Your-Child-is-Ready.aspx)
[NIDDK — Symptoms and Causes of Constipation in Children](https://www.niddk.nih.gov/health-information/digestive-diseases/constipation-children/symptoms-causes)

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by an Indian paediatrician, paediatric gastroenterologist or continence specialist. The final edition should represent:

items: homes with shared or outdoor bathrooms;
school toilets with varying levels of privacy and hygiene;
children cared for by domestic workers;
travel by school bus or public transport;
and children with motor, sensory, communication or developmental differences.

blocks.text: Suggested visual resources include:

items: and a private caregiver information sheet for school.

blocks.text: Medical treatment for constipation, urinary symptoms, infections or bedwetting must remain outside the scope of general parenting advice and should be individualised by qualified clinicians.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety
pages.epigraph: “Technology is part of childhood now. Our task is not simply to fear it or surrender to it, but to teach children how it fits into a healthy life.”

blocks.text: The Video That Would Not End

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: A mother gave her four-year-old a phone while she prepared dinner. She needed fifteen uninterrupted minutes. The child watched one video. Then another began automatically. When dinner was ready, the mother reached for the phone. The child screamed, tightened his grip and kicked the chair. “You’ve watched enough,” she said. “No! One more!” The mother felt guilty for offering the phone and furious that he would not return it. What began as a practical solution had become a battle.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Screens are unusually good at holding attention. Bright movement, sound, rapid rewards and endless content make stopping difficult—especially for a young brain that is still developing self-control and the ability to shift attention. This does not mean screens are inherently harmful or that parents are failing when they use them. Screens can:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: and give an exhausted parent a necessary pause.

blocks.text: The question is not simply:
“Are screens good or bad?”
A better set of questions is:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: What habits is the family building?

blocks.text: Think Beyond Minutes
Time matters, particularly in early childhood. But screen health is not explained by a timer alone. Consider five parts of media use:
1. The Child

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: How old is the child? What is their temperament? Can they understand what they see? Do they become frightened or highly dysregulated? Does the child have communication, sensory or accessibility needs?
2. The Content
Is it:

items: and reasonably free from manipulative advertising?

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: 3. The Context
Is the child:
4. What It Replaces
Is screen use regularly displacing:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: 5. The Child’s Response

Can the child disengage with support? Does screen use regularly lead to intense distress? Is the child copying concerning behaviour? Does the experience support curiosity—or leave the child restless and disconnected? These questions often tell us more than the number of minutes alone.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: General Guidance for Young Children

The World Health Organization advises limiting sedentary screen time in children under five and protecting active play, responsive interaction and adequate sleep. For children aged two to four, its guidance recommends no more than one hour of sedentary screen time, with less being preferable; for children under two, it recommends none. [WHO](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241550536) Published recommendations are useful guides, but families should also consider:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: the child’s age and developmental needs;
and what the screen is replacing.

blocks.text: A video call with a grandparent is not identical to an hour of rapidly changing short videos. A child using an aided-communication device is not simply “having screen time.” Avoid treating all digital experiences as equal.
A Family Media Plan

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: A media plan reduces decisions made in the middle of conflict. It answers questions such as:

items: Which devices may the child use?
Which apps or programmes are approved?
What happens when screen time ends?
Which parts of the day are protected?

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics encourages families to create media plans that protect sleep, play, relationships and other important activities. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/family-life/Media/Pages/How-to-Make-a-Family-Media-Use-Plan.aspx) The plan should be simple enough to remember. For example:
“Screens are available after the afternoon snack.”
“The phone stays out of the bedroom.”
“An adult chooses the programme.”
“The programme ends after one episode.”
“We do not watch during dinner.”
A clear rule is easier to maintain than a daily argument.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Use Predictable Screen Windows
Screens become harder to manage when the child believes they may appear at any time after enough asking. A predictable window may help:

items: a short period while dinner is prepared;
a family film on a weekend;
or a video call at a familiar time.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: This does not mean every family needs daily screen use. It means that if screens are part of the routine, predictability can reduce constant negotiation. When the child asks outside that time:
“Screens are available after your snack. They are not available now.”
You do not need to invent a new explanation each time.
Choose Content Deliberately
Before giving a young child access, examine:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: and whether the programme has a clear ending.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Be especially cautious with open video platforms. A child may begin with suitable content and move quickly into unrelated or disturbing material through recommendations or autoplay. Whenever possible:

items: and remain within hearing or viewing distance.

blocks.text: “Educational” is a marketing label, not a guarantee of quality. Ask:
“What is my child actually doing and learning here?”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Co-Viewing: Join the Experience
When an adult watches or plays alongside the child, the screen can become a shared experience rather than an isolated one. You might:

items: or help the child notice when something is pretend.

blocks.text: “That character is feeling left out. What could her friend do?”
“We saw a bird like that in the park.”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: “The advertisement wants us to ask for that toy. Watching it does not mean we need to buy it.”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Co-viewing does not mean turning every programme into a lesson. Sometimes enjoying it together is enough. Recent AAP guidance describes co-viewing and co-playing as useful parts of a family media plan because they allow adults to guide children’s digital experiences. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/family-life/Media/Pages/why-co-viewing-is-important-tips-to-share-screen-time-with-your-kids.aspx) Parents cannot co-view every minute. The goal is regular involvement, not constant supervision from directly beside the child.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Help Digital Experiences Move Into Real Life
Young children learn best when screen experiences connect with physical activity, conversation and play. After a programme, you might:

items: look for a related object outdoors;
make something shown in the programme;
or talk briefly about what happened.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: If the child watches animals building a home, provide cushions or boxes for pretend play. If the child sees a dance, stand up and try it together. The screen becomes a beginning rather than the whole experience.
Ending Screen Time
Stopping is often the most difficult part. A child may cry even when the limit was clear. That does not automatically mean the boundary is wrong.
Before Screen Time Begins
State the limit:
“You may watch one episode. When it finishes, the tablet goes on the shelf.”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Choose content with a clear ending whenever possible.
Before It Ends
Give one brief warning:
“Five minutes remain.”
or:
“This is the last song.”
Warnings help some children. Others become more anxious when repeatedly reminded. Notice what works.
At the End
Use the same short message:
“The episode is finished. The tablet goes away.”
Then state what comes next:
“Now we wash hands for dinner.”
If the Child Protests

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Acknowledge without changing the limit:
“You wanted another one. It is hard to stop.”
“I won’t let you hit. The tablet is finished.”
Avoid restarting the programme to stop the crying. That may teach the child that escalation changes the boundary.
Do Not Snatch Without Warning—Except for Safety
Taking a device suddenly can intensify panic and conflict. Use warnings and predictable endings when possible. However, immediate removal is appropriate when:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: the child is contacting an unknown person;
or another urgent safety concern exists.

blocks.text: Say:
“I’m taking the device because this is not safe. I will explain when we are calm.”
Safety comes first.
When the Child Has a Screen Meltdown
Stay calm and protect everyone.
“You are very angry that screen time ended.”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: “I won’t let you throw the phone.”
“You may cry. The screen is still finished.”
Do not lecture about technology during the meltdown. After the child is calm, review:

items: Was the child tired or hungry?
Did autoplay remove the natural stopping point?
Was the screen used for too long?
Does this type of content consistently produce difficulty?

blocks.text: The answer may be to shorten, change or pause access—not simply to become louder at the end.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Screens as a Calming Tool
A screen can quiet a distressed child quickly. Sometimes that is genuinely useful:

items: or when a parent needs to complete an essential task.

blocks.text: But if screens become the only way the child can tolerate boredom, waiting, sadness or frustration, other coping skills receive less practice. Build additional options:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: or simply being bored for a manageable period.

blocks.text: The goal is not never to use a screen for relief. It is to make sure the family has more than one tool.
Screens During Meals

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Screens may help a child remain seated or accept spoon-feeding, but they can also reduce awareness of eating and fullness. If screens are established at every meal, change gradually:

items: Turn the device off for the first few minutes of a meal.
Replace video with conversation or quiet audio.
Seek feeding support if intake becomes a serious concern.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Do not abruptly remove the child’s only feeding support when there are growth, swallowing or severe selective-eating concerns without professional guidance.
Screens and Sleep
Protect the period before sleep from highly stimulating media. Helpful boundaries include:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: charging devices outside the child’s sleep area;
avoiding frightening or fast-paced content near bedtime;
and replacing screen use with a repeatable wind-down routine.

blocks.text: A device should not remain beside a young child overnight with unrestricted internet access.
Video Calls Are Different—but Still Need Support

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Video calls can help children maintain relationships with grandparents, travelling parents and distant relatives. Young children may not sit and converse for long. Make calls more engaging:

items: or let the child move in and out of the conversation.

blocks.text: Do not force the child to perform:
“Recite the poem for everyone.”
“Say something clever.”
Connection is the purpose—not presentation.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Online Privacy Begins Early
Young children may not understand how digital information travels or remains available. Parents must protect:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: UNICEF recommends reviewing device permissions, limiting unnecessary access to location, photographs, cameras and microphones, and using age-appropriate privacy settings and parental controls. [UNICEF’s online privacy checklist](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/child-care/online-privacy) Before installing an app, check:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: Does it need the microphone or camera?
Can data or recordings be shared?

blocks.text: If an app requests information unrelated to its purpose, reconsider using it.
Teach Simple Safety Rules
A child between two and seven will not manage online safety independently. Use clear rules:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: “Only adults enter passwords.”
“Do not press a purchase button.”
“Tell me if a picture or video frightens you.”
“Do not talk to someone we do not know.”
“Your name, school and address stay private.”
“If something strange happens, you will not be in trouble for telling me.”

That final message is essential. Children may hide an online problem if they expect the device to be taken away immediately or believe they will be blamed. Safety grows when the child can ask for help.
Cameras, Photographs and “Sharenting”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Parents often share children’s photographs with affection and pride. Before posting, consider:

items: Is the child undressed, distressed, unwell or embarrassed?
Does the image show the school, home address or regular location?
Is the child old enough to express a preference?
Could the child reasonably object later?
Who can download or forward it?
Does the post reveal personal medical or behavioural information?

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Young children cannot fully understand or consent to a permanent digital record. UNICEF advises parents to think carefully about children’s privacy and the long-term implications of sharing their images and personal lives online. [UNICEF](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/child-care/sharenting) Build the habit of asking:
“May I take your picture?”
“May I send this to Grandma?”
A young child’s answer is not legally decisive in every situation, but respecting it teaches that their image and dignity matter. Never post:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: A child’s difficult moment is not public entertainment.
Advertising and In-App Purchases
Young children may not understand that advertisements are designed to influence them. Explain simply:
“This is an advertisement. It wants us to want something.”
“The game says we need to buy that, but we do not.”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Disable or password-protect purchases. Do not store payment information on a child’s unrestricted profile where possible. Be cautious of:

items: and games that interrupt play until the child watches an advertisement.

blocks.text: A child should not be responsible for navigating commercial pressure alone.
Parental Controls Help—but Do Not Replace Parenting
Parental controls can help:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: But no control is perfect. Settings change. Children discover new routes. Content can be mislabelled. Use controls as one layer alongside:

items: and a relationship in which the child can report problems.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: The US Federal Trade Commission provides an overview of the types of controls available and the importance of explaining family rules to children. [FTC Consumer Advice](https://consumer.ftc.gov/articles/how-use-parental-controls-keep-your-kid-safer-online)
Adults Are Part of the Media Environment
Children notice when adults:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: or become distressed when separated from their own screens.

blocks.text: Parents do not need to model perfection. Acknowledge what you are doing:
“I’m using the phone to pay the electricity bill.”
“I’m reading a school message. Then I’ll put it away.”
“I’ve been scrolling for too long. I’m stopping now.”
Create shared boundaries where possible:
“Everyone’s phones stay away during dinner.”

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Children accept limits more readily when the rule reflects a family value rather than a punishment designed only for them.
When Screens Are Necessary for the Parent
Sometimes a parent needs the child safely occupied. There may be:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Do not let unrealistic advice turn necessary screen use into shame. Instead, make it more intentional:

items: use a clear beginning and ending;
protect sleep and meals when possible;
and offer movement or connection afterwards.

blocks.text: A screen used thoughtfully during a demanding hour does not define the whole relationship.
Common Digital-Habit Mistakes

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: 1. Giving the Device Without Explaining the End
State the limit before access begins.
2. Allowing Open, Unsupervised Browsing
Young children need restricted content and adult oversight.
3. Using Screens for Every Difficult Feeling
Help the child build other ways to wait, rest and recover.
4. Threatening to Remove Screens for Weeks
Use consequences you can realistically maintain.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: 5. Restarting Content After a Meltdown
Hold the original boundary while supporting the emotion.
6. Focusing Only on Time
Content, context, privacy, sleep and what media replaces all matter.
7. Assuming “Educational” Means Safe
Review the actual app, programme and data practices.
8. Posting Without Considering the Child’s Dignity

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: A private family moment does not automatically belong online.
9. Expecting a Young Child to Self-Regulate an Endless Feed
Design the environment. Do not rely on undeveloped impulse control.
10. Applying Rules Adults Never Follow
Build some shared device-free spaces.
Red Flags
Take action if:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: an unknown person contacts the child;
an app requests private images or personal information;
the child encounters sexual, violent or frightening content;
the child is asked to keep an online interaction secret;

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: the child is pressured to move to another platform or private chat;
screen use is seriously disrupting sleep, eating, school or relationships;
the child becomes persistently distressed, secretive or fearful after going online;
an adult shares humiliating or intimate images of the child;
or a device is being used to threaten, exploit or monitor someone in the family.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Preserve relevant information when safe to do so, block contact, report the account or content through the platform and seek appropriate local support. If a child may have been sexually exploited, threatened or placed in immediate danger, contact the relevant child-protection or law-enforcement service promptly. Do not blame the child.
Your Family Media Plan
What Screens Are For in Our Family

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Screen Times That Usually Work
Screen-Free Times or Places

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Approved Content
How Screen Time Ends
Where Devices Are Stored or Charged

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Who Controls Passwords and Purchases
What the Child Should Do if Something Frightens Them
A Content Check
Before approving an app, game or programme, ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: Is it appropriate for my child’s age and development?
Does it collect location, voice, images or contacts?
Can the child leave the app for open internet access?
Have I watched or used it myself?
How does my child behave during and after using it?

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: A Screen-Ending Script
Before:
“You may watch one episode. When it ends, the device goes away.”
Near the end:
“This is the last part.”
At the end:
“The episode is finished. The device goes on the shelf.”
If the child cries:
“You wanted more. It is hard to stop. The screen is still finished.”
If the child grabs or throws:
“I won’t let you damage the device or hurt anyone. I’m moving it somewhere safe.”
Then help the child transition to the next part of the day.
Remember This

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: The goal is not to raise a child who never uses technology.
The goal is to raise a child who gradually learns that technology has a place—but not every place—in a healthy life. Young children cannot manage that balance alone. They need adults to design it.
Try This Today
Make one small digital change. Choose only one:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: remove one device from the bedroom;
watch one programme with your child;
or write down the sentence you will use when screen time ends.

blocks.text: A family media culture is not changed through one angry confiscation. It changes through clear, repeatable habits.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Screens often enter family life when someone needs help. A tired parent needs to cook. A child needs to wait. A grandparent wants to see a face from far away. A rainy afternoon stretches too long. There is no need to treat technology as either a villain or a substitute parent. Use it with purpose. Know what your child is seeing. Protect sleep, movement, conversation and privacy. Expect that stopping may be difficult. Then keep teaching.

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: The child will eventually enter a digital world you cannot completely control. What you can build now is something more durable: A child who knows limits exist. A child who understands that private information deserves protection. A child who can leave a screen and return to life. And, most importantly, a child who knows they can bring anything frightening or confusing back to you.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: [World Health Organization — Guidelines on Physical Activity, Sedentary Behaviour and Sleep for Children Under Five](https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/9789241550536)

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: [UNICEF — What Parents Should Know About Sharing Children Online](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/child-care/sharenting)

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

blocks.text: Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by Indian child-development and online-safety professionals. The final edition should address:

items: shared family phones rather than individual devices;
video calls with travelling parents and relatives;
children consuming content in multiple Indian languages;

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: and children who use devices for communication or disability support.

blocks.text: The final resources could include:

pages.chapterTitle: Screens, Digital Habits and Online Safety

items: a guide to device settings and parental controls;
and current Indian child-safety reporting contacts verified immediately before publication.

blocks.text: Any platform-specific instructions should be checked again during final editing because settings, privacy practices and safety features change frequently.

pages.epigraph: “When children play, they are not taking a break from learning. Play is one of the main ways they learn.”

blocks.text: The Worksheet and the Cardboard Box

A father bought a set of early-learning workbooks for his five-year-old. The pages promised to develop numbers, letters, reasoning and school readiness. His daughter completed two pages and then wandered away. Nearby was the cardboard box in which the books had arrived. She turned it into a bus. She drew circles for wheels, counted passengers, made tickets, wrote marks that represented destinations and asked her brother where he wanted to go.
Later, the box became a shop. Then a house for a toy dog. Her father watched and realised that she had spent nearly an hour practising:

She had not avoided learning. She had entered it through play. Worksheets and direct teaching can have an appropriate place. But young children do not learn only when they are sitting quietly and producing an answer an adult already knows. They also learn when they explore, pretend, build, move, ask, fail, revise and try again.
What Children Learn While Playing
Play can support many areas of development at the same time.

rows: Balance, planning, spatial understanding and persistence
Counting, language, turn-taking and social roles
Hand control, representation, creativity and concentration
Memory, sound awareness, rhythm and language
Rules, turn-taking, counting and coping with losing
Sensory exploration, measurement and cause and effect

blocks.text: Children are often practising several skills at once. The goal does not need to be announced for learning to occur.
Play Is Not One Single Activity
Play takes many forms.
Physical Play
Running, jumping, dancing, balancing, chasing, throwing and climbing help children understand their bodies and physical capabilities.
Constructive Play
Children use materials to make something:
They plan, test and revise.
Pretend Play

A spoon becomes a microphone. A blanket becomes a tent. A child becomes a doctor, shopkeeper, teacher, animal or parent. Pretend play helps children explore roles, stories, feelings and possibilities.
Social Play

Children observe, play beside one another, share materials, create games and gradually cooperate. Young children may not begin with smooth teamwork. Arguing about roles is itself part of learning how to play socially.
Games With Rules
Simple board games, matching games, hide-and-seek and traditional group games help children practise memory, turn-taking, self-control and coping with outcomes.
Quiet and Solitary Play
Children also benefit from playing alone. Independent play can support concentration, imagination and self-direction. A child who is playing alone is not necessarily lonely.
Free Play, Guided Play and Direct Teaching
Children benefit from different kinds of learning experiences.
Free Play
The child chooses:
The adult creates a safe environment but does not control the direction.
Guided Play
The child still has meaningful choice, while the adult gently supports a learning idea. For example, the adult may provide containers and water, then wonder:
“Which container do you think will hold more?”
The child experiments rather than being shown the answer immediately.
Direct Teaching
The adult demonstrates or explains a specific skill. This may be useful for:

items: learning how to use scissors safely;

blocks.text: Good early learning does not require choosing one approach forever. The National Association for the Education of Young Children describes play as central to developmentally appropriate learning while recognising a continuum between child-directed play, guided experiences and intentional teaching. [NAEYC](https://www.naeyc.org/resources/position-statements/dap/principles) The balance should respect the child’s age, abilities, interests and need for active engagement.
Children Need Time to Go Deep
Adults sometimes interrupt play just as it becomes valuable. The child begins building. The structure falls. The child studies it, rebuilds and changes the base. Then the adult says:
“Time for another activity.”
Learning requires enough time to:

A schedule filled with many short “educational activities” may provide less sustained thinking than one long period with boxes, blocks or pretend materials. Protect some unhurried play when family life allows.
Boredom Is Not an Emergency
A child says:
“I’m bored.” The parent immediately feels responsible for producing entertainment. But boredom can be the uncomfortable space before an idea appears. You might respond:
“You don’t know what to do yet.”
“You may choose drawing, blocks or helping me.”
“I’m not organising an activity right now. I’m interested to see what you decide.”

The child may complain before beginning. That does not mean the parent must solve the feeling. Children need gradual practice initiating play without an adult or device providing every idea. This skill develops more easily when:

items: the number of options is manageable;
screens are not always the quickest alternative;
and the parent allows time for the child to begin.

blocks.text: You Do Not Need Expensive Toys
A toy’s cost does not determine its learning value. Open-ended materials can be used in many ways:
An electronic toy may entertain in one predetermined way. A cardboard tube may become:
The best material is often the one that leaves room for the child’s idea. Check all household and recycled materials for:
Create Invitations, Not Performances
Parents do not need to plan elaborate activities that look impressive in photographs. An invitation to play can be simple:

items: paper and three crayons on a table;
a bowl of water with cups;
or leaves with glue and paper.

blocks.text: Then let the child decide what happens. Avoid becoming disappointed when the child uses the materials differently from the plan. If you set out leaves for a collage and the child turns them into pretend food, the activity has not failed. The child found another idea.
How Adults Can Support Play
Observe Before Joining
Watch for a moment. Ask yourself:

items: What is the child trying to do?
Are they asking for help or simply concentrating?
Would my involvement expand the play or take it over?

blocks.text: Describe
“The bridge keeps falling when the truck reaches the middle.”
“You gave every animal a different home.”
Description shows attention without controlling the solution.
Wonder
“I wonder how the water could reach the other container.”
“I wonder what the shopkeeper will do when the fruit finishes.”
Wondering invites thought without turning it into a test.
Add One Useful Element
Offer tape, another box, a cloth or a new character. Then step back.
Follow the Child’s Story
If the child says the purple cow is driving a train, you do not need to correct the logic. Pretend play is allowed to be impossible.
Questions That Support Thinking
Questions can deepen play when used lightly. Helpful questions include:

items: “Would you like help or more time?”
“Can you tell me about your picture?”

blocks.text: But too many questions can make the child feel examined. Avoid turning play into a quiz:
“What colour is that?”
“How many do you have?”
“What letter does it begin with?”
“Are you sure?”
Use comments and silence too. A good conversation does not require the parent to test everything the child knows.
Let the Child Struggle Safely

When a puzzle piece does not fit, an adult may immediately turn it around. When a tower falls, the adult may rebuild it. When a child cannot open a container, the parent may take over. Quick help prevents frustration—but it also removes the chance to develop persistence. Pause. You might say:
“That is difficult.”
“You’ve tried it two ways.”
“Would you like a hint, help or another try?”
Intervene when:

items: the child is becoming completely overwhelmed;
the task is far beyond current ability;
or the child clearly asks for help.

blocks.text: Offer the smallest amount of help needed. Instead of completing the puzzle, rotate one piece. Instead of building the tower, hold the base. The message becomes:
“You can work through difficulty, and support is available.”
Mistakes Are Part of Learning

If adults correct every mistake immediately, children may become afraid to experiment. A child writes a letter backwards. A drawing has five legs. A block structure leans. Ask whether correction is necessary in that moment. Sometimes direct instruction is useful:
“Let me show you where your fingers go on the scissors.”
At other times, curiosity is better:
“Tell me about this part.”
A young child’s work is not always a product to improve. It may be evidence of thinking still in progress.
Learning Language Through Daily Life
Language learning does not require flashcards alone. Children develop language through:

items: and hearing words connected to real experiences.

blocks.text: During cooking:
“The dough is sticky. Now we are pressing and rolling it.”
During travel:
“That bus is crowded. The smaller car is beside it.”
During play:
“Your animal is hiding underneath the table.”
Use rich language without demanding repetition. If the child says:
“Dog runned away.”
you can respond naturally:
“Yes, the dog ran away very quickly.”
The child hears the correct form without being shamed.
Home Languages Are Learning Resources

Some families worry that speaking more than one language will confuse a child. Children can learn multiple languages, though their vocabulary may be distributed across them and their confidence may differ by setting. Speak the language in which you can offer:

Grandparents can share rhymes and stories in the family’s home language. Parents do not need to replace a rich home language with limited, anxious English in order to support learning. If there are concerns about language development, assessment should consider all the languages the child hears and uses. A genuine communication difficulty does not disappear when only one language is counted.
Early Literacy Without Pressure
Early literacy begins before formal reading. It includes:

items: and watching adults use reading and writing in real life.

blocks.text: Helpful experiences include:

items: allowing the child to turn pages;
labelling the child’s drawing with their words;

blocks.text: Do not require the child to read every word before enjoying the story. Reading together should not become a daily examination.
Early Mathematics Is Everywhere
Young children encounter mathematics when they:

items: or work out whether everyone has a turn.

blocks.text: Use mathematical words naturally:
Instead of announcing a formal lesson, invite participation:
“We need five spoons. Can you help?”
“Which container holds more water?”
“There are four pieces and three people. What could we do?”
The child does not need to produce the correct answer immediately for the experience to matter.
Art Is More Than a Perfect Craft

Adult-led craft often asks every child to create the same final object. The adult cuts the pieces. The child glues them in the “correct” place. The result looks attractive—but the child makes few meaningful decisions. Process-focused art offers:
and allows the child to explore. Avoid asking:
“What is it?”
as though art must represent something recognisable. Try:
“Tell me about what you made.”
or:
“I notice strong lines and many colours.”
The child may have created a story, a feeling, an experiment—or nothing that needs a name.
Music and Movement
Children learn through rhythm, repetition and movement. Music can support:
Families can:
Do not reserve music only for children considered talented. Musical play belongs to everyone.
Outdoor Play
Outdoor environments offer experiences difficult to reproduce indoors:
A child can:

items: or invent games with other children.

blocks.text: Outdoor play does not require a formal playground. It may happen in:
Assess traffic, water, animals, heat, air quality and local safety. Give the child challenge without avoidable danger.
Risk and Safety in Play

Children need manageable physical challenges to develop judgement. They may climb, balance, jump and test what their bodies can do. Removing every challenge can limit learning. Ignoring serious hazards is equally unhelpful. Distinguish between:
A Risk the Child Can Learn to Assess

items: or carrying a light breakable-looking but safe object.

blocks.text: A Hazard the Child May Not Recognise
Stay close enough to protect without directing every movement. You might say:
“Notice where your foot will go next.”
rather than:
“You’ll fall! Get down immediately!”
unless immediate danger truly requires it.
Playing With Other Children
Children do not begin cooperative play with adult-level social skills. They may:

items: play beside rather than with another child;
or cry when an idea is rejected.

blocks.text: These moments require guidance.
“Both of you want the truck.”
“She said she doesn’t want to play that game.”
“You may ask him to join. You may not force him.”
“What could each person do in your shop?”
Do not solve every disagreement instantly. Give children a chance to suggest solutions when everyone is safe enough to think.
Siblings and Play

Parents often hope siblings will entertain each other peacefully. Sometimes they will. At other times, differences in age, power and temperament make play difficult. Protect the younger or less powerful child from being controlled or hurt. Avoid expecting the older child to become a constant unpaid caregiver. Say:
“You may choose to play together, but your sister is allowed to say no.”
“You can build beside him without taking over his structure.”
“The game must be safe for both of you.”
Shared play should remain a relationship—not an obligation placed entirely on one child.
Gender and Play
Children benefit from access to a wide range of play. Do not limit:

items: dolls and caregiving play to girls;
building and science materials to boys;
art and emotional expression to girls;
or particular colours to one gender.

blocks.text: A child caring for a doll practises empathy and daily-life skills. A child building a machine practises planning and spatial reasoning. Both experiences are valuable for every child. UNICEF encourages families to notice and reduce gender stereotypes in play so children can explore a fuller range of interests and abilities. [UNICEF](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/child-development/how-remove-gender-stereotypes-playtime)
When the Child Repeats the Same Play
Children often repeat an activity because repetition supports mastery. They build the same tower. Ask for the same story. Pretend to be the same animal. Repeating play can help the child:

Repetition is not automatically a lack of imagination. However, if play is extremely restricted, causes distress when interrupted, involves little flexibility or appears alongside other developmental concerns, discuss the overall pattern with a qualified professional. One behaviour should not be interpreted in isolation.
Play After a Difficult Experience
Children sometimes replay:
A toy may receive an injection repeatedly. A doll may be sent away. A block house may fall again and again. Do not assume the play has one obvious meaning. Observe. You might say:
“The toy is going through that experience many times.”

Allow the child to lead unless the play becomes unsafe. Seek professional support if the child is persistently distressed, the play is highly frightening or repetitive after trauma, or there are broader changes in sleep, behaviour or functioning.
When a Child Says, “Play With Me”
Parents cannot always play. A truthful answer is better than resentful participation:
“I can play for ten minutes after I finish this task.”
“I can help you begin, but then I need to cook.”
“I’m not available to play now. You may play near me.”

When you do join, offer genuine attention for a manageable period. Children benefit from parent play, peer play, sibling play and independent play. No parent must become a full-time entertainment service.
When a Child Does Not Seem to Play
Some children:

items: or use materials in ways adults do not expect.

blocks.text: Begin with the child’s interest. If the child likes spinning wheels, sit nearby and imitate once. If they arrange objects, describe the pattern. If they prefer movement, join a simple physical game. Do not force elaborate pretend play. Consult a paediatrician or developmental professional if you have broader concerns about:
Support should build from the child’s strengths.
Common Learning-and-Play Mistakes
1. Turning Every Activity Into a Lesson
Children need experiences not controlled by adult outcomes.
2. Asking Constant Test Questions
Conversation supports learning better than continuous examination.
3. Correcting the Product
Focus on thinking, effort and exploration—not making the child’s work look adult-made.
4. Buying Too Many Toys
Too many options can scatter attention. Rotate a smaller number if helpful.
5. Interrupting Too Quickly
Allow time for deep play and repeated attempts.
6. Solving Every Problem
Offer the smallest useful amount of help.
7. Comparing Skills
One child reads early. Another builds complex structures. Development is not a race with one track.
8. Treating Mess as Misbehaviour
Set clear boundaries and involve children in cleanup, but recognise that exploration can be untidy.
9. Overscheduling
A full calendar can leave too little time for self-directed play.
10. Believing Play Must Look Academic to Be Valuable
Movement, imagination, relationships and sensory exploration are also learning.
Red Flags
Seek professional guidance if a child:

items: loses previously acquired language, movement, play or social skills;
shows persistent difficulty seeing, hearing, moving or using their hands;
rarely communicates needs or interests in ways others can understand;
shows little awareness of or response to people across settings;
cannot participate in play because of intense distress;
has persistent difficulties that affect preschool, school or daily life;
or has a pattern that concerns caregivers, teachers or healthcare professionals.

blocks.text: Development varies, but losing skills always deserves prompt attention. Do not wait for a child to “grow out of” a significant concern without discussing it with an appropriate professional.
Your Child’s Play Map
My Child Often Chooses
Strengths I Notice
A Type of Play We Could Make More Available
Materials We Already Have
One Time of Day That Could Hold Unhurried Play
One Way I Can Support Without Taking Over
A Low-Cost Play Basket
Choose several safe, open-ended materials:

items: Leaves, stones or shells large enough to be safe

blocks.text: Do not provide everything at once. A small, changing selection may invite deeper attention.
Five Useful Adult Responses
When the child is playing, try:

items: “Would you like help, a hint or more time?”
“You kept working even when it fell.”

blocks.text: These responses value the process rather than demanding a perfect result.
Remember This
School readiness is not built only through early worksheets, memorised answers or sitting still. It is also built through:
Play gives children a place to practise all of these.
Try This Today
Offer your child one ordinary material:
Do not explain what to make. Say:
“I wonder what this could become.”
Then wait. Observe before helping. Follow the child’s idea for ten minutes without correcting, testing or improving it. You may discover how much learning was already waiting inside something simple.
From Mom’s Village

Parents are often told that childhood is a race. Read earlier. Count faster. Write more neatly. Join another class. Do not fall behind. But childhood also needs time that appears unproductive from the outside. Time to build something that falls. Time to repeat a story. Time to make rules, change them and argue about them. Time to stare at an insect. Time to become bored enough to invent. Learning is not always visible on a page.

Sometimes it looks like a cardboard bus with uneven wheels and an impossible destination. Respect that work. It is helping your child become not only someone who knows answers—but someone willing to wonder, explore and create.
Authoritative References
Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by Indian early-childhood educators and developmental professionals. The final edition should represent:

items: children in small or shared homes;
indoor play during extreme heat, rain or poor air quality;
traditional Indian games, songs, rhymes and storytelling;
children living in rural, urban and informal-settlement environments;
and families with limited access to toys or safe outdoor spaces.

blocks.text: Suggested final resources include:

items: an illustrated map of different kinds of play;
and a “play versus performance” comparison page.

blocks.text: Any developmental milestone material added during editing should be reviewed against current Indian paediatric guidance and should clearly state that milestones guide observation rather than diagnose an individual child.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence
pages.epigraph: “School readiness is not knowing everything before the first day. It is having enough security, curiosity and support to begin learning in a new place.”

blocks.text: The First Morning

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: On the first morning of preschool, a four-year-old wore his new uniform proudly. He carried his bag to the car and talked excitedly about the toys he might find. Then they reached the classroom door. The teacher smiled and held out her hand. The child froze. He wrapped both arms around his mother’s leg and began to cry. His mother felt everyone watching. Other children seemed to walk inside confidently. She wondered whether she had failed to prepare him.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: “See? There’s nothing to cry about,” she said, trying to sound cheerful. But there was something to cry about. He was entering an unfamiliar place, surrounded by new people, while the person who represented safety was preparing to leave. His tears did not mean he was unready for school. They meant the separation mattered. The teacher crouched nearby. His mother gave him the familiar two kisses they had practised, said she would return after lunch and left.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: She cried in the car. He cried for several more minutes. Then he noticed a tray of toy animals. Both of them had done something difficult. That is often what starting school looks like—not an absence of fear, but a supported step through it.
What School Readiness Really Means
Families are often told that a child must know:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: before school begins. These skills can be useful, but they are only one part of readiness. A child also benefits from being able, with developmentally appropriate help, to:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: and relate to other children and adults.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: The American Academy of Pediatrics emphasises that social, emotional and behavioural capacities are as important to school success as early academic knowledge. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/preschool/Pages/Is-Your-Child-Ready-for-School.aspx) Readiness is not located entirely inside the child. Families, schools and communities also have a responsibility to be ready for the child. A developmentally appropriate school asks:
“How can we support the children who are arriving?”
not only:
“Why can’t every child already manage our expectations?”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Five Broad Areas of Readiness
1. Physical Wellbeing
This includes:

items: and the stamina required for the school day.

blocks.text: 2. Social and Emotional Development
The child is learning to:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: 3. Language and Communication
The child needs a way to:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Speech is not the only valid form of communication. Some children use gestures, signs, pictures or communication devices.
4. Approaches to Learning
These include:
5. Early Thinking and Knowledge
Children gradually build:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: These areas develop together. A child who cannot yet write their name may still bring rich language, creativity, kindness and problem-solving to the classroom.
Choosing a Preschool or School

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Families do not always have many options. Cost, location, admission rules, transport and availability may determine the choice. When choices exist, look beyond attractive buildings and promises of early academic achievement. Consider:
The Emotional Climate

items: Do adults speak respectfully to children?
Do teachers appear warm and attentive?
Are children allowed to ask for help?

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: The Approach to Learning

items: Is there meaningful time for play?
Are young children expected to sit for long periods?
Are stories, movement, art and conversation included?

blocks.text: Discipline

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: How does the school respond to hitting, crying, accidents or refusal?
Are children shouted at, shamed, isolated or physically punished?
Are families informed about serious incidents?

blocks.text: Safety

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: Are toilets supervised while preserving privacy?
How are allergies and medicines handled?
Are staff trained in first aid and child protection?

blocks.text: Inclusion

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: Can the school support differences in language, development, disability, health and learning?
Will staff collaborate with the child’s specialists when appropriate?
Does the environment allow reasonable adjustments?

blocks.text: Communication With Families

items: Can parents ask questions without fear?
Does the school discuss the child respectfully?

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: No school will be perfect. Look for safety, respect, developmentally appropriate expectations and willingness to work with families.
Questions to Ask the School
You might ask:

items: What does a typical day look like?
How much time do children spend in free and guided play?
How do you support children who cry at separation?
How do you respond to difficult behaviour?

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: What happens after a toileting accident?
How are meals and snacks handled?
How do you communicate with families?
How are children released at pickup?
What support is available for developmental or medical needs?
How do you handle bullying or repeated peer conflict?

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: Are teachers trained in first aid?
How do you manage allergies and medicines?
What should a child bring each day?

blocks.text: The school’s attitude while answering may tell you almost as much as the answers themselves.
Preparing Before the First Day
Visit if Possible
Let the child see:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: and the adult who may receive them.

blocks.text: UNICEF recommends familiarising children with the school environment before the first day when possible. [UNICEF](https://www.unicef.org/indonesia/id/kampung-pengasuhan/menyiapkan-anak-memasuki-dunia-prasekolah) If an in-person visit is unavailable, use:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Explain the Day in Child-Sized Terms
Avoid vague promises:
“You’ll love every minute.”
Instead say:
“I will take you to the classroom. Your teacher will help you. You will play, eat your snack and have story time. I will return after lunch.”
Young children understand events better than clock time. Use:

items: or another event the child can recognise.

blocks.text: Practise Everyday Skills

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Without turning home into a training camp, practise:

items: and telling an adult when help is needed.

blocks.text: Read and Play About School
Use dolls or toys to act out:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Let the child decide what happens in the story. Their play may reveal questions they have not expressed directly.
Do Not Threaten With School
Avoid:
“Your teacher will fix you.”
“At school nobody will tolerate this.”
“If you cry, the other children will laugh.”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: These statements turn school and teachers into threats before the child even arrives. Instead say:
“Your teacher is another adult who can help you.”
“Some things will feel new. You will learn the routine.”
“You can tell your teacher if you need something.”
School should represent growing independence supported by trustworthy adults—not punishment for childhood behaviour.
The Goodbye
A helpful goodbye is:
Create a small ritual:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: or placing a note in the bag.

blocks.text: Say clearly that you are leaving. Do not disappear while the child is distracted. Secretly leaving may avoid immediate tears, but it can teach the child to monitor the parent anxiously because departures happen without warning. A simple goodbye might be:
“It is time for me to leave. Your teacher will look after you. I will return after lunch. Two kisses, one wave. I love you.”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Then allow the teacher to help. The AAP recommends short, consistent goodbye rituals and returning when promised as ways of supporting separation. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/toddler/Pages/Soothing-Your-Childs-Separation-Anxiety.aspx)
When the Child Cries at Drop-Off
Crying does not automatically mean the school is harmful or the child is unready. Ask the teacher:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: How long does the distress last?
Do they eventually play, eat or participate?
Is the crying decreasing over time?
Does the child appear distressed throughout the day?
Are there specific people, places or routines linked to fear?

blocks.text: A child who cries for several minutes and then joins the day is having a different experience from a child who remains terrified, withdrawn or inconsolable for hours. Listen to the full pattern. Do not shame:
“You are the only one crying.”
Try:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: “Goodbyes are hard. Your teacher will help you, and I will come back.”
When the Parent Finds Separation Difficult
A parent may also feel:

items: or fear that the child no longer needs them.

blocks.text: These feelings are understandable. But the child should not become responsible for comforting the parent. Avoid:
“Mama will be so lonely without you.”
“Don’t have too much fun and forget me.”
Say:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: “I will miss you, and I’m glad you have this place to learn. I can handle saying goodbye.”
Find another adult with whom you can share your own tears and worries. The child needs permission to connect with the teacher without feeling disloyal.
What to Expect During Adjustment
Children adjust in different ways. Some cry immediately. Some appear confident for several days and then resist. Some hold themselves together at school and melt down at home. Some become:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: A demanding school day can use much of a young child’s regulatory capacity. After school, offer:
Do not fill every afternoon with classes while the child is still adapting.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Asking About the School Day
“How was school?” is a large question. A tired child may say: “Fine.” “Nothing.” “I don’t know.” Try smaller invitations:

items: “Would you like to tell me now or later?”

blocks.text: Do not interrogate. Some children talk best:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: or while playing beside the parent.

blocks.text: If the child does not want to talk, remain available.
Supporting Learning at Home
Home does not need to become a second school. Helpful support includes:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: a predictable homework space for older children;
and interest in what the child is learning.

blocks.text: Avoid immediately correcting every page. Ask:
“Tell me how you worked this out.”
“Which part felt easy?”
“Where would you like help?”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: A parent’s job is not to complete the work or protect the child from every mistake. It is to support effort, understanding and communication with the teacher when necessary.
Homework in the Early Years
Homework expectations vary. For young children, long periods of repetitive written work may crowd out sleep, play and family life. If homework regularly takes far longer than expected:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: break the task into smaller parts;
check whether the instructions are understood;

blocks.text: Do not complete the work to make it look perfect. Do not keep a distressed child working late into the night simply to avoid an unfinished page. Communicate honestly:
“She worked for twenty minutes and became too tired to continue. Could we discuss how to support this task?”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Praise Learning, Not Labels
Avoid labels such as:
“You are the smart one.”
“Maths is not your strength.”
“You are lazy.”
“Your sister was always better at reading.”
Labels can make children afraid to attempt something that may change how adults see them. Focus on process:
“You tried another way.”
“You asked for help.”
“You practised even when it was difficult.”
“You noticed your mistake and corrected it.”
This does not mean praising everything. It means giving useful feedback without defining the child’s identity through one performance.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Mistakes, Marks and Comparison
A child’s marks provide limited information. They may show:

items: how well they understood the instructions;
or where more teaching is needed.

blocks.text: They do not measure the whole child. When the child brings home disappointing work, begin with curiosity:
“What happened here?”
“Which part did you understand?”
“What support would help next time?”
Avoid:
“What will people think?”
“Your cousin scored higher.”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: “You have embarrassed us.”
Fear may produce short-term compliance while damaging honesty and willingness to seek help.
Building a Partnership With the Teacher
Parents and teachers see the child in different environments. The parent knows:
The teacher sees:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Both perspectives matter. When raising a concern:

items: Agree on one or two actions.
Choose a time to review progress.

blocks.text: For example:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: “She has recently cried every morning and says lunch feels frightening. What are you noticing during mealtime?”
or:
“He says he is always punished during group time. Could you help me understand what is happening?”
Respectful partnership does not require accepting unsafe or inappropriate practice. Parents may question, document and escalate serious concerns.
When a Teacher Raises a Concern
Hearing that your child:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: may feel painful. Try not to react with immediate denial or punishment. Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: Is the behaviour seen across settings?
Should hearing, vision, health or development be reviewed?

blocks.text: A concern is not automatically a diagnosis. But it deserves calm investigation. Avoid confronting the child with:
“Your teacher told me everything.”
Say:
“Your teacher says group time has been difficult. Tell me what it feels like for you.”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Inclusion and Additional Support
Some children need:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Support is not an unfair advantage. It is a way of giving the child access to learning. Families should share relevant health and developmental information with the appropriate school staff while protecting privacy. Create a written plan for:
when applicable. Never ask a young child to carry sole responsibility for explaining a serious medical condition.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Friendship in the Early School Years
Parents often ask:
“Did you make friends?”
Friendship develops gradually. A child may:

items: play with different children on different days;
or need adult support to enter play.

blocks.text: Help with specific skills:
“Can I play too?”
“Would you like to build with me?”
“I’m still using this. You can have it when I’m finished.”
“I don’t like that game.”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Do not force a friendship because the families know one another. Children are allowed to have preferences, while still being expected to behave respectfully.
Conflict, Exclusion and Bullying
Not every disagreement is bullying. Young children may:

items: or say hurtful things without understanding the full impact.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Bullying generally involves repeated harmful behaviour and a power imbalance. Take all reports seriously without deciding the full story immediately. Ask:

items: Does the child feel safe returning?

blocks.text: Tell the child:
“You did the right thing by telling me.”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Avoid advising physical retaliation as the first solution. Work with the school on supervision, safety and skill-building. Immediate action is needed when there are threats, injury, sexual behaviour, extortion, discriminatory harassment or serious fear.
School Refusal and Persistent Distress
Occasional reluctance can occur after holidays, illness or a difficult day. Persistent inability to attend requires attention. Possible contributors include:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: or another mental-health or developmental need.

blocks.text: Do not simply label the child stubborn. Ask:
“What is the hardest part of school?”
“When does the worry begin?”
“Where do you feel it in your body?”

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Work with the school and paediatric or mental-health professionals when distress is sustained or significantly disrupting attendance and family life.
Red Flags at School
