Take prompt action if your child reports or shows signs of:

items: being asked to keep secrets from parents;

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: repeated injuries without a clear explanation;
sexualised behaviour or knowledge that concerns you;
denial of toilet, water, food or necessary medicine;
unexplained major changes in sleep, mood or behaviour;

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Remain calm enough to listen. Do not repeatedly question the child in a leading way. Record the child’s words as accurately as possible and follow appropriate child-protection and legal procedures. The child’s immediate safety comes before the school’s reputation or the family’s embarrassment.
Common School-Transition Mistakes
1. Promising the Child Will Not Cry
Say that feelings are manageable, not that they will not occur.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: 2. Leaving Secretly
Use an honest, predictable goodbye.
3. Lingering Indefinitely
Warmth and confidence are usually more helpful than repeatedly restarting the separation.
4. Threatening With the Teacher
Do not make school the punishment for behaviour at home.
5. Overscheduling After School
Allow recovery time, especially during adjustment.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: 6. Focusing Only on Academics
Social, emotional, physical and communication skills matter.
7. Completing the Child’s Work
Teachers need to see what the child can currently do.
8. Comparing Marks or Timelines
Comparison creates pressure without explaining what support is needed.
9. Dismissing the Child’s Concern
Investigate patterns while avoiding panic.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: 10. Treating the School as Always Right—or Always Wrong
Build partnership, ask for evidence and protect the child when necessary.
Your School-Readiness Map
My Child Can Currently

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: Eat or open part of the lunch independently
Spend some time with another trusted adult
Recover from small frustrations with support

blocks.text: Skills We Can Practise Gently

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Information the School Should Know
What My Child Is Excited About
What My Child Is Worried About
Our Goodbye Ritual
How My Child Will Recognise Pickup Time

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: A First-Week Plan
Before school:

items: Practise opening the lunchbox and bottle.

blocks.text: During the first week:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: Offer food, water and recovery after school.
Communicate with the teacher about persistent concerns.

blocks.text: Remember This
A child does not need to arrive at school knowing everything. They need adults who will:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Readiness is not perfection before entry. It is the supported ability to begin.
Try This Today
Ask your child:
“What do you think happens during a school day?”
Listen without correcting immediately. Then draw or arrange five simple pictures:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Let the child retell the sequence. End with the part they most need to know:
“You will be with trusted adults, and I will come back.”
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: The first day of school is often described as the child’s beginning. It is also a parental transition. You are trusting someone else with a part of your child’s day. Your child is discovering that safety can exist beyond home. There may be tears at the door. There may be tired afternoons and stories that arrive slowly. Do not measure the whole journey by the goodbye. Look for what grows over time: A hand that releases yours. A teacher’s name spoken with trust.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: A song brought home. A problem described. A friendship attempted. A child who learns that leaving and returning can both be safe. That is what school readiness looks like in real life—not fearlessness, but expanding confidence.
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence
Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by Indian early-childhood educators, school counsellors and child-protection professionals. The final edition should reflect:

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

items: Anganwadi, nursery, LKG, UKG and primary-school transitions;
varied state and school admission ages;
government, private, alternative and inclusive schools;
school buses, vans and parent pickup;
children cared for by grandparents or domestic caregivers;
and disability accommodations under applicable Indian frameworks.

pages.chapterTitle: Starting Preschool and School with Confidence

blocks.text: Suggested final resources include:

Legal statements about school admission, inclusion, corporal punishment, safeguarding and reporting should be checked against current Indian national and state requirements immediately before publication.

pages.epigraph: “Children are not born knowing how to be friends. Friendship is learned through belonging, disagreement, repair and many imperfect attempts.”

blocks.text: The Birthday Party
A five-year-old returned from a birthday party unusually quiet. His mother asked whether he had enjoyed himself. He shrugged. Later, while changing his clothes, he said:

“They wouldn’t let me play.” His mother felt an immediate rush of anger. She wanted to call the other parents, question the children and make sure her son was never excluded again. Instead, she paused. “What happened?” she asked. “They were playing superheroes. I said I would be the tiger, but they said there was no tiger.” “Then what did you do?” “I pushed their building.” The story was not as simple as one innocent child and several unkind ones.
Her son had felt excluded. He had also responded by destroying the game. Both parts needed attention. Children’s friendships are full of moments like this:
Parents cannot prevent every social disappointment. We can help children develop the words, judgement and confidence to move through them.
Friendship Develops Gradually

A toddler may play beside another child without sharing a common plan. A preschooler may begin creating games with others but still struggle with turn-taking and flexibility. An early-primary child may form more stable friendships while remaining sensitive to rejection, fairness and group belonging. Social development does not follow one exact timeline. A child may be:

items: interested mainly in older or younger children;
or in need of more solitary recovery time.

blocks.text: A child does not need to be popular with everyone. They need opportunities to experience safe, respectful connection.
What Young Children Are Learning Socially
Friendship requires many separate skills:

items: coping when an idea is rejected;

blocks.text: These abilities are still developing between ages two and seven. Children therefore need guidance—not labels such as “selfish,” “bossy,” “shy” or “mean.”
Social Temperament Matters
Some children walk into a playground and begin talking immediately. Others stand beside the parent and observe for twenty minutes. Both may be socially capable. A slow-to-warm child often needs:

items: one approachable child rather than a large group;
and permission not to perform immediately.

blocks.text: Avoid:
“Why are you being shy?”
“Go and play. Everyone is looking at you.”
Try:
“You like to watch before joining.”
“Would you like me to walk closer with you?”
“We can ask that child what they are building.”
Similarly, a highly sociable child may need help noticing whether another child wants space.
“You are excited to play. Look at her body—she is moving away. Ask once, then give her room.”
Social confidence includes approaching others and respecting their response.
Helping a Child Join Play
“Can I play?” is useful, but it may not always work. A group already absorbed in a complicated game may say no without intending permanent rejection. Teach several ways to join.
Observe First
“What game are they playing?”
Move Nearby
The child can begin a related activity without taking over.
Offer a Role or Material
“Do you need someone to drive the ambulance?”
“I have another bucket. Can I help build?”
Ask a Specific Question
“Can I be the shopkeeper?”
Specific invitations may be easier for the group to answer.
Accept the Answer
If the children say no:
“That hurts. You may ask once more later, invite someone to another game or choose something else.”
Children should not be forced to include another child in every private moment. But adults should intervene when exclusion becomes repeatedly targeted, humiliating or based on prejudice or power.
Friendship Scripts to Practise
Young children often know what they feel but not what to say. Practise during calm moments:
Joining
“What are you playing?”
“Can I help?”
“May I have a role?”
Asking for a Turn
“Can I use it when you’re finished?”
“How many turns before mine?”
Protecting an Ongoing Activity
“I’m still using this.”
“Please don’t knock it down.”
Offering an Alternative
“You can use this one.”
“Let’s build another.”
Setting a Boundary
“I don’t like that.”
“Stop.”
“I’m going to tell an adult.”
Leaving
“I don’t want to play this game anymore.”
“I’m going somewhere else.”
Practising through dolls, puppets or role-play makes the words easier to retrieve during a real conflict.
Sharing Is a Developing Skill
Adults frequently tell young children:
“You must share.”
But sharing can mean several different things:

items: allowing another person to use it;

blocks.text: Be specific. Instead of:
“Share nicely.”
say:
“Rohan is using the truck. You may use it when his turn is finished.”
or:
“There are two children and one bowl of clay. Let’s divide it.”

The American Academy of Pediatrics describes sharing and cooperation as skills that develop across the preschool years and recommends helping children use words, exchange turns and find simple solutions. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/preschool/Pages/Social-Development-in-Preschoolers.aspx)
Children Do Not Need to Share Everything Immediately
Imagine an adult receiving a new phone and being told:
“Give it to that visitor. You need to share.”
We would understand the hesitation. Children may keep a few special belongings private. Before a play visit, help the child put away objects they are not ready to share. Then explain:
“The toys left in the play area are available for taking turns.”

This creates a clearer expectation. However, communal resources—such as school materials or playground equipment—require turn-taking. The child’s ownership does not remove every social responsibility, but neither should “sharing” mean surrendering every valued object on demand.
Waiting for a Turn
“Wait” is difficult when the child does not understand how long the wait will be. Use concrete supports:

items: or a visible place in a queue.

blocks.text: “She has two more pushes on the swing. Then it is your turn.”

If the current child is deeply engaged, rigid equal-time rules may interrupt meaningful play. Use judgement. Sometimes another similar toy or activity is a better solution. The goal is not to calculate perfect equality. It is to help children experience that their needs matter alongside someone else’s.
When Two Children Want the Same Thing
Begin by describing:
“Both of you want the red car.”
Protect safety:
“I won’t let either of you pull or hit.”
Then invite solutions:
“What could we do?”
Young children may need options:

items: or choose a different activity while waiting.

blocks.text: Do not automatically give the item to the child who cries loudest or the child who is younger. If one child already had it, preserve that turn where practical:
“Mira is using it. When she finishes, it will be available.”
This teaches that possession is not transferred through grabbing.
Conflict Is Not the Same as Bullying
Children’s ordinary conflict may include:

items: or an impulsive act between children with relatively equal power.

blocks.text: Bullying is generally a pattern of intentional or harmful behaviour involving repetition and a power imbalance. Power may come from:
Not every unkind moment is bullying. But every harmful moment deserves an appropriate response. Using the correct distinction helps adults avoid two mistakes:

items: dismissing a serious pattern as “just children”;
or labelling every disagreement as bullying before understanding it.

blocks.text: UNICEF describes bullying as a repeated pattern involving harmful intent and power. [UNICEF](https://www.unicef.org/lac/en/parenting-lac/security-protection/how-talk-your-children-about-bullying)
When Your Child Says, “Nobody Likes Me”
Do not immediately reply:
“That’s not true.”
The child is describing how the moment feels. Try:
“You felt very alone today.”
Then gather information:

items: “Is there someone you feel comfortable with?”

blocks.text: The child may be describing:

items: or a broader difficulty joining peers.

blocks.text: Listen before deciding.
When Your Child Is Excluded
Exclusion can be painful without always being malicious. Children are allowed to:

items: or say no to a particular activity.

blocks.text: But adults should intervene when exclusion becomes:

items: organised by a powerful child or group;
or intended to isolate and harm.

blocks.text: Help your child identify multiple options:
“You may ask to join another role.”
“You may invite one child to play something else.”
“You may tell the teacher this keeps happening.”
“You may leave a game that makes you feel unsafe.”
The goal is not to make every child become your child’s friend. It is to ensure safety, dignity and access to the social environment.
When Your Child Excludes Others
Avoid public shame:
“You are such a mean child.”
Instead address the behaviour.
“You may choose a small game with your friend. You may not gather everyone to laugh at Tara or tell others they are forbidden to speak to her.”
Help the child distinguish:

items: and using exclusion as a weapon.

blocks.text: Ask:
“What were you trying to make happen?”
“How do you think the other child was affected?”
“What can you do differently next time?”
Require repair where appropriate.
Aggression During Play
If children begin hitting, biting, kicking or throwing objects:

items: Hear each child separately if necessary.

blocks.text: Say:
“I won’t let you hit.”
“You were angry because the rule changed. Hitting is not safe.”
“Next time say, ‘I’m not playing if you change the rule.’”
Do not demand an immediate hug between children. The harmed child does not owe physical affection.
Apologies That Mean Something
A forced “sorry” can teach a child to say a word without understanding or repairing harm. Help the child recognise:

items: and what can be done now.

blocks.text: A repair might include:

items: or making a plan for next time.

blocks.text: You can guide:
“You pushed her and she fell. Check whether she is okay.”
“Her building was damaged. What could help repair it?”
An apology may follow when the child is ready:
“I’m sorry I pushed. I was angry, but pushing was not safe.”

The AAP notes that children need help understanding why they are apologising, not simply repeating the word. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/ages-stages/preschool/Pages/Social-Development-in-Preschoolers.aspx)
When Your Child Has Hurt Another Child
Parents may feel embarrassed and become defensive:
“My child would never do that.”
Or they may punish harshly to demonstrate that they are responsible parents. Neither response necessarily helps. Stay accountable and curious. Ask the adult:
Tell your child:
“I love you, and hurting someone is not acceptable. We need to understand what happened and make it safer.”
Look for:
Understanding the cause does not excuse the harm. It helps prevent repetition.
When Your Child Is Being Bullied
Begin with:
“I’m glad you told me.”
“This is not your fault.”
“We will work on this together.”
Avoid:
“Just ignore it.”
“Hit back harder.”
“Why didn’t you stand up for yourself?”
Document:
Contact the teacher or appropriate school leader. Ask for a plan addressing:
Do not arrange an angry confrontation between the children or families without considering safety and school procedures.
Teach Assertiveness, Not Blame
Depending on the situation, a child may practise:
“Stop. I don’t like that.”
“That is not okay.”
“I’m leaving.”
“I’m telling an adult.”

Assertiveness may help, but responsibility for stopping bullying does not belong solely to the child being targeted. Adults must create safety. A child who freezes, cries or cannot speak has not caused the bullying.
Warning Signs of Bullying or Serious Peer Difficulty
A child may not tell adults directly. Possible signs include:

items: or a significant change in learning or behaviour.

blocks.text: These signs can have many causes. They are reasons to talk and investigate—not proof by themselves. [StopBullying.gov](https://www.stopbullying.gov/bullying/warning-signs)
The Child Who Witnesses Harm
Children may observe another child being excluded, mocked or hurt. Teach safe bystander options:

items: invite the excluded child to join;

blocks.text: Do not ask a young child to physically confront a larger or aggressive group. Courage includes seeking help.
Playdates and Social Opportunities
Children need opportunities to practise friendship, but not every family can arrange formal playdates. Social opportunities may happen:
For a child who finds groups difficult, begin small:
Prepare the environment:

items: provide more than one appealing activity;
and end before exhaustion creates conflict.

blocks.text: Do Not Manage Every Friendship
Parents may be tempted to choose:

items: which conflict should end a friendship;
and what every child must do next.

blocks.text: Stay involved in safety and guidance, but leave room for the child’s preferences. Ask:
“How do you feel after spending time with this friend?”
“Can you be yourself with them?”
“What happens when you disagree?”
“Do both of you get a say?”
These questions gradually teach the child to evaluate relationships.
When Parents Are Friends but Children Are Not
Two parents may enjoy each other’s company while their children repeatedly clash. Do not force closeness for adult convenience. Arrange:
Say:
“You do not have to be close friends. You do need to behave safely and respectfully.”
Children should not carry responsibility for preserving an adult friendship.
Friendship and Difference
Children notice:
Silence does not prevent prejudice. If a child excludes someone because of a difference, respond clearly:
“We do not say she cannot play because of her skin colour.”
“His communication device helps him speak. It is not a toy.”
“Families can be organised in different ways.”
Provide books, stories and relationships that represent varied lives without making one child responsible for teaching everyone. Belonging should not require a child to hide who they are.
Common Friendship-Coaching Mistakes
1. Demanding Immediate Sharing
Teach turns and context rather than requiring instant surrender.
2. Solving Every Disagreement
Give children a chance to suggest solutions when everyone is safe.
3. Calling a Child Shy, Bossy or Mean
Describe the behaviour and teach the missing skill.
4. Forcing Apologies or Hugs
Support meaningful repair and bodily autonomy.
5. Dismissing Exclusion
Investigate whether it is an isolated event or a harmful pattern.
6. Calling Every Conflict Bullying
Understand repetition, harm and power.
7. Advising Immediate Retaliation
Teach safety, assertiveness and adult help.
8. Defending Your Child Before Hearing the Facts
Love and accountability can coexist.
9. Comparing Popularity
One secure friendship may be more meaningful than many superficial ones.
10. Publicly Discussing Social Problems
Protect the child’s privacy.
Red Flags
Act promptly if:

items: a child is repeatedly injured, threatened or humiliated;
money, food or belongings are being taken;
a child is forced to perform dangerous or degrading acts;
threats continue online or outside school;
the child talks about self-harm, running away or not wanting to live;
the child is afraid of serious retaliation for telling;
or adults responsible for safety repeatedly fail to respond.

blocks.text: Seek urgent mental-health or emergency support when a child may harm themselves or someone else. Do not promise complete secrecy. Say:
“I will only involve the people needed to help keep you safe.”
Your Child’s Friendship Map
My Child Seems Most Comfortable
Skills My Child Already Uses
Situations That Are Difficult
One Script We Can Practise
Adults the Child Can Ask for Help
A Safe Opportunity for Social Practice
A Conflict-Coaching Plan
When conflict occurs:

items: Protect: Stop hitting, threats or damage.
Describe: “Both of you want the same toy.”
Repair: Return, rebuild, help or apologise meaningfully.
Review: Practise what to say next time.

blocks.text: Not every small conflict requires all eight steps. Use the least adult control necessary for safety and learning.
Remember This
Social confidence does not mean being liked by everyone. It means gradually learning to:

items: and seek help when a relationship becomes unsafe.

blocks.text: Friendship is not one skill. It is a collection of skills practised through real relationships.
Try This Today
Choose one friendship phrase your child may need:
“Can I play?”
“I’m still using this.”
“Stop. I don’t like that.”
“Can I have a turn when you’re finished?”
“I need help.”
Act out a short situation with toys. Let your child practise both roles. Do not wait for the next conflict to discover whether the words are available.
From Mom’s Village

Friendship brings some of childhood’s greatest joy—and some of its first heartbreak. A friend moves away. A game changes. A child is left out. Someone says something cruel. Someone else returns the next day and asks to play again. We cannot stand between our children and every painful moment. Nor should we leave them alone with experiences they do not yet know how to understand. Stay near enough to notice. Listen without rushing to judgement.

Teach words where there were only tears or hitting. Protect when there is a pattern of harm. And remind your child of something friendship should never make them forget: They deserve respect. They must also offer it.
Authoritative References
Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by Indian early-childhood educators, school counsellors and child-protection professionals. The final edition should reflect:

items: large classrooms and mixed-age neighbourhood play;
pressure to share possessions with guests;
language, caste, religion, disability, gender and economic differences;
and the distinction between ordinary conflict, ragging, harassment and bullying under applicable school policies.

blocks.text: Suggested final resources include:

Any legal or reporting information about bullying, discrimination, school violence or child protection should be checked against current Indian national and state requirements immediately before publication.

pages.epigraph: “Siblings do not need to be best friends every day. They need a home in which each child’s safety, dignity and belonging are protected.”

blocks.text: The Broken Tower

Two brothers were building with blocks. The older child had spent nearly twenty minutes making a tall tower. The younger child reached over and knocked it down. The older brother pushed him. Both began screaming. Their mother rushed into the room. “You’re older,” she told the first child. “You should know better.” Then she turned to the younger one. “And you must share properly.” Neither child felt understood. The older child heard:
“What you built did not matter because you are older.” The younger child heard:

“Sharing means I should have access to whatever I want.” The mother had wanted to restore peace quickly. Instead, both children left believing the other one had been protected. A more useful response would have begun with safety and facts:
“I won’t let you push.”
“Your tower was knocked down, and you were furious.”
“You wanted the blocks, but knocking down his work was not safe or respectful.”

Now the parent could help one child repair the harm and the other express anger without using force. Sibling conflict rarely disappears because an adult announces who should have known better. Children need help understanding:
Sibling Relationships Contain Many Feelings

A child can love a sibling and still wish the sibling would disappear for an afternoon. They can enjoy playing together and become furious over one toy. They can protect each other outside the home and argue intensely inside it. Sibling relationships can contain:

These mixed feelings are not evidence that the relationship has failed. The goal is not to eliminate all rivalry. It is to keep rivalry from becoming a pattern of humiliation, violence or fixed family roles.
Why Siblings Fight
Common causes include:

items: and unresolved hurt from an earlier conflict.

blocks.text: Sometimes the visible argument is not the whole problem. The fight may appear to be about a pencil when the deeper feeling is:
“You always choose her.”
“He is allowed to do everything.”
“The baby gets all your attention.”
“I never get anything that is only mine.” Listening beneath the object can help the parent respond more effectively.
Fair Does Not Always Mean the Same
Children often say:
“That’s not fair!”
They may mean:

items: “I did not get the same thing.”

blocks.text: Fairness does not require identical treatment. A three-year-old and a seven-year-old may have different:
Explain simply:
“Your brother stays awake later because his body and school schedule are different. When you are older, your bedtime will be reviewed too.”
“The baby needs help eating. You can eat independently, and I will still spend time with you.”
Avoid using fairness to dismiss the feeling:
“Life isn’t fair. Stop complaining.”
Try:
“You wanted the same thing. Let me explain the decision.”
Not every decision will satisfy the child, but clear reasoning protects trust.
Equal Love Does Not Require Equal Minutes

A sick child may need more care. A baby may need frequent feeding. A child with homework may need help. Another child may have a medical appointment or emotional difficulty. Parents cannot divide every day into mathematically equal portions. What children need is confidence that their needs will not disappear simply because a sibling has needs too. You can say:
“Your sister needs me at the doctor today. You and I will have our walk tomorrow.”
“I’m feeding the baby now. Sit beside me and tell me about your drawing.”
“I cannot listen properly this minute. I will come to you after I finish changing him.”
Then return as promised whenever possible. Reliability matters more than exact equality.
Avoid Comparisons
Comparison is one of the quickest ways to turn differences into rivalry. Avoid:
“Why can’t you eat like your brother?”
“Your sister never gives me this much trouble.”
“He is the clever one.”
“She is the responsible child.”

Even positive labels create pressure. The “clever” child may fear making mistakes. The “responsible” child may feel unable to need help. The “difficult” child may begin living according to the role assigned. Describe each child individually:
“You remembered your bag today.”
“Your brother needs more help with this skill.”
“You approach new situations differently.”

HealthyChildren.org notes that siblings can be very different despite sharing a family and that each child’s temperament and experiences contribute to those differences. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/family-life/family-dynamics/Pages/brothers-and-sisters.aspx)
Protect Ownership
Not everything in a family must belong equally to everyone. Children benefit from understanding three categories.
Personal Belongings
These belong to one child. The owner may decide whether and when another child uses them, within reasonable family limits.
Shared Family Belongings
These require turns or agreed rules. Examples may include:
Special or Fragile Items
These may require adult permission or supervision. Clear categories prevent repeated arguments. Before a younger sibling enters the room, do not simply say:
“You must share all your toys.”
Help the older child put away a few special belongings, then identify what remains available for shared play. Ownership and generosity can coexist.
Privacy Is a Family Need
Children need some control over:
In crowded or shared homes, a private bedroom may not be possible. Privacy can still mean:

items: or allowing a child to play alone without a sibling joining.

blocks.text: Say:
“She wants to play alone for ten minutes. You may choose another activity.”
A sibling relationship should not remove the right to say no.
Older Does Not Mean Always Responsible
Older siblings are often expected to:
Age may bring greater capacity, but it does not erase the older child’s rights. Avoid:
“You’re older, so give it to him.”
Try:
“You had the toy first. You may finish your turn. Then we’ll help him wait.”
Older children can contribute to family care in developmentally appropriate ways, but they should not become substitute parents by default. They should not be made responsible for:

items: feeding or medication beyond their ability;

blocks.text: Helping is a family contribution. Parentification is an unfair transfer of adult responsibility.
Younger Does Not Mean Exempt
A younger child may understand less and need more help. But “They’re little” should not mean their behaviour never has limits. Say:
“He is still learning, and I will stop him from knocking down your work.”
“She may be younger, but she cannot bite you.”
Development explains why more support is needed. It does not require the older child to remain unprotected.
When to Let Siblings Work It Out
Parents do not need to intervene in every disagreement. You may observe when:

items: the power difference is not overwhelming;
and they appear capable of finding a solution.

blocks.text: You might say:
“I hear that you both want different games. Try to find a plan. I’m nearby if you need help.”
This gives children practice. Intervene when:

items: or the children clearly cannot resolve it.

blocks.text: The AAP notes that sibling conflict is normal and that the decision to intervene should consider age, temperament, maturity and safety. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/family-life/family-dynamics/Pages/Sibling-Synergy.aspx)
Do Not Become a Courtroom Judge
Parents often arrive after the conflict has begun. Each child says:
“They started it!”
It may be impossible to determine every detail. You do not always need a verdict before restoring safety. Say:
“I did not see how it started. I can see that the game is no longer safe.”
“The blocks are being put away until both of you are ready to use them without throwing.”
“I will listen to each of you separately.”
Focus on:
If serious harm occurred, investigate carefully. But minor conflicts do not require a full trial every time.
A Practical Sibling-Conflict Process
1. Stop Harm
“I won’t let you hit.”
Separate bodies or remove the dangerous object.
2. Calm the Situation
Do not demand problem-solving while everyone is screaming.
3. Describe Without Taking Sides
“You both reached for the same car.”
“The tower was knocked down, and then there was pushing.”
4. Hear Each Child
“Tell me what happened from your side.”
Children may remember the same event differently.
5. Name the Boundary
“Being angry is allowed. Hitting and destroying are not.”
6. Invite Solutions
“What needs to happen now?”
7. Support Repair
Repair may include:

items: or making a plan for next time.

blocks.text: 8. Practise the Missing Skill
“Say, ‘Please don’t touch my building.’”
“Ask for a turn instead of grabbing.”
Do Not Force Immediate Reconciliation
After a fight, adults often say:
“Hug each other.”
“Say sorry now.”
“You are siblings. You must love each other.”
A child may need space before reconnecting. Say:
“You don’t have to hug. You do need to keep each other safe.”
“When you are ready, we will decide how to repair the damage.”
Forgiveness cannot be commanded on schedule. Safety and respectful behaviour can be required.
Building Positive Experiences
Sibling relationships cannot be built only through conflict management. Create opportunities for shared pleasure without forcing togetherness. Examples include:

items: or helping prepare for a celebration.

blocks.text: Choose activities that reduce direct competition. Cooperative goals can help:
“Can both of you build a bridge long enough for this truck?”
“Let’s prepare the fruit together—one washes and one arranges.”
Do not turn every family activity into a contest.
Notice Cooperation
Parents often notice children only when conflict becomes loud. Call attention to ordinary cooperation:
“You moved your bag so your sister could sit.”
“Both of you solved that without shouting.”
“You waited while he finished.”
Avoid exaggerated praise that creates pressure:
“You are finally being a good brother.”
Describe the specific behaviour. This helps children recognise what cooperation looks like.
One-to-One Time
Individual time reassures children that the parental relationship does not exist only inside the sibling group. One-to-one time might be:

items: or ten minutes with the child’s chosen activity.

blocks.text: It does not have to be expensive or perfectly equal. Tell the other child:
“This is my time with your brother. You will have your time too.”
Do not make children compete for whose special time was longer or more exciting.
Welcoming a New Sibling
A new baby changes the older child’s life permanently. Prepare realistically:
Avoid promising:
“You’ll have a best friend.”
The older child may feel:
All are possible. Say:
“You can love the baby and still dislike how much has changed.”
Include the child where appropriate without forcing involvement:
“Would you like to choose the baby’s clothes or keep playing?”
UNICEF advises preparing children for practical changes and allowing time for adjustment when a new sibling arrives. [UNICEF](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/child-care/preparing-for-a-new-sibling)
Regression After a Baby Arrives
An older child may:

items: ask for help with tasks they had mastered;

blocks.text: These behaviours often communicate:
“Is there still room for me?” Respond without ridicule:
“You want help getting dressed today. I can help with the first part.”
Maintain reasonable routines while offering additional reassurance. Do not say:
“Stop acting like a baby.”
The child is not actually trying to become a baby. They are checking whether dependency is still allowed.
Stepsiblings and Blended Families
Children in blended families may face:

items: and grief for the previous family arrangement.

blocks.text: Do not demand instant love. Begin with:

items: and continued access to important existing relationships when safe.

blocks.text: Avoid forcing one child to call a stepparent or stepsibling by a title they are not ready to use. Relationships grow through trust, not declarations.
When One Child Has Additional Needs
A child with a disability, chronic illness or developmental need may require more appointments, supervision or accommodation. Siblings may feel:

items: or guilty for having easier opportunities.

blocks.text: Provide age-appropriate explanations while protecting medical privacy. Say:
“Your brother needs help communicating. That explains why we use pictures. It does not mean he is allowed to hurt you.”
Make room for the sibling’s feelings:
“It is okay to feel tired of waiting during appointments.”
Do not turn one child into a permanent helper or expect them never to complain. Seek sibling support when family demands are substantial.
When Rivalry Becomes a Pattern of Abuse
Not all sibling conflict is ordinary rivalry. Take action when there is:

items: sexual behaviour involving force, secrecy or developmental concern;

blocks.text: Do not dismiss serious behaviour with:
“Siblings fight.”
Separate children as necessary and seek qualified professional help. The harmed child’s safety comes before preserving the appearance of a close family.
Common Sibling-Parenting Mistakes
1. Comparing Children
Comparison turns differences into rank.
2. Always Blaming the Older Child
Greater maturity does not remove the right to protection and fairness.
3. Excusing the Younger Child
Development calls for more teaching, not absence of boundaries.
4. Forcing Constant Sharing
Protect personal belongings and teach turns for shared resources.
5. Requiring Immediate Hugs
Repair should respect bodily autonomy.
6. Investigating Every Minor Dispute
Give children opportunities to solve manageable conflict.
7. Ignoring Power Differences
A younger, smaller or disabled child may need active protection—but older children can also be harmed by younger siblings.
8. Treating All Attention as a Competition
Offer reliable individual connection.
9. Making Siblings Responsible for Each Other’s Emotions
A child may behave respectfully without being required to make the sibling happy.
10. Expecting Lifelong Closeness Now
A respectful childhood relationship is a worthy goal. Adult closeness cannot be guaranteed.
Red Flags
Seek professional help when:

items: one child is afraid to be alone with another;
threats, coercion or humiliation are repeated;
one child controls the other through fear;
sexualised behaviour involves force, secrecy, distress or a significant developmental difference;
animals or vulnerable family members are harmed;
a child’s belongings are repeatedly destroyed;
one child talks about running away, self-harm or harming the sibling;
conflict is linked to severe parental violence;
or adults cannot keep the children safe.

blocks.text: Do not require children to share a bedroom or remain unsupervised together when safety is in question. Seek urgent help when there is immediate danger.
Your Family Fairness Map
Personal Belongings
Each child may keep these private: ____________________________________
Shared Belongings
Shared Spaces
One-to-One Time
A realistic moment with each child: ____________________________________
Our Non-Negotiable Safety Rule
Words We Will Avoid

items: “You’re older, so you must always give in.”
“The baby doesn’t know better, so accept it.”

blocks.text: A Sibling-Conflict Script
“Stop. I won’t let either of you hurt the other.”
“I did not see how it began. I will listen to both of you.”
“You may feel angry. Hitting, threatening and breaking things are not allowed.”
“What needs to happen now?”
“What can each of you do differently next time?”
Use fewer words when children are highly distressed. Problem-solving can wait until bodies are calm.
Remember This
The goal is not to make siblings agree, share everything or enjoy each other constantly. The goal is to teach:

items: and confidence that every child belongs.

blocks.text: Affection grows more freely when it is not forced.
Try This Today
Listen for one comparison you often make—even a positive one. Replace it with an individual observation. Instead of:
“You are the calm child.”
say:
“You waited while I finished.”
Instead of:
“Your brother is better at drawing.”
say:
“You both make pictures in different ways.”
Give each child permission to become themselves without being assigned a position in relation to the other.
From Mom’s Village

Siblings may share parents, rooms, meals and memories. They do not share the exact same childhood. One remembers the new baby arriving. Another remembers always having an older sibling. One experiences a parent during a busy season. Another meets the same parent years later, changed by experience. We cannot make every moment equal. We can make the family message clear: There is room for each of you. You may be different. You may be angry.

You may need privacy. You must not harm one another. And nobody has to lose their place in order for someone else to be loved. That is the foundation from which a genuine sibling relationship can grow.
Authoritative References

items: [UNICEF — Preparing a Child for a New Sibling](https://www.unicef.org/parenting/child-care/preparing-for-a-new-sibling)

blocks.text: Editorial Review Note for the Indian Edition
Before publication, this chapter should be reviewed by Indian family therapists, child-development professionals and child-protection specialists. The final edition should reflect:

items: cousins raised within the same household;
shared bedrooms and limited private space;
older children caring for younger siblings;
gendered expectations around caregiving and household work;
children with disabilities or chronic illness;
migration and parents working away from home;
and inheritance or family-status language that affects sibling roles.

blocks.text: Suggested final resources include:
Any material addressing sexual behaviour, serious violence or child protection must be clinically and legally reviewed, with current Indian reporting and emergency resources added before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults
pages.epigraph: “We cannot promise children that nothing frightening will ever happen. We can teach them that their body matters, unsafe behaviour is never their fault and they can always come to us.”

blocks.text: The Hug at the Family Gathering

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: At a family gathering, a six-year-old moved behind her mother when a relative asked for a hug. “Don’t be rude,” her mother said. “Go and hug Uncle.” The child obeyed, but her body remained stiff. The mother had wanted to teach respect. Without realising it, she had taught a different lesson:
“An adult’s wish for affection matters more than my discomfort.” Children should learn courtesy—but courtesy does not require physical contact. A child can greet someone with:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: or a high-five they willingly choose.

blocks.text: Body safety begins in ordinary moments like this. When adults respect a child’s reasonable “no,” children learn that body boundaries are real—not rules that disappear whenever an older person feels offended.
The Five Essential Messages
Young children need simple, repeated messages.
1. Your Body Belongs to You

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: A child may say no to unwanted:
The parent may still need to carry, clean, examine or restrain a child briefly for genuine safety or healthcare reasons. Explain what is happening:
“You don’t want the injection. The doctor needs to give it to protect your health. I will stay with you.”
Body autonomy does not mean children make every safety decision. It means their feelings and dignity continue to matter.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: 2. Private Parts Are Private
Teach clear names for body parts, including genitals and bottom. Explain:
“The parts covered by underwear are private.”

There are limited situations when a trusted caregiver or healthcare professional may need to see or touch private parts—for cleaning, health or safety. The adult should explain why, involve the parent or appropriate caregiver and preserve the child’s dignity.
3. You Can Tell Me Anything
Say regularly:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: “You will not be in trouble for telling me about a touch, picture, conversation or secret that made you uncomfortable.”
Children may remain silent because they fear:
4. Unsafe Behaviour Is Never the Child’s Fault
A child cannot consent to sexual activity with an adult. A child may freeze, obey, feel confused or fail to say no. None of these responses makes the child responsible.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: 5. Keep Telling Until Someone Helps
Help the child identify several adults:
“If I am unavailable, tell Papa, Nani, your teacher or another adult on your safety list. Keep telling until someone listens.”
Surprises and Unsafe Secrets
Not every secret is dangerous. A temporary surprise has an ending:
“We are keeping the birthday gift quiet until Sunday.”
An unsafe secret is used to hide behaviour and may make the child feel frightened, ashamed or confused:
“Do not tell your parents.”

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: “This is our special game.”
“Your family will be angry if they find out.”
Teach:
“No one should ask you to keep a secret about touching, private parts, photographs or something that frightens you.”

The American Academy of Pediatrics recommends teaching children the difference between safe surprises and secrets that should be shared with a trusted adult. [HealthyChildren.org](https://www.healthychildren.org/English/safety-prevention/at-home/Pages/Sexual-Abuse.aspx)
Do Not Rely Only on “Stranger Danger”

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Children are often warned about strangers. But unsafe behaviour may come from:
This does not mean teaching children to fear everyone. It means judging behaviour rather than reputation. Say:
“Most adults want children to be safe. These body rules apply to everyone—even people we know and love.”

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Teach Consent in Everyday Life
Children learn consent through daily family behaviour.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

rows: “Ask first and wait for the answer.”
Explain what you will do and why
Ask before taking or sharing it

blocks.text: Avoid continuing tickling after the child says no simply because they are laughing. Laughter can be an automatic physical response. Stop, check and continue only if the child clearly wants more.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Body Safety Without Fear
Do not give frightening lectures describing every possible danger. Short conversations repeated over time work better. You might say:
“Your private parts belong to you.”
“No one should ask you to look at or touch their private parts.”
“No one should take pictures of your private parts.”
“If something feels confusing, tell me. You will not be in trouble.”

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Books, dolls and ordinary bathing or dressing routines can create natural opportunities for these conversations. The NSPCC’s child-safety guidance similarly teaches that private parts are private, the child’s body belongs to them and worries should be shared with a trusted adult. [NSPCC](https://www.nspcc.org.uk/keeping-children-safe/support-for-parents/pants-underwear-rule/)
Online Body Safety
Body-safety rules apply online too. Teach children:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: never take or send photographs of private parts;
never show their body on a video call because someone asks;
tell an adult if an image or message feels strange;
never move a conversation to a secret platform;
and do not share their name, school, location or passwords without parental permission.

blocks.text: Young children should not communicate online with unknown people without close adult supervision. Parental controls help, but they do not replace conversation and supervision.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: If Your Child Tells You Something
The first response matters.
Stay as Calm as You Can
Strong anger or panic may make the child stop talking.
Believe and Reassure
Say:
“I’m glad you told me.”
“This is not your fault.”
“You are not in trouble.”
“I will help keep you safe.”
Listen Without Interrogating
Use open invitations:
“Tell me what happened.”
“What happened next?”

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Avoid leading questions that suggest an answer.
Do Not Confront the Suspected Person in Front of the Child
Prioritise immediate safety and professional guidance.
Record the Child’s Words
Write down what the child said as accurately as possible, including the date and circumstances.
Seek Appropriate Help

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Contact relevant medical, child-protection or law-enforcement professionals according to the situation and local requirements. Do not repeatedly make the child retell the account to many relatives.
Signs That Deserve Attention
No single behaviour proves abuse. Possible warning signs can include:

items: sudden fear of a person or place;

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: sexual behaviour or knowledge that seems concerning for the child’s development;
pain, bleeding or irritation around private parts;
or a significant change in eating, toileting or mood.

blocks.text: These signs can have many explanations, but substantial or sudden changes deserve calm investigation and professional advice.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Common Mistakes
1. Forcing Affection
Respectful greeting does not require touching.
2. Teaching Only About Strangers
Safety rules must apply to known people too.
3. Asking the Child to Be Responsible for Preventing Abuse
Adults remain responsible for supervision, boundaries and protection.
4. Threatening the Child
Never say:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: “If anyone touches you, I’ll be furious.”
The child may fear your anger and remain silent.
5. Promising Complete Secrecy
Say:
“I will involve only the people needed to help keep you safe.”
6. Ignoring Boundary Violations Because the Adult Is Respected
Reputation does not replace safe behaviour.
7. Publicly Sharing Intimate Images
Toileting, bathing, medical and distressing moments belong to the child’s private life.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Adult Behaviour That Requires Attention
Be cautious when an adult or older child:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: insists on affection after the child refuses;
seeks unnecessary time alone with the child;
or makes the child fearful or unusually uncomfortable.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Do not wait for proof of abuse before changing an unsafe supervision arrangement. Safety precautions are allowed while concerns are investigated.
Your Child’s Safety Circle
Help your child identify at least three trusted adults.
Ask:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: Will they take the child seriously?
Do they know how to reach the parent or emergency help?

blocks.text: A trusted adult is not simply someone the family likes. It is someone likely to protect the child appropriately.
Five Sentences to Practise
Teach the child:
“No. I don’t want that.”
“Stop touching me.”
“I need to leave.”
“I’m telling my trusted adult.”
“Someone asked me to keep an unsafe secret.”

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Children may not be able to use these words during a frightening event. Practising them builds options—but failure to use them never makes harm the child’s fault.
Remember This
Body-safety education is not one frightening conversation. It is a family culture in which:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: secrets about unsafe behaviour are not kept;
and children know they can speak without being blamed.

blocks.text: Try This Today
Ask your child:
“If something made you feel worried or uncomfortable and I was not nearby, which grown-ups could you tell?”
Write or draw three people together. Then say:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: “You can always tell me—even if someone says I will be angry, even if you broke a rule and even if the person is someone we know.”
From Mom’s Village
We teach children to cross roads safely, but adults still supervise traffic. Body safety works the same way. The child needs knowledge and words. Adults must still:

items: and act when something feels unsafe.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: The most protective message is not:
“You must make sure nothing happens.”
It is:
“Your body matters. Unsafe behaviour is never your fault. You can tell me, and I will help you.”
Authoritative References

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults
Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Indian Law and Support Services

India’s Protection of Children from Sexual Offences (POCSO) Act, 2012 makes reporting mandatory for everyone, not only professionals. Any person who knows, or has reasonable cause to suspect, that a child has been sexually abused is legally required to report it (Section 19); failing to do so can itself be an offence (Section 21). You do not need to be certain, and you do not need proof—a reasonable concern is enough to report.
If your child tells you something, or you are worried:

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: Call Childline on 1098, a free, 24-hour helpline for children. In several states this service is now being merged with 112, India’s general emergency number—both should currently connect you to help, and 112 is being positioned as the single number going forward.

Go to the nearest police station, or ask for the Special Juvenile Police Unit—POCSO cases must be recorded and handled by police trained for child victims, in child-friendly language, and (wherever possible) by a woman officer.
Use the National Cyber Crime Reporting Portal (cybercrime.gov.in) or call 1930 if the concern involves an online image, message or contact.
Contact your child’s paediatrician for a safe, appropriate medical assessment—this should never be arranged through repeated informal examinations by relatives or unqualified adults.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

items: Every district has a Child Welfare Committee and a District Child Protection Unit, which can be reached through the local police or Childline for ongoing support.

blocks.text: This is general information current as of 2026, not legal advice. Procedures can vary by state, and school-specific safeguarding duties should be checked against your child’s school’s own POCSO policy, which every school is required to have.

pages.chapterTitle: Body Safety, Consent and Trusted Adults

blocks.text: Editorial note for review: Helpline numbers and procedures should be re-verified immediately before publication, as services and their integration (particularly the 1098/112 merger) continue to change.

pages.epigraph: “Children do not need every caregiver to be identical. They need the important adults in their lives to protect the same basic sense of safety.”

blocks.text: Two Different Answers

A six-year-old asked his father whether he could watch another programme. His father said no. The child immediately went to his grandmother. “Dadi, Papa said I can watch one more.” She switched on the television. When the father objected, she replied:

“You watched plenty of television as a child. Let him enjoy himself.” The conversation quickly became an argument about respect, authority and who understood the child best. The child watched quietly. He was learning something—but it was not about healthy screen habits. He was learning that adult disagreement could be used to change a decision. Differences between caregivers are normal. Parents, grandparents, stepparents and other caregivers bring different:
The goal is not perfect agreement. It is to prevent those differences from making the child responsible for adult conflict.
Consistency Does Not Mean Sameness
Children can understand:
“Grandfather tells longer bedtime stories.”
“At home, we eat at the table. At your cousin’s house, everyone sits on the floor.”
“Mama helps you calm down quietly. Papa likes to take a short walk with you.”
Different relationships may have different rituals. Greater agreement is needed around:
Adults may differ in style while sharing core boundaries.
Choose the Non-Negotiables
Trying to create one rule for every detail may cause unnecessary conflict. Choose a short list.

rows: Different ways of calming the child
Greeting with words, a wave or a chosen hug
Different programmes from the approved list

blocks.text: A family with ten clear priorities may function better than one with fifty rules nobody consistently follows.
Discuss Differences Away From the Child
Avoid arguing about discipline while the child waits to see which adult wins. Say:
“We see this differently. Let’s discuss it privately.”
If a decision is needed immediately, one adult can say:
“For now, the screen stays off. We will review the rule later.”
Do not ask the child:
“Who do you think is right?”
The child should not become judge, messenger or evidence in an adult disagreement.
Do Not Undermine Another Caregiver
Avoid:
“Your mother is too strict.”
“Don’t tell Papa.”
“Grandma lets you get away with everything.”
“Your father doesn’t understand children.”
These comments create divided loyalties. A child should not feel that loving one caregiver betrays another. If you disagree with a decision, address the adult directly.
Present a Changed Decision Honestly
Adults sometimes make a poor decision or receive new information. Changing your mind is not weakness. Say:
“We talked about it and decided you may attend the party.”
or:
“I said yes before checking with Mama. We have now realised the timing will not work.”
Avoid pretending the first adult never made the decision. This teaches that families can reconsider without blaming one another.
When One Parent Is More Permissive
The more permissive adult may fear:

items: or repeating the harshness of their own childhood.

blocks.text: Ask:
“Which feelings are hardest for you to tolerate when you hold a boundary?”
A child’s disappointment does not automatically mean the limit is harmful. The adult can remain warm:
“I know you wanted more. The answer is still no.”
When One Parent Is Stricter
The stricter adult may believe:
Ask:
“Is this rule protecting safety or mainly protecting adult control?”
Firm guidance does not require:
Respect grows more reliably when authority is calm, clear and accountable.
Grandparents and Extended Family
Grandparents may bring enormous love, practical support and family continuity. They may also use approaches that parents no longer want. Speak respectfully and specifically:
“We appreciate how much you care for her. We are teaching her that she may decline physical affection, so please offer a wave or high-five if she does not want a hug.”
“Please do not threaten to hit him. We use calm consequences instead.”

Avoid giving a long list of criticism. Begin with essential safety and one or two high-impact changes. If an adult repeatedly ignores a serious boundary, the supervision arrangement must change—even when that conversation is uncomfortable.
Caregivers and Domestic Workers
Anyone caring for the child needs clear information about:
Do not simply say:
“Take good care of her.”

Write down the important instructions. Treat caregivers respectfully. Children learn from how adults treat people who work in the home. At the same time, monitor care appropriately and make sure the child has ways to communicate concerns.
Separated and Co-Parenting Families
Children moving between homes may experience different routines. Perfectly matching households may be impossible. Try to agree on:

items: and how major decisions are made.

blocks.text: Do not use the child to carry hostile messages:
“Tell your father he still owes me money.”
Communicate adult matters directly whenever safe and possible. The child needs permission to enjoy both homes without reporting every detail to prove loyalty.
When a New Partner Joins the Family
A stepparent or parent’s partner should not be expected to assume full parental authority immediately. Relationships need time. Early priorities include:

The established parent should initially carry more responsibility for major discipline while the new adult builds connection. Do not force the child to use a parental title or display immediate affection.
Adult Conflict in Front of Children
Children can witness respectful disagreement:
“I see it differently.”
“Let’s pause and discuss it after dinner.”
“I was wrong about that. I’m sorry.”
This teaches repair. What harms children is repeated exposure to:
If conflict is becoming unsafe, the priority is not presenting a united parenting front. The priority is safety and professional support.
A Ten-Minute Parenting Meeting
Once a week, discuss only:

items: What might the child be communicating?
Which boundary matters most this week?
What words will both adults use?
When will we review the plan?

blocks.text: Keep the meeting focused. Do not use it to reopen every unresolved relationship grievance.
A Shared Response
For a repeated difficulty, agree on one short script. For hitting:
“I won’t let you hit. We will help your body become safe.”
For screens:
“Screen time is finished. You may be upset; the limit remains.”
For bedtime:
“The routine is complete. It is time to rest.”
Children learn faster when the message remains recognisable, even if each adult’s tone and style differ.
Common Mistakes
1. Trying to Agree on Everything
Focus first on safety and high-impact routines.
2. Correcting Another Adult Publicly
Discuss differences privately unless immediate safety requires intervention.
3. Using the Child as a Messenger
Adult communication belongs between adults.
4. Allowing “Don’t Tell” Arrangements
Children should not carry secrets between caregivers.
5. Assuming Grandparents Must Follow Every Minor Preference
Leave room for different, safe relationships.
6. Ignoring Serious Boundary Violations to Preserve Peace
Family harmony must not come before a child’s safety.
7. Competing to Be the Favourite
Parenting is not a popularity contest.
When Disagreement Becomes Unsafe
Seek help when:

items: one adult uses physical punishment or humiliation;
a caregiver ignores medical or allergy instructions;
the child is asked to keep secrets;
an adult drives unsafely with the child;
conflict includes threats, coercion or violence;
one parent uses the child to monitor or punish the other;
a caregiver repeatedly violates body boundaries;
or the child appears afraid of being alone with someone.

blocks.text: Do not describe abuse as merely a “difference in parenting style.” When there is coercive control or family violence, ordinary joint parenting conversations may be unsafe. Seek specialised support.
Your Family Agreement
Our Five Essential Rules
Areas Where Caregivers May Differ
The Behaviour We Most Need a Shared Response For
The Words We Will Use
When We Will Review the Plan
Remember This
Children do not need perfectly matched adults. They need adults who can:

items: and place the child’s needs above the need to win.

blocks.text: Try This Today
Choose one recurring disagreement between caregivers. Ask:
“What outcome are both of us trying to protect?”
You may discover that both adults want the same thing—such as sleep, respect or safety—but are using different methods. Agree on one boundary and one sentence to use this week. Start there.
From Mom’s Village

A child can be loved by adults who are different. One may be playful. One may be organised. One may tell stories. One may create steady routines. The difference is not the danger. The danger begins when adults make the child carry their rivalry, hide important information or surrender safety to avoid conflict. Let the child receive love from many directions. Keep the essential boundaries clear. And when adults disagree, show the child that relationships can hold difference without turning someone into the enemy.
That lesson may become one of the most valuable forms of guidance they receive.
Editorial Note for the Indian Edition

The final edition should reflect joint families, grandparents as primary caregivers, separated households, stepparents, domestic caregivers and parents working away from home. A family therapist and Indian child-protection professional should review the sections concerning family violence, custody conflict and caregiver safety before publication.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting
pages.epigraph: “A regulated parent is not one who never becomes overwhelmed. It is one who notices, pauses, repairs and reaches for help before exhaustion becomes harm.”

blocks.text: The Spilled Milk
A child accidentally knocked over a glass of milk. His mother shouted:
“Can’t you do anything carefully?” The child froze. Almost immediately, she regretted it. The spill was small. Her reaction belonged to something larger:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting
Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: She was not angry only about milk. Her nervous system had run out of room. Parents often believe they must manage children calmly while ignoring their own hunger, exhaustion, grief and overload. But parenting happens through a human body. When that body remains under pressure for too long, patience, judgement and self-control become harder to access. Self-care is not a reward for finishing every responsibility. It is part of creating a safer family.
Parenting Is Emotionally Demanding
Young children:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

items: and have strong feelings before they have strong self-control.

blocks.text: A parent may simultaneously carry:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Feeling overwhelmed does not mean you do not love your child. It means the demands may be exceeding the support available.
Notice Your Early Warning Signs
Anger rarely begins at its loudest point. Early signs may include:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

items: wanting the child to become afraid;
or thoughts such as, “I cannot take one more thing.”

blocks.text: These signs are a signal:
“I need to reduce danger and pause.”
Do not wait until you lose control.
A Parent Pause Plan

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: 1. Make the Child Safe
Place a young child in a safe space. Move away from traffic, water, stairs, kitchens or dangerous objects.
2. Use Fewer Words
Say:
“I am very upset. I am taking a minute so I can speak safely.”
3. Regulate the Body
Try:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

items: or looking at one fixed object.

blocks.text: 4. Contact Someone
Call or message a trusted adult if you need immediate support.
5. Return
The pause should protect the relationship—not become silent punishment.
“I’m ready to talk now.”
Never leave a young child unsupervised in an unsafe situation.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: When You Shout
Repair matters. A repair does not erase the boundary or make the child responsible for forgiving immediately. Say:
“I shouted and frightened you. That was not okay.”
“The milk was an accident. I was overwhelmed, but my reaction was my responsibility.”
“Next time I will put the glass down and take a pause.”
Avoid:
“I’m sorry, but you made me angry.”
That turns the apology back into blame. The child may still need to clean up the spill. Accountability and responsibility can coexist.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Repair Does Not Weaken Authority
Some parents fear that apologising makes children disrespectful. A sincere apology teaches:
You do not need to provide adult details:
“I shouted because your father and I are having problems.”
Keep the explanation appropriate:
“I was stressed and did not handle it well. That was not your fault.”

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Identify What Restores You
Self-care advice often sounds unrealistic. A parent may not have time, money or childcare for a holiday, spa or quiet afternoon. Look for different sizes of support.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting
Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

rows: Ask for one task to be taken over

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Small actions help, but they should not be used to hide a serious lack of support. A breathing exercise cannot repair an unsafe marriage, untreated depression or impossible workload. Sometimes the problem requires structural change.
Share the Invisible Work
Parenting includes tasks that are easy to overlook:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Write the tasks down. Then discuss:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: “Helping” suggests one parent owns the work. Shared parenting means adults carry genuine responsibility.
Let “Good Enough” Be Enough

Not every meal must be elaborate. Not every holiday needs a perfect activity. The home will sometimes be untidy. A child may wear mismatched clothes. A birthday may be simple. Perfection consumes energy that families need for connection and safety. Ask:
“Does this truly matter?”
“What is the simplest acceptable version?”
Children need dependable care—not a permanently impressive home.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Protect Small Parts of Your Identity
Parenthood changes life, but it does not have to erase the adult. Keep one small connection with something that belongs to you:
This is not selfish. Children benefit from seeing that adults have interests, limits and inner lives.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Accept Help Without Surrendering Safety
Support may come from:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Be clear about essential safety expectations. Then allow the helper to do some things differently. If help must be performed exactly as you would perform it, support may never feel possible. However, do not accept help from someone who ignores serious medical, physical or body-safety boundaries.
When Guilt Appears
Parents feel guilty about:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Ask:
“Is this guilt pointing to something I need to repair—or is it demanding an impossible standard?”
Useful guilt may lead to action:
“I have been distracted every evening. I will protect ten minutes at bedtime.”
Unhelpful guilt says:
“A good parent would never be tired, angry or unavailable.”
No human can meet that standard.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Know When You Need More Than Rest
Persistent distress may need professional support. Possible signs include:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

items: inability to sleep even when given the opportunity;
using alcohol or substances to cope;
or believing the family would be better without you.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: These experiences are not moral failures. Speak with an appropriate doctor or mental-health professional. Support may include medical care, counselling, practical help or crisis intervention.
Act Immediately If Safety Is at Risk
Get urgent help if you believe you may:

items: hit, shake or seriously harm the child;
or lose control in a dangerous way.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Place the child somewhere safe, move away from weapons or dangerous objects and contact a trusted adult or emergency service. Never shake a child. Do not remain alone with the crisis simply because you feel ashamed. Asking for immediate help is an act of protection.
Common Parental-Care Mistakes
1. Waiting Until Complete Burnout
Respond to early warning signs.
2. Calling Basic Needs Selfish

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Sleep, food, healthcare and support are foundations.
3. Using the Child as Emotional Support
Share adult fears with appropriate adults, not through the child.
4. Expecting One Parent to Carry Everything
Make invisible work visible.
5. Treating Repair as Failure
Repair is part of healthy relationships.
6. Using Self-Care to Avoid Structural Problems

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Some situations need genuine changes, not another calming exercise.
Your Parent Safety Plan
A safe place for my child during a short pause: ____________________________________
People I can contact:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: One professional resource I can contact if needed: ____________________________________
Remember This
Your child does not need a parent who never struggles. They need a parent who:

items: and seeks support when the burden becomes too heavy.

blocks.text: Try This Today
Complete this sentence:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: “The part of parenting that is exhausting me most right now is __________.”
Then choose one real response:

items: ask someone to take one task;
or tell a trusted person honestly that you are struggling.

blocks.text: Do not begin with what you should handle. Begin with what you are actually carrying.
From Mom’s Village

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Parents are often told to pour endlessly from love. But even love becomes harder to express through an exhausted body. You are not separate from the family’s wellbeing. You are part of it. Feed the child—and yourself. Protect the child’s sleep—and look honestly at your own. Offer compassion after their difficult moments—and allow yourself support after yours. The goal is not endless calm. It is a family in which nobody’s distress becomes invisible, including the parent’s.
Indian Support Resources

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

blocks.text: Reaching out is not a last resort. These national helplines are free, confidential and available in multiple Indian languages:

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

items: Tele-MANAS — 14416 or 1800-891-4416: the national tele-mental-health helpline, 24 hours, for distress, anxiety, depression or any mental-health concern, including for parents themselves.
KIRAN — 1800-599-0019: a 24-hour mental-health rehabilitation helpline in 13 languages, for early support, distress management and referral.
Women Helpline — 181: a 24-hour national helpline for domestic violence, harassment or any threat to a woman’s safety, with referral to police, hospitals and shelters.
Childline — 1098 (or 112, as the two are being merged in many states): for any concern about a child’s safety, including a parent who feels they may lose control.

pages.chapterTitle: Caring for the Parent Behind the Parenting

items: 112: India’s general emergency number, for police, medical or fire emergencies.

blocks.text: A parent who is overwhelmed, frightened of their own reactions, or unsafe at home deserves the same compassion this book asks her to offer her child. Calling one of these numbers is a sign of care, not failure.
Editorial note for review: Numbers current as of 2026; please re-verify before publication, and consider adding state-specific and language-specific resources where available.

pages.epigraph: “Confident parenting is not knowing exactly what to do in every moment. It is knowing how to pause, understand what matters and choose the next helpful step.”

blocks.text: The Question Beneath Every Question
Throughout the early years, parents ask:

items: How do I stop the tantrums?
Should my child be reading yet?
Why will my child not listen?
Have I already made too many mistakes?

blocks.text: Beneath these questions is often a quieter one:
“Am I giving my child what they need?”

No book can answer that question for every family situation. Children differ. Families differ. Life changes. What this companion can offer is a way to think. When the next difficult moment arrives, you do not need to remember every page. Return to a few essential questions:

items: What might the behaviour be communicating?
What boundary or skill is needed?
Can I respond calmly enough to teach?

blocks.text: That is the heart of guidance.
The Mom’s Village Guidance Path
Step 1: Begin With Safety
Before discussing feelings or consequences:

items: and seek urgent help when necessary.

blocks.text: Connection never requires delaying immediate safety.
Step 2: Look Beneath the Behaviour
Ask whether the child may be:
Understanding does not excuse harmful behaviour. It helps you teach the right lesson.
Step 3: Set the Boundary
Use clear words:
“I won’t let you hit.”
“The screen is finished.”
“You may be angry. You may not break the toy.”
“Your body belongs to you, and her body belongs to her.”
Boundaries work best when they are calm, realistic and repeatable.
Step 4: Teach the Missing Skill
A child may need to learn how to:
Punishment alone does not provide these skills.
Step 5: Repair
After a difficult moment, return.
“That was hard.”
“I shouted. I’m sorry.”
“You hit your brother. We need to check that he is okay.”
“Let’s work out what could happen next time.”
Repair does not erase responsibility. It makes responsibility useful.
Your Family’s Core Values
Rules are easier to maintain when they grow from values. Choose five values you want family life to reflect. Possible values include:
Our Five Family Values
Now translate each value into behaviour.

rows: What it looks like in our home
We stop when someone says their body is not safe
We help repair what we damage
Children can tell the truth without humiliation

blocks.text: A value is not something placed on a wall. It is something children experience in how adults use power.
Your Essential Family Boundaries
Do not create rules for every possible situation. Choose the boundaries that protect daily life.
Safety
We do not hit, bite, threaten or deliberately hurt.
Bodies
Everyone’s body deserves privacy and respect.
Belongings
We ask before taking personal items.
Screens
Devices have agreed times and places.
Sleep
The family protects a regular opportunity for rest.
Respect
Feelings are allowed; humiliation and cruelty are not.
Truth
A child can report a mistake or unsafe situation without losing our love.
Write your family’s most important boundaries:
Know Your Child
My Child’s Strengths
My Child Finds These Situations Difficult
Early Signs of Overwhelm
What Usually Helps
What Usually Makes Things Worse
Ways My Child Communicates
Skills We Are Currently Teaching
____________________________________ This page should change as the child grows. Do not turn today’s difficulty into a permanent identity.
Know Yourself
My Parenting Triggers
Signs That I Am Becoming Overwhelmed
A Safe Pause I Can Take
